gmw_00000324.name: Akn 564
gmw_00000324.overview: Akn 564 is a nearby narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2304.12383, arXiv:1804.03503] characterized by a strong soft X-ray excess [arXiv:1804.03503, arXiv:1908.07272] and rapid X-ray variability [arXiv:1804.03503, arXiv:1504.01226]. The active nucleus features a resolved multi-component radio structure [arXiv:2007.11327] and exhibits high dust extinction [arXiv:0708.1083].

sources.summary: The paper studies Akn 564 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, finding soft-to-hard X-ray lags, an energy-dependent power spectrum, warm absorbers, and a two-component corona explanation for its soft excess.

quotes.quote: We investigate the origin of the soft X-ray excess emission from narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies Akn 564 and Mrk 1044 using observations. We find clear evidence for time delays between the soft and hard X-ray emission from Akn 564 based on a 100 long observation. The variations in the 4-10 band lag behind that in the 0.2-0.5 band by 1768 122 . The full band power density spectrum (PDS) of Akn 564 has a break at 1.2 10^ 3 with power-law indices of 1 and 3 below and above the break. The hard ( 3-10 ) band PDS is stronger and flatter than that in the soft ( 0.2-0.5 ) band.

The high resolution spectrum of Akn 564 obtained with the reflection grating spectrometer (RGS) shows evidence for a highly ionized and another weakly ionized warm absorber medium. The smeared wind and blurred ionized reflection models do not describe the EPIC-pn data adequately. The spectrum is consistent with a complex model consisting of optically thick Comptonization in a cool plasma for the soft excess and a steep power-law, modified by two warm absorber media as inferred from the RGS data and the foreground Galactic absorption.

sources.summary: The paper uses Akn 564 soft- and hard-band X-ray timing to argue that its soft excess cannot be simple hard-X-ray reprocessed emission.

quotes.quote: Here we present cross-correlation between soft and hard band X-ray emission from the NLS1 galaxies Akn 564 and Mrk 110 and discuss implications of the measured time lags on the origin of the soft excess emission. We also discuss how can be used to constrain the models for the soft excess emission. Figure f1 ( left ) shows the cross-correlation function (CCF) between soft and hard band light curves, obtained from a 100 observation, as a function of time delay. Positive delay means hard band lags the soft band. As the separation between the two bands increases, the correlation at zero lag disappears and a new correlation at positive delay ( 2000 ) appears.

There is also evidence for energy dependent time delay from Akn 564 . The lag between the 0.7-2 and 5-10 bands is about a factor of two larger than the lag between the 0.7-2 and 2-5 bands. The lag spectrum of Akn 564 is very similar to that of Galactic black hole binaries (BHBs) in their very high states except that the time lag is about 4-5 orders of magnitude lower in BHBs .

sources.summary: The paper uses Akn 564 XMM-Newton light curves to validate analytic cross-correlation, phase, and time-lag estimates, comparing with previous lag measurements and stationarity tests.

quotes.quote: To test and validate the effectiveness of the scheme, we analyse the lightcurve of a well studied Active Galactic Nucleus, Akn 564. The temporal and spectral properties of the source was studied using an XMM-Newton observation of the source by . They computed the cross-correlation function for different energy bands and estimated a possible time-lag between the hard and soft bands to be 1768 secs using the peak of the function as a measure. Using Monte Carlo simulations they estimated an error on the time lag to be 100 secs due to measurement error.

We extracted light curves of the source using the XMM-Newton observation, in different energy bands. Details of the extraction process are given in . The usable continuous time duration for the observation is for 10^5 secs. Our motivation here is not to analyse in detail the temporal properties of the source and their physical interpretation, but instead to show as an example and validate the method described in this work.

sources.summary: The paper models Ark 564 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 whose coronal-line gas is launched from the dusty torus, accelerates, and later slows under host-galaxy and bulge influence.

quotes.quote: We use the photoionisation code cloudy to determine both the location and the kinematics of the optical forbidden, high ionisation line (hereafter, ) emitting gas in the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy Ark 564. The results of our models are compared with the observed properties of these emission lines to produce a physical model that is used to explain both the kinematics and the source of this gas. The main features of this model are that the \ emitting gas is launched from the putative dusty torus and is quickly accelerated to its terminal velocity of a few hundred km s ^ 1 . Iron-carrying grains are destroyed during this initial acceleration. This velocity is maintained by a balance between radiative forces and gravity in this super-Eddington source.

Eventually the outflow is slowed at large radii by the gravitational forces of and interactions with the host galaxy. In this model, \ emission traces the transition between the AGN and bulge zones of influence.

sources.summary: Akn 564 is described as a very high accretion-rate AGN whose PSD and lag spectrum support a very-high-state interpretation rather than a hard-state one.

quotes.quote: Arakelian 564 is one of the highest accretion rate AGN known ( m _ E 1 ) and is the only AGN for which there is presently good evidence that its PSD contains more than one bend. From observations with , evidence for a low frequency bend was presented and evidence for a second bend, at high frequencies, was also found from analysis of ASCA ASCA observations .

Although the PSD of Akn 564 can be fitted with a doubly-bending powerlaw as well as with two Lorentzian Lorentzian components, strong support for the Lorentzian interpretation is given by the spectrum of the lags between the hard and soft X-ray bands.

In most AGN the PSD and lag spectra are not defined well enough to distinguish different Lorentzian components but for Akn 564 we can measure the lag spectrum (Fig. fig:564 , top panel). We see that the lag changes rapidly from one fairly constant level to another at the frequency where the PSD changes from one Lorentzian to another. Given the extremely high accretion rate of Akn 564, we interpret these observations as strongly supporting the VHS, rather than hard state, interpretation.

sources.summary: The paper presents Ark 564 as a nearby bright NLS1/AGN with strong Fe II emission, X-ray/UV features, optical variability, correlated Fe II and H beta flux, and stable Lorentzian permitted-line profiles.

quotes.quote: Arakelian 564 (Ark 564, IRAS 22403+2927, MGC +05-53-012) is a bright V=14.6 mag. dev91 , nearby narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy ( z=0.02467 ), with an X-ray luminosity L_ 2-10 keV =2.4 10^ 43 , erg \ s^ 1 tu01 . This AGN is one of the brightest NLS1s in the X-ray band bol96,co01,sm08 , and it shows a soft excess below 1.5 keV and a peculiar emission-line-like feature at 0.712 keV in the source rest frame ch04 . The variations of the X-ray amplitude in the short-timescale light curve is very similar to those in the long-timescale light curve po01 , that is in contrast to the stronger amplitude variability on longer timescales, which is a characteristic of broad-line Seyfert 1 (BLS1) galaxies. In the UV part of the spectrum, this galaxy shows intrinsic UV absorption lines cr99 .

We found that during the monitoring period the spectral variation (F _ max /F _ min ) of Ark 564 was between 1.5 for H to 1.8 for the Fe 2 lines. The correlation between the Fe 2 and H flux variations is of higher significance than that of H and H (whose correlation is almost absent). The permitted-line profiles are Lorentzian-like, and did not change shape during the monitoring period.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes eight XMM-Newton observations of Ark 564, finding variable double-Comptonization X-ray components and UV variability correlated with the high-energy index, favoring geometry-dependent X-ray reprocessing.

quotes.quote: We analyse eight XMM-Newton observations of the bright Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 galaxy Arakelian 564 (Ark 564). These observations, separated by 6 days, allow us to look for correlations between the simultaneous UV emission (from the Optical Monitor) with not only the X-ray flux but also with the different X-ray spectral parameters. The X-ray spectra from all the observations are found to be adequately fitted by a double Comptonization model where the soft excess and the hard X-ray power law are represented by thermal Comptonization in a low temperature plasma and hot corona, respectively. Apart from the fluxes of each component, the hard X-ray power law index is found to be variable.

The UV emission is found to be variable and well correlated with the high energy index while the correlations with the fluxes of each component are found to be weaker. Using viscous time-scale arguments we rule out the possibility that the UV variation is due to fluctuating accretion rate in the outer disc. If the UV variation is driven by X-ray reprocessing, then our results indicate that the strength of the X-ray reprocessing depends more on the geometry of the X-ray producing inner region rather than on the X-ray luminosity alone.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Suzaku X-ray data for Ark 564, a Narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, using reflection spectroscopy to constrain its inner disk, spin, deformation parameters, and Kerr-black-hole consistency.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we present our results obtained by analyzing Suzaku data of the Narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1) Ark 564. This source is suitable for such studies because it has a simple reflection spectrum without any complicated emission or absorption features. Moreover, previous studies show that the inner edge of the disk is very close to the black hole and is thus influenced by the strong gravity region. Ark 564 was observed by Suzaku on 26 June 2007 for about 86 ks.

Here, we present the constrains on a_* , _ 13 , and _ 22 using X-ray reflection spectroscopy of the supermassive black hole in Ark 564. After analyzing different models, we can conclude that model 4 is the best model. The double reflection model does not improve the statistics significantly.

sources.summary: The paper treats ARK 564 as a well-studied NLS1 and finds four QPO candidates whose frequencies shift with time while remaining mostly in 2-3 keV bands.

quotes.quote: ARK 564 ARK 564 is another well-studied NLS1, both on short (e.g. Kara2017ARK564 ) and long (e.g. 2006McHardyAGNScaling ) timescales, providing deeper insights into the complex nature of the broad-band noise, which we discuss in Section 5. Initial plc search We detect QPO candidates in 4 out of 11 XMM-Newton observations. The nature of these candidates appears less stable than in the other AGN in our sample, and Figure fig:ARK_multiplot demonstrates the apparent movement of the QPO candidate towards lower frequencies with time. The first observation in which we find a 3 outlier (OBSID:0006810101) is similar to the higher frequency QPO candidate we have located in IRAS 13224-3809, at a frequency of 1.88 10^ 3 Hz.

In OBSID:0670130501, we detect a QPO candidate at a frequency of 8.59 10^ 4 Hz, prior to the observations of OBSID:0670130701 and OBSID:0670130801 in which we find QPO candidates at 2.73 10^ 4 Hz and 2.54 10^ 4 Hz respectively. The energy ranges in which these four QPO candidates are detected are largely consistent, being dominated by counts at 2-3 keV. The full range of candidate significance as a function of energy is shown in Figure fig:Heatmap_panels .

sources.summary: The paper presents a contemporaneous multiwavelength SED for Ark 564, describes it as a nearby NLS1 galaxy, and models its extreme-UV/soft-X-ray dominated emission and line-emitting gas.

quotes.quote: We present the intrinsic spectral energy distribution (SED) of the Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1) MGC +05-53-012 Ark 564 , constructed with contemporaneous data obtained during a multi-wavelength, multi-satellite observing campaign in 2000 and 2001. We compare this SED with that of the NLS1 Ton S180 and with those obtained for Broad-Line Seyfert 1s to infer how the relative accretion rates vary among the Seyfert 1 population. Although the peak of the SED is not well constrained, in our parameterization most of the energy of this object is emitted in the 10--100 eV regime, constituting roughly half of the emitted energy in the optical/X-ray ranges. This is consistent with a primary spectral component peaking in the extreme UV/soft X-ray band, and with disk--corona models, hence high accretion rates. Indeed, we estimate that m 1 .

Arakelian 564 (Ark 564, IRAS 22403+2927, MGC +05-53-012) is a bright, nearby NLS1 galaxy, with z = 0.02467 and V = 14.6 mag rc3.9catalogue , and a mean 2--10 keV luminosity L_ 2--10 keV 2.4 10^ 43 with flux variations of a factor of a few in a few thousand seconds hereafter Paper I Akn564I . It was the object of an intense multiwavelength monitoring campaign that included simultaneous observations from ASCA (2000 June 1 to July 6, Paper I; Poundsea01,Edelsonea02 ), XMM-Newton (2000 June 17, Vignaliea03 ), Chandra (2000 June 17, Matsumotoea02 ), HST (2000 May 9 to July 8, Collierea01 , Paper II; Crenshawea02 , Paper IV), FUSE (2001 June 29--30, Romanoea02b , Paper V), and from many ground-based observatories as part of an AGN Watch http://www.astronomy.ohio-state.edu/ agnwatch All publicly available data and complete references to published AGN Watch papers can be found at http://www.astronomy.ohio-state.edu/ agnwatch. project 1998 November to 2001 January, Paper III Shemmerea01 . Ark 564 has shown a strong associated UV absorber ( Crenshawea99 , Paper II; Paper IV; Paper V). There are indications that it also possesses a warm X-ray absorber, as seen by the narrow absorption lines of O 7 and O 8 detected in a Chandra spectrum Matsumotoea02 , and that the UV and X-ray absorbers in Arakelian 564 are physically related, possibly identical, and may be spatially extended along the line of sight (Paper V).

sources.summary: The paper fits Akn 564 with a slim-disk/corona model, deriving a low black-hole mass, super-critical accretion rate, weak hot corona, and important transition radius.

quotes.quote: Arakelian 564: Pounds et al. (2001) estimated that the black hole mass in Akn 564 is 10^7 M_ and the accretion rate m 0.2-1 from the break frequency of the power spectrum of the light curves. The multiwavelength spectra of Akn 564 have been extensively studied by Romano et al. (2004). They found that M_ BH =4 10^6M_ and 1 .

The observed spectral energy distribution of Akn 564 is taken from Comastri et al. (2001). The comparison of the present model with the observations provides M_ BH =10^6M_ , m =1.1 , =0.32 and f=0.021 . The hot corona is relatively weaker than that in RE J1034+396.
The transition radii, where _ eff =1 , are 11.5 and 15.5R_ g for RE J1034+396 and Akn 564, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 564 as an X-ray-bright NLS1, measuring coherent X-ray bands and hard lags over broad frequencies and arguing its spectral-timing behavior resembles very high or intermediate-state black-hole binaries.

quotes.quote: Received /Accepted firstpage - lastpage 2006 We use a 100 ks long \ observation of the Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 galaxy \ and combine it with the month-long monitoring of the same source produced by , to calculate the phase lags and coherence between different energy bands, over frequencies 10^ 6 to 10^ 3 Hz. This is the widest frequency range for which these spectral-timing properties have been calculated accurately for any AGN. The 0.7--2 and 2--10 keV \ light curves, and the \ light curves in corresponding energy bands, are highly coherent ( 0.9 ) over most of the frequency range studied. We observe time lags between the energy bands, increasing both with time-scale and with energy separation of the bands. The time lag spectrum shows a broad peak in the 10^ 5 5 10^ 4 Hz frequency range, where the time lags follow a power law slope 0.7 .

The strong drops in the lag spectrum of , together with the larger amplitude of the lags, show that the data resemble the lag spectrum of Cyg X-1 in the intermediate state (or equivalently, the very high state in BHXRB transients, where similar properties are seen at higher luminosities). Therefore, the spectral-timing data supports the earlier suggestion by Papadakis_ark that Ark 564 is similar to a BHXRB in the very high state .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Ark 564 as an X-ray-bright narrow-line Seyfert 1, finding stable broad-band spectral shape, ionized gas, weak iron emission, and delayed hard X-ray reverberation.

quotes.quote: Arakelian 564 (Ark 564, z=0.0247 ) is an X-ray-bright narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy. By using advanced X-ray timing techniques, an excess of ``delayed'' emission in the hard X-ray band ( 4-7.5 keV) following about 1000 seconds after ``flaring'' light in the soft X-ray band ( 0.4-1 keV) was recently detected. We report on the X-ray spectral analysis of eight XMM-Newton and one Suzaku observation of Ark 564.

Despite the strong variability in flux during our observational campaign, the broad-band spectral shape of Ark 564 does not vary dramatically and can be reproduced either by a superposition of a power law and a blackbody emission or by a Comptonized power-law emission model. High-resolution spectroscopy revealed ionised gas along the line of sight at the systemic redshift of the source, with a low column density ( N_H 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 ) and a range of ionisation states ( 0.8 < ( / erg cm s ^ 1 ) < 2.4 ). Broad-band spectroscopy revealed a very steep intrinsic continuum (photon index 2.6 ) and a rather weak emission feature in the iron K band (EW 150 eV); modelling this feature with a reflection component requires highly ionised gas, ( / erg cm s ^ 1 )> 3.5 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 564 as one of two sources needing a combined continuum plus reverberation model, allowing a low-height high-spin interpretation despite large single-model heights.

quotes.quote: The only exceptions were Ark 564 and NGC 7314, whose observed time-lag spectra we also fitted to a combined model A+B as well for reasons we discuss in Sect. subsec62 below.

The best-fit h values for Ark 564 and NGC 7314 are 83r_ g and 100r_ g (which is the highest allowed fitting value for h ), respectively. The X-ray source height is consistent with the value of 100r_ g for Ark 564. The lower limit on the best-fit h value is 28r_ g and 82r_ g for Ark 564 and NGC 7314, respectively.

The quality of the combined model A+B fit is similar to that of model A: ^2_ min / dof =14.6/18 , and ^2_ min / dof =26.6/20 in the case of Ark 564 and NGC 7314, respectively. This result shows that the observed iron line vs continuum time-lags of Ark 564 and NGC 7314 can be fitted well by a combination of a continuum plus reverberation component, the latter of which corresponds to a low h and high a value.

sources.summary: The paper models Ark 564 time lags, describing it as a bright NLS1 with soft excess, rapid variability, iron reverberation, and low-height X-ray source fits.

quotes.quote: ARK 564 ( z=0.02468 ) shows the typical characteristics of a Narrow Line Seyfert I (NLS1): steep X-ray spectrum, a strong soft excess, and rapid variability. It also has the benefit of being extremely bright in the soft X-ray band ( F_ 0.3-10  keV =1.4 10^ 10  erg/s/cm ^2 ; kara13b ). Because of these qualities, it has been observed by all the major X-ray observatories, including XMM-Newton , Suzaku and NuSTAR kara17 .

Ark 564 was the first source where negative time-lags were, tentatively, detected mchardy07 . kara13b measured its lag-energy spectrum at different temporal frequencies. They found that low-frequency lags ( 10^ 5 10^ 4  Hz) show a featureless log-linear increase with energy, while the high-frequency lags ( 10^ 4 10^ 3  Hz) showed clear evidence for iron K reverberation.

sources.summary: The paper fits Ark 564 with the same wind-scattering model, arguing that its face-on geometry and smaller wind solid angle explain weaker Fe-K features.

quotes.quote: This broad iron line feature in the lag spectrum is most significant in super-Eddington sources such as Ark 564 ( L/L_ Edd 1 ) and 1H 0707--495 ( L/L_ Edd 10 ). The observed lag timescales correspond to very short distances of several R_g/ , so that they have been used to argue for extremely small `lamp-post' coronae close to the event horizon of rapidly spinning black holes. Here we show for the first time that these Fe-K short lags are more likely to arise from scattering in a highly-ionised wind, launched at 50 R_g , rotating and outflowing with a typical velocity of 0.2c . We show that this model can simultaneously fit the time-averaged energy spectra and the short-timescale lag-energy spectra of both 1H 0707--495 and Ark 564.

The Fe-K line in 1H 0707--495 has a strong P-Cygni-like profile, which requires that the wind solid angle is large and that our line of sight intercepts the wind. By contrast the lack of an absorption line in the energy spectrum of Ark 564 requires rather face-on geometry, while the weaker broad Fe-K emission in the energy and lag-energy spectra argue for a smaller solid angle of the wind. This is consistent with theoretical predictions that the winds get stronger when the sources are more super-Eddington, supporting the idea of AGN feedback via radiation pressure driven winds.

We speculate that this is associated with the larger super-Eddington luminosity ( L/L_ Edd >10 ; don16 ) in 1H 0707-495, than in Ark 564 which is only mildly super-Eddington ( L/L_ Edd 1 ; mul09 ). The difference of the wind solid angle can be naturally explained by a larger radiation pressure at higher super-Eddington luminosities.

sources.summary: Ark 564 is used as the long-monitored NLSy1 comparison object; it shows intrinsic Baldwin effect, weak line-continuum correlation, strongest Beff, and a short time delay.

quotes.quote: We found that all six galaxies exhibit intrinsic Beff, including the NLSy1 Ark 564, and that this effect is probably not caused by the geometry of the broad line region (BLR). Additionally, we showed that there is no connection between global and intrinsic Beff .

Here we present our preliminary results of our study of the intrinsic Beff of broad H line in five NLSy1 galaxies from SDSS-RM, compared to the findings in Ark 564 from . We selected objects from SDSS-RM explained in with z<0.8 and we cross-matched with NLSy 1 catalog of . Additionally, we used the data of long-term monitored NLSy1 galaxy Ark 564 published in .

Interestingly, in Ark 564 there is a weak correlation between line and continuum flux, but there is the strongest intrinsic Beff present. We note that Ark 564 has been monitored for 11 years, while SDSS galaxies for only about 2.5 years, this might be the reason behind weaker correlation found in SDSS galaxies.It is also important to emphasize that in the case of SDSS objects, line fluxes are not corrected for the time delay with respect to the continuum. However, we do not expect this will have significant impact on the Baldwin effect results, since in NLSy1 the time delay is small and the variability is weak, e.g. we tested this effect in case of Ark 564 which has the time delay of 5 days and weak variability of 10 , and there is no difference in the result.

sources.summary: The paper treats Akn 564 as an EESBH with evident AGN radio activity, resolved multi-component VLA emission, a 4 kpc radio structure, and quasi-continuous radio ejection.

quotes.quote: In our EESBH sample, Akn 564 is the only source showing evident AGN activity with a collimated linear structure extending 1 kpc towards the north, and three resolved components in the VLA 5 GHz and 8.4 GHz images (see Figure fig:akn564 ).

Akn 564 : We have measured a peak flux density of 5.69 0.03 mJy beam ^ 1 at C band, which is consistent with the peak flux density of 5.68 0.02 mJy beam ^ 1 obtained by 2018A&A...614A..87B . The source is resolved into three components at C and X bands, we only take account of the integrated flux density of the central component, which is 5.84 0.03 mJy at C band and 3.18 0.07 mJy at X band. 2001ApJS..132..199S also fit the X-band image with three components, and the inferred integrated flux density of the central component is 3.1 mJy, and being consistent with our results. The size of the radio structure is 2 arcsec (corresponding to physical linear size of 4 kpc).

There is only one source (Akn 564) in our sample that shows such quasi-continuous radio ejection, and VLBA images show that B3 1702+457 in our EESBHs sample has a bipolar emission 2011ApJ...738..126D .

sources.summary: The paper monitors Akn 564 across optical, UV, and X-ray bands, finding strong X-ray variability, weaker optical/UV changes, short interband lags, and only limited evidence for reprocessing.

quotes.quote: We present the results of a two-year long optical monitoring program of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Akn 564. The majority of this monitoring project was also covered by X-ray observations ( ) and for a period of 50 days, we observed the galaxy in UV ( HST ) and X-rays ( \ ASCA ) simultaneously with the ground-based observations. Rapid and large-amplitude variations seen in the X-ray band, on a daily and hourly time-scale, were not detected at optical and UV wavelengths, which in turn exhibited much lower variability either on short (one day) or long (several months) time-scales.

The only significant optical variations can be described as two 2--4 day events with 10 flux variations. We detect no significant optical line variations and thus cannot infer a reverberation size for the broad-line region. The UV continuum follows the X-rays with a lag of 0.4 days, and the optical band lags the UV band by 2 days.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ark 564 as a monitored narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy with rapid X-ray events, lower-amplitude UV/optical responses, and some X-ray events lacking optical counterparts.

quotes.quote: Ark 564 is our best-studied Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 (NLS1). Fig. 2 shows the results of our simultaneous ASCA , HST , and optical monitoring of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy Ark 564. The 1 keV ASCA data are a 0.3-day running average taken from the observations reported in Turner01 and Edelson02 . The 1365 UV fluxes are from Collier01 , and the optical photometry from Shemmer01 . It can be seen that there are numerous X-ray events on sub-day timescales and some of the largest of these have lower-amplitude UV and optical events following them by 1.5 days.
Third, as with NGC 5548, there were X-ray events with no optical counterparts (notably the short flare at JD 2451706).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes archival XMM-Newton observations of Ark 564 and reports a high-frequency Fe K reverberation lag, plus variable light curves and lag-energy behavior across a 500 ks observation.

quotes.quote: We use archival XMM-Newton observations of Ark 564 and Mrk 335 to calculate the frequency dependent time-lags for these two well-studied sources. We discover high-frequency Fe K lags in both sources, indicating that the red wing of the line precedes the rest frame energy by roughly 100 s and 150 s for Ark 564 and Mrk 335, respectively. Including these two new sources, Fe K reverberation lags have been observed in seven Seyfert galaxies.

The light curves of Ark 564 in Fig. ark_lc show a bright and highly variable source over the 500 ks observation, making it a good candidate for searching for time lags. Fig. ark_lagfreq shows the lag as a function of temporal frequency, using the technique described in Section fourier .

sources.summary: The paper compares AKN 564 analyses, noting earlier combined spectra found emission lines and three warm absorber components, whereas this work uses one dataset and detects one component.

quotes.quote: AKN 564- This source was stuided by . All the four datasets from 2001 and one from 2005 (100 ks) were combined to obtain the final RGS spectrum. We have used only 2005 (100 ks) data set. They detected 5 emission lines and 3 WA components. =-0.3 0.5 , =0.9 0.7 10^ 20 , and =1.0 0.4 , =1.1 0.6 10^ 20 , and =2.2 0.4 , =2.2 2.1 10^ 20 . We however detect only one WA component.

sources.summary: Ark 564 has weaker low-energy and iron-line suppression than Class A sources, smoother pivoting, and a third component suggesting a reflection-linked soft excess.

quotes.quote: Ark 564 The first PC found for Ark 564 shows features that are very similar to those found in the primary components of the Class A objects: suppression of the power law variability at low energies, and at the energy of the iron line. However, these features are rather weaker than in the Class A sources, implying that the constant component giving rise to them is correspondingly weaker. The second component is also much smoother, flattening at low energies and steepening at high energies, with no sharp breaks. Finally, the third component shows a correlation between low and high energies, but with no noticeable iron line feature. This is qualitatively similar to the third or fourth order component found in the objects shown in Figs. mcg6_spectra , 4051_spectra and 3516and766 , and can be produced by the presence of a variable high ionisation reflection spectrum, as shown in Fig. reflectionsims .

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 564 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with weak line-continuum response, nearly constant Balmer flux, low H-line variability, and outlying BLR radius-luminosity behavior.

quotes.quote: The objects have been selected so that representatives of type 1 AGN with different BEL profiles are in the sample: Arp 102B and 3C 390.3 have BELs with double-peaked line profiles, Ark 564 is a Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1), and NGC 4151 and NGC 5548 are well-studied Seyfert 1.5 with strongly variable BEL profiles, with bumps and asymmetries present in the line profiles (see Figure 1).
The two objects, Arp 102B and Ark 564 have very low correlation coefficients (0.37 and 0.59, respectively), indicating a weak response of the line to the continuum variability.

Obviously the NLSy1 Ark 564 is clearly an outliner. This is expected due to the low flux variability of this object ( 7 for H line) and poor significance of the obtained BLR radius. Therefore, a simple fit was done through all objects except Ark 564, and we obtained =0.887 (Figure 6).

sources.summary: The paper flags Ark 564 observations for pile-up treatment and finds its iron-line PSD has low Poisson noise and a low BPL null-hypothesis probability.

quotes.quote: We checked all source light curves for pile-up using the sas task epatplot , and found that only observations 670130201, 670130501 and 670130901 of Ark 564 are affected.

The Poisson noise dominates the observed variations at frequencies higher than 10^ 3 Hz, except from four sources, namely MCG--6-30-15, NGC 4051, Ark 564, and NGC 7314. The iron-line band PSD in these sources is well defined over the lowest sampled frequency decade, i.e. \ between 10^ 4 and 10^ 3 Hz. This is not surprising, as these sources are among the X-ray brightest in our sample (hence the Poisson noise level is low) and they probably host a low mass BH as well, implying large amplitude variations at high-frequencies.
In fact, the null hypothesis probability for the best-fitting BPL model fit to the iron-line PSDs of NGC 7314 and Ark 564 is rather low ( p_ null =1.6 and 0.6 per cent, respectively).

sources.summary: The paper includes Ark 564 among bright, highly variable AGN and presents its 0.3-1 versus 2-4 keV coherence and time-lag estimates, finding coherence decreases with frequency.

quotes.quote: Figure fig1 shows the 0.3-1 vs. 2-4 keV coherence and time-lag estimates (top and bottom panels, respectively) of Ark 564 and NGC 4051. Both sources are X-ray bright and highly variable. Figure fig2 shows the same results for 1H 0707--495 and Mrk 335 (two sources that are fainter, and, in the case of Mrk 355, less variable). They were calculated using equations eq3 and eq1 , respectively. The coherence estimates decrease with increasing frequency in all cases.

sources.summary: The paper includes Ark 564 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 AGN with strong Fe II emission, narrower broad lines, weak variability, and intrinsic Baldwin-effect behavior.

quotes.quote: We investigate the intrinsic Baldwin effect (Beff) of the broad H and H emission lines for six Type 1 active galactic nuclei (AGNs) with different broad line characteristics: two Seyfert 1 ( NGC 4151 and NGC 5548 ), two AGNs with double-peaked broad line profiles ( 3C 390.3 and Arp 102B ), one narrow line Seyfert 1 ( Ark 564 ), and one high-luminosity quasar with highly red asymmetric broad line profiles ( E1821+643 ). We find that a significant intrinsic Beff was present in all Type 1 AGNs in our sample. Moreover, we do not see strong differences in intrinsic Beff slopes in different types of AGNs, which probably have different physical properties such as inclination, broad line region (BLR) geometry, or accretion rate.

Narrow line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1) AGN Ark 564 , which has a strong Fe II emission and somewhat narrower broad lines (FWHM 2000 \ kms^ 1 ) with a prominent narrow component. The variability of this AGN is weak, but there are occasional flare-like outbursts see sh12 ; High-luminosity quasar E1821+643 , a low-redshift ( z =0.297) radio-quiet quasar.

sources.summary: The paper includes Ark 564 among AGN with complex corona hints, wavy reverberation residuals, statistically good spherical-corona fits, and mass constraints consistent with prior work.

quotes.quote: Our sample consists of 1H0707--495, Ark 564, NGC 4051 and IRAS 13224--3809, all of which have shown hints of possessing a complex corona or having more than one variability component. found some wavy-residuals in the lag-frequency spectra of 1H0707--495 and Ark 564 which are not well-fitted by the KYNREFREV model, the public reverberation model that assumes a lamp-post geometry.

We find that the spherical corona model provides statistically good fits to all AGN investigated here. The constrained mass is consistent with those reported in the literature e.g., for 1H0707--495, Ark 564, NGC 4051 and IRAS 13224--3809, respectively Zhou2005,Botte2004,Emmanoulopoulos2014,Chainakun2016 .
Recent studies show that by fitting the reverberation lags with the lamp-post assumption, the BH spin in some AGN cannot be well-constrained e.g., Ark 564; Caballero-Garcia2018,Epitropakis2016 .

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes a long NuSTAR observation of the X-ray-bright NLS1 galaxy Ark 564 and finds evidence that its coronal temperature decreases as flux increases.

quotes.quote: Recently, a 200 ks NuSTAR observation of Ark 564 in 3--50 keV band, revealed the presence of one of the coolest coronae with temperature kT_e 15 keV in the time-averaged spectrum. Ark 564 is a X-ray bright NLS1 galaxy at a red-shift z = 0.02469 Huchra1999 with a 2--10 keV flux of 2 10^ 11 erg cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 Vaughan1999 and the corresponding luminosity is 2.4 10^ 43 erg s ^ 1 Turner2001 . The source shows rapid variability, a very steep spectrum in the 0.3--10 keV band, strong soft excess and iron K emission line 1999ApJ...526...52T, Leighly1999, Turner2001 .

Here, we re-analyse the 200 ks NuSTAR observation to investigate whether any correlation or trend exists between the coronal electron temperature and flux of the X-ray luminous source Ark 564. The flux resolved spectroscopy of Ark 564 undertaken in this work, indicates that the coronal temperature decreases as the flux increases.

sources.summary: The paper uses Ark 564 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 comparison source with a cooler corona in softer/brighter phases, lying below the pair-production limit with likely weak pair production.

quotes.quote: A possible counter-example is the narrow line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1) Ark 564, for which Barua_2020 found cooler corona (though statistically marginal) during the softer and brighter phases within a 200 ks observation.
For Ark 564 we convert the corona temperature kT_ e given by into E_ cut , assuming an optically thick corona and kT_e E_ cut /3 Petrucci_2001 .

Consequently the ``cooler-when-softer/brighter'' trend or the pattern only exists in sources lacking strong pair production, like the NLS1 SWIFT J2127.4+5654 and Ark 564. We note that Fabian2017 proposed that the coronae lying well below the pair limit (such as in Ark 564) could also be pair dominated if containing both thermal and non-thermal particles. However, if the possible link aforementioned does exist, it suggests the pair production in the cool coronae of Ark 564 and is indeed weak.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ark 564 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: Ark 564 is an X-ray bright narrow-line Seyfert 1 AGN located at z=0.0247 with 3 to 10 keV luminosity of 2.4 10^ 43 erg.s ^ 1 Turner2001 . This source is a rapid variable source in the X-ray domain with a strong soft excess and a steep spectrum in 0.5 to 10.0 keV band Turner1999,Turner2001 . From the analysis of XMM-Newton observations of this source, Sarma2015 showed that this high-Eddington NLS1 exhibits a prominent correlation between the spectral slope and flux. We considered the mass of the central black hole of this object as 2.5 10^6 M _ . XMM-Newton and Swift /XRT observed Ark 564 multiple times from 2000 to 2018.

sources.summary: The paper describes Akn564 as an NLS1 with band-limited flat-top noise and high Eddington ratio, possibly analogous to a very high GBH state.

quotes.quote: However, another NLS1, Akn564, convincingly shows band-limited flat top noise, where the flat top extends over only 2 decades in frequency (Fig. fig:agn_pds taken from a combination of low- and high-frequency data in Markowitz et al. 2003 and Vignali et al. 2004). Such band-limited, high rms ( 30 per cent) noise is consistent with the extreme low/hard state of GBH (Fig. fig:pds ). However, Akn 564 has high L/L_ Edd 1 (Table tab:agn ), so should instead be comparable to the high mass accretion rate GBH. Its PDS is clearly unlike that of NGC 4051 (and the high/soft state of Cyg X-1). Instead it could correspond to the PDS of the Compton tail seen in the very high state (transient) GBH.

sources.summary: The paper finds Ark 564 can be fit by broad emission or two absorption components, with two edges improving the fit and detector divergence complicating the soft-X-ray interpretation.

quotes.quote: Comparable fits are obtained with either a broad emission feature at 1 keV or two absorption components. A single absorption edge at E= 1.23 0.02 keV gives ^ 2 / =1862/1565 whereas two edges improves the fit to ^ 2 / =1819/1563 (the edge energies being E= 1.14 0.03 keV and 1.37 0.04 keV with =0.18 0.04 and 0.15 0.03 , respectively). This is consistent with the Brandt (1994) modelling of the \ spectrum of Ark 564 which employed a deep absorption edge at 1.15 keV. The broad Gaussian absorption line model detailed in Table 4 is clearly not the best representation of the data ( ^ 2 / =1854/1564 ). A broad emission line provides a slight improvement over two edges ( ^ 2 / =1815/1564 ) with E= 0.99 0.01 keV, = 0.10 0.03 keV and EW= 39^ +8 _ 4 eV.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes XMM-Newton RGS spectra of NLS1 galaxy Arakelian 564, fitting three photoionized X-ray absorbing phases with low RMS and flow velocities and distinct ionization and column densities.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed analysis of the photoionized, X-ray absorbing and emitting gas present in the NLS1 galaxy Arakelian 564. The high-resolution X-ray spectra are from five observations with XMM-Newton 's Reflection Grating Spectrometer. The absorption profile is fitted with three phases of X-ray absorbing gas with average RMS velocity 60 km s ^ 1 and flow velocity 10 km s ^ 1 . Each phase has a different ionization (log =-0.86, 0.87, 2.56 ) and column density ( N_H=0.89 10^ 20 , 2.41 10^ 20 , 6.03 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 respectively).

sources.summary: The paper estimates Ark 564 black-hole mass, describes it as near the Eddington limit, and under the Akn spelling finds a simple power-law model and no detected iron line.

quotes.quote: Data selection and reduction In Tab. tab:tab1 , we list the 10 AGN in our sample, some of their properties (distance, redshift and BH mass estimates), and details of the \ observations we used in this work. We used the petersonea:2004 estimates, from reverbaration mapping, for the BH mass (M _ BH ) in Fairall 9, NGC 4051, NGC 3227, NGC 5548, NGC 3783, and NGC 3516. In the case of Mrk 766 and MCG-6-30-15 we used the stellar velocity dispecrsion measurements of botteea:2004 and mchardyea:2005 , respectively, and the tremaineea:2002 M_ BH relation to estimate BH mass. The BH mass estimates for Ark 564 and NGC 5506 were taken from zhangwang:2006 and wangzhang:2007 , respectively.

Summary of the acceptable model fits = = 1. A simple PL model describes well the spectra of Fairall 9, Akn 564 and Mkn 766. 2. In NGC 4051, NGC 3227, and NGC 5548 a statistically acceptable global fit to the data is obtained with the PLG model. 3. In NGC 3783 and NGC 5506, the addition of an absorption edge with energy fixed at 7.1 keV is needed to achieve an acceptable model fit (ePLG). 4. In MCG 6-30-15 the best global fit is reached by the ePLG model but only when the edge energy is allowed to vary (the resulting mean edge energy is E _ edge 7.4 keV). 5. Finally, the same ePLG model provides an acceptable global fit to the NGC 3516 spectra but only when both the energy of the iron line and the energy of the absorption edge were left free to vary ( E _ line 6.1 , and E _ edge 7.95 ).

An iron K_ line is detected in many spectra of all sources (except for Akn 564, Fairall 9 and Mrk 766). To investigate the line's EW variations, we considered the spectral fit results in the case when the line is detected at the 2 level in a spectrum of a source. The number of spectra which do show such a strong line, and the average equivalent width, EW , using the individual EW values from these spectra only, are listed in Tab. tab:tab4 . The fourth column in Tab. tab:tab4 lists the ^2 values when we fit a constant to the EW light curves. The result is that we do not detect significant EW variations, except for NGC 5548, MCG -6-30-15, and NGC 3516.

sources.summary: The paper describes Akn 564 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 whose highly ionised gas line ratios and kinematics were modeled as dusty-torus gas accelerated outward.

quotes.quote: Akn 564 used new spectroscopic data on the NLS1 Akn 564, and extended the modeling techniques described in . They were able to build a self consistent model which explains both the line ratios and kinematics of the highly ionised gas. In essence this model invokes gas liberated from the the dusty torus, and then accelerated to its terminal velocity. In principle this model can be tested and constrained using additional HIL species, and high spatial resolution spectroscopy.

sources.summary: The paper treats Akn 564 as an exceptional AGN with a second low-frequency break, high accretion-state behavior, and X-ray variability stronger than optical variability.

quotes.quote: The only exception is Akn 564, which exhibits a second low-frequency break arev06a,mchardy07 . The high accretion rate m 1 , rom04 led mchardy07 to suggest that this source is analagous to the VHS.

The only possible exception appears to be Akn 564, where the X-ray variability amplitude is larger at all time time scales. Akn 564 is noteworthy as it is the only AGN known to exhibit a second low-frequency break, and the additional X-ray variability may be due to an increase in the strength of the X-ray power-law component, such as is seen in GBH in the VHS.

sources.summary: The paper cites Ark 564 as the first tentative negative X-ray time-lag detection, processes its piled-up observations, and places it among low-spin AGN candidates.

quotes.quote: After their first tentative detection in the AGN Ark 564 mchardy07 , where an origin in reflection from the accretion disc was first proposed, the first statistical significant detection came from fabian09 for the AGN 1H 0707-495.

We found that only three observations of Ark 564 were affected by moderate pile-up with Obs. IDs: 0670130201, 0670130501 and 0670130901. In these cases, we used an annular region to exclude the innermost source emission having a radius, in pixels, of: 280, 200 and 250, respectively.
On the other hand, the best-fitting spin parameters differ significantly from unity in the case of NGC 4051, Ark 564, 1H 0707-495 and NGC 3516 suggesting that they may host a non-rotating BH.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ark 564 as a highly variable NLS1 with previous reverberation-lag studies, high inferred iron abundance, and strong iron-band and soft-excess features.

quotes.quote: Ark 564 Ark 564 is a very variable NLS1 in the X-ray band. Detailed studies of its X-ray reverberation lags with \ observations have been done in previous analyses e.g. kara13 . A high iron abundance ( Z_ Fe /Z_ 3 ) was obtained by analysing the simultaneous \ and \ spectra above 1 keV kara17 .
A very strong emission line feature is shown in the iron band and a very strong soft excess is shown below 3 keV.

sources.summary: The paper treats Ark 564 as a bright type 1 AGN template for simulations, using its reverberation feature, disc-density constraints, X-ray flux, and lag-energy spectra.

quotes.quote: Set up We simulate an observation with 260 ks XMM-Newton exposure and 100 ks exposure on each focal plane module (FPM) of NuSTAR , basing our source parameters on Ark 564. This is a perfect target source, since an iron K reverberation feature has previously been detected in its lag-energy spectrum Kara2013,Kara2016 , and disc density constraints have also been placed from X-ray spectral fits Jiang2019AGN .
Consistent with Ark 564, the 1-10 keV flux of the model is 3.55 10^ 11 ergs cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .
We again base our lag-energy spectra on Ark 564 Kara2013 , simulated for the two frequency ranges considered by Kara2016 : [ 4-20 ] 10^ 5 Hz and [ 3-11 ] 10^ 4 Hz.

sources.summary: The paper includes ARK 564 among five AGN whose power spectra flatten with energy and are modeled with energy-dependent emissivity profiles.

quotes.quote: We apply strict data criteria (both in rejecting observations with an insufficient number of Fourier bins at frequencies below the white noise cutoff, and sources for which there were too few observations to provide error estimates), leaving us with five AGN: IRAS 13224-3809, 1H 0707-495, NGC 4051, ARK 564 and MRK 766. In all five cases, we observe that the power-law index -- which well describes the power spectrum in this frequency range (and is preferred over a broken power-law according to maximum likelihood fitting) -- is steep at low energies and becomes increasingly flat towards higher energies. Adopting the framework of 2006ArevaloUttley , we model the power-spectra of our AGN sample in five energy bands, assuming energy-dependent changes are driven by propagation only, and extract the emissivity index as a function of energy, parameterising it as a linear function. This modelling is remarkably successful in most cases, with the index starting at < 3 at low energies (which may well be consistent with an ADAF providing some of the soft excess emission, e.g. 2012DoneIntrinsicDiscEmission ) and rising to > 4 above 4-6 keV.

sources.summary: The paper models Ark 564 as a high-Eddington narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, using NuSTAR and XMM-Newton reverberation data to constrain its iron line, corona, black-hole mass, and soft excess.

quotes.quote: Ark 564 is an extreme high-Eddington Narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, known for being one of the brightest, most rapidly variable soft X-ray AGN, and for having one of the lowest temperature coronae. Here we present a 410 ks NuSTAR observation and two 115 ks XMM-Newton observations of this unique source, which reveal a very strong, relativistically broadened iron line. We compute the Fourier-resolved time lags by first using Gaussian processes to interpolate the NuSTAR gaps, implementing the first employment of multi-task learning for application in AGN timing. By fitting simultaneously the time lags and the flux spectra with the relativistic reverberation model reltrans , we constrain the mass at 2.3^ +2.6 _ 1.3 10^6M_ , although additional components are required to describe the prominent soft excess in this source.

sources.summary: The paper reports strong coronal and molecular emission in ARK 564, detects P and Ni lines, and argues that shocks contribute little to its Fe emission.

quotes.quote: We also report the detection of [ Fe 13 ] 1.074,1.079 m in three of our objects and the first detection of [ P 2 ] 1.188 m and [ Ni 2 ] 1.191 m in a Seyfert 1 galaxy, ARK 564. Using the ratio [ P 2 ] / [ Fe 2 ] we deduced that most Fe present in the outer NLR of ARK 564 is locked up in grains, and the influence of shocks is negligible.

For ARK 564, the only galaxy in which the [ P 2 ] 1.188 m line was detected, we found that the [ Fe 2 ] / [ P 2 ] ratio is 0.82, suggesting that shocks fronts have little or no importance in the production of the [ Fe 2 ] emission. oli01 also points out that [ Fe 2 ] / [ P 2 ] almost solely depends on the Fe/P relative abundance. According to their calculations, a solar Fe/P 100 abundance ratio implies in a [ Fe 2 ] / [ P 2 ] 50 (i.e. assuming that all iron is in gaseous phase), quite near to the value measured in supernovae remnants, where fast shocks destroy most the grains. The low ratio found in ARK 564 ( [ Fe 2 ] / [ P 2 ] = 0.82) indicates that most of the Fe is locked up into grains.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ark 564 as extended along a known triple radio structure, with the radio peak coincident with the optical nucleus and poorly constrained faint VLBI structure.

quotes.quote: Ark 564 (2240+294) Y : Although our map does not clearly resolve the triple structure extending to 320 pc in P.A. = 6 ^ seen by (8.4 GHz, VLA A array), it is extended in the direction of this structure. Our radio peak coincides with the optical nucleus Clements81 within the errors. The total flux density at 5 GHz is the same as that quoted by within the errors. G : This is the faintest source in our sample and was detected on the least number of baselines.

sources.summary: The paper reports that Ark 564 has high dust extinction, similar Ly-fluorescence contributions in both regions, and a detected 1.3165 micron narrow component.

quotes.quote: For three sources, namely, Mrk 817, NGC 4151, and NGC 4593, the dust extinction is much below this value, whereas for another three sources, namely, Mrk 876, Ark 564, and HE 1228 + 013, it appears to be as high as A_ V 2 mag.
Three sources, namely, Mrk 509, H 2106 099, and Ark 564, have in both emission line regions similar contributions from Ly fluorescence of 50 , 65 , and 75 , respectively.
Similarly, 1.3165 m narrow components were detected in 3C 273 and Ark 564, but not in HE 1228 + 013 and H 1934 063.

sources.summary: The paper finds Ark 564 is well fit by the PLC+RDC interpretation, lacks narrow iron or absorption lines, and has a spin consistent with prior blurred-reflection work.

quotes.quote: Ark 564: The basic PLC+RDC interpretation provides a good fit to the data. No narrow iron emission or absorption lines are detected. The spin obtained here is consistent with that inferred from the inner radius obtained by , who modelled the long 2005 \ observation with blurred reflection.

sources.summary: The review describes Ark 564 as an AGN whose lag-frequency behavior shows a step change; its low-frequency lag-energy spectrum is featureless and resembles hard lags in black-hole binaries.

quotes.quote: Prompted by the similarity with BHXRBs, mchardy07 compared the lag-frequency dependence of the AGN Ark 564 over a broad range of Fourier frequencies with that seen in BHXRBs, finding evidence for a step-change in the lags rather than a continuous power-law dependence of lag and Fourier-frequency, similar to what is seen in hard and intermediate state BHXRBs, and suggestive of different processes producing the lags on different time-scales.

The differences between the low and high frequency lag-energy spectra of Ark 564 can be seen in Fig. ark564 kara13c . The low-frequency lag-energy spectrum, with no strong spectral features, is very similar to that of the hard lags found in black hole binaries, which can be understood as intrinsic lags associated with the continuum, possibly linked to the propagation of variations in the accretion flow which modulate the coronal emission kotov01,arevalo06,uttley11 .

sources.summary: The paper includes Ark 564 as a highly X-ray-variable radio-quiet AGN, reporting a large 95 GHz excess and an Eddington ratio exceeding unity in the sample table.

quotes.quote: Six of the targets, namely NGC 3783, NGC 5548, NGC 7469, NGC 3227, Ark 564, and Mrk 766, are highly variable in the X-rays according to the extensive monitoring of markowitz04 .
The excess is large ( 4 - 7 ) for NGC 3783, ARK 564, and NGC 3227, and smaller (but still > 1 ) for MR 2251-178, NGC 5506, and NGC 7469.
The present sample includes high L_ bol /L_ Edd sources, as listed in Table  MBH (exceeding unity for NGC 7469 and Ark 564).

sources.summary: The paper presents the first NuSTAR observation of Ark 564, a steep-spectrum Narrow-line Seyfert 1, finding strong soft-excess and iron-line behavior plus one of NuSTAR's lowest-temperature coronae.

quotes.quote: firstpage - lastpage 2015 Ark 564 is an archetypal Narrow line Seyfert 1 that has been well observed in soft X-rays from 0.3--10 keV, revealing a steep spectrum, strong soft excess, iron K emission line and dramatic variability on the order of hours.
We report here on the first NuSTAR observation of Ark 564.
These spectral results show that Ark 564 has one of the lowest temperature coronae observed by NuSTAR to date.

sources.summary: The monitoring review identifies Ark 564 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 with very low long-term line and continuum variability and atypically narrow lines.

quotes.quote: Here we present some interesting findings of the long-term (more than a decade) optical monitoring campaign of a sample of seven type 1 AGN of different spectral types: Seyfert 1 galaxies (NGC 5548, NGC 4151, NGC 7469), Narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy - NLSy 1 (Ark 564), double-peaked line radio loud (3C 390.3) and radio quiet (Arp 102B) galaxy, and a luminous quasar (E1821+643), a supermassive binary black hole candidate.
The least variable objects are the NLSy1 Ark 564 and the highly luminous quasar E1821+643, for which the line and continuum flux are almost constant for decades (for the H line, F _ var 7 ).

It is worth noticing that in Figure fig:4 , if we discard Ark 564 (with very narrow lines that are not typical for type 1 AGNs and with a very large error-bars in estimated time-delays) and E1821+643 (a quasar with an outstanding luminosity), the remaining objects will apparently closely follow the dependence of .

sources.summary: The paper describes Ark 564 as a bright nearby Seyfert 1 galaxy with a prominent soft X-ray excess, redshift z 0.025, and high foreground column density.

quotes.quote: Ark 564 is a bright Seyfert 1 galaxy with a prominent soft X-ray excess that has been a prominent topic of study in recent years 43 . It is even closer than the previous galaxies with z = 0.025 and has an appreciable column density of N_ H = 5.41 10^ 20 cm ^ 2 . This column density is the highest of those previously mentioned, meaning it provides a high likelihood of displaying carbon features not present in the instrument itself.

sources.summary: Akn 564 is used as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 comparison, with prior monitoring showing a slower soft-excess component, different soft-hump/power-law amplitudes, and possibly saturated corona cooling.

quotes.quote: This is particularly interesting since the results of a 35-day long observational campaign on the NLS1 Arakelian (Akn) 564 indicate that superimposed on a fast-varying continuum component that dominates the spectrum is a slower-varying soft excess emission Akn564I,Edelsonea01 .

In Akn 564, Akn564I showed that the different amplitudes of variability of the soft hump and power-law ( 6.44 3.30 , compared to 3.97 0.06 ) explain the gross change in softness ratio across the observation.

The correlation observed between the soft hump flux and the photon index in Ton S180 is expected in disk-corona models where an increase of the flux in the soft X-ray/UV component can cool the corona and steepen the power-law continuum Haardtea94,PDO95 . This is not the case for Akn 564, where Akn564I suggest the corona cooling might be saturated.

gmw_00000325.name: Mrk 110
gmw_00000325.overview: Mrk 110 is a nearby, radio-quiet narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1801.03519, arXiv:2206.12497] classified as an Sa spiral galaxy [arXiv:1610.00429] that exhibits large-scale tidal tails [arXiv:2307.06087]. The galaxy is highly variable across multiple wavebands [arXiv:astro-ph/0203238, arXiv:2104.04530], hosting a central supermassive black hole [arXiv:astro-ph/0203238] and a nearly pole-on accretion disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0311283].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 110 as a well-studied radio-quiet NLS1 with extended diffuse emission to the north, west, and south, plus a substantial diffuse flux fraction.

quotes.quote: J0925+5217 Mrk 110 is a well studied radio-quiet NLS1, detected by NVSS and often studied at 5 GHz, finding fluxes consistent with our measurements Nelson00, Wu05, Kataoka11 . Its core has a rather flat spectral index, while the total flux is steep. At VLBA scales, this object shows only a point-like core of 1.1 mJy Doi13 . Our map (Fig. fig:J0925p5217 ) shows an extended diffuse emission toward North, West and South. In particular, at PA 209 ^ the emission reaches a distance of 5.8 arcsec (11.4 kpc), while a bright spot can be found at PA 16 ^ at 2.3 arcsec (4.5 kpc).

The emission in the North direction was already detected by Kukula98 . The diffuse flux represents approximately 51 of the source total emission. The unresolved core in our image has a size of 200 130 pc.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mkn 110 to motivate AGN time-delay simulations, describing it as a nearby radio-intermediate narrow-line Seyfert 1 with disk-reverberation measurements and complex multi-timescale variability.

quotes.quote: Complex variability was recently discussed in detail for the source Mkn 110 mkn1102021 . So, in order to put our simulations onto realistic footing, we predominantly focus on parameters well representing this source and the light curve duration and spacing characteristic for this source. Mkn 110 Mkn1969 is a well-studied nearby optically bright, radio-intermediate (R 1.6) narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1s) at a redshift z = 0.036 see e.g., dasgupta2006 . As implied by the relativistically broadened X-ray emission line (O vii ), the cold, standard disk extends there at least up to 20-100 r_g reeves2021 and Fe K line study implied that the cold disk down to 1.24 r_g is needed mantovani2016 , although we note that such a fit was achieved for unlikely inclination of 80 deg. A more recent study of combined data from XMM-Newton and NuSTAR gave more conservative values of 20 r_g porquet2021 .

The source has been monitored with Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory 2004ApJ...611.1005G , with a cadence roughly once per day, for about 200 days, and we adopted this setup in our simulations. A detailed disk reverberation is studied by mkn1102021 , using good cadence data from Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory and LCO network and they found the variability on two different time scales in Mkn 110. The variability time scale below ten days is mostly consistent with accretion disk reverberation with a maximum two-day lag between the shortest wavelength (W2 band) and longest z band.

sources.summary: The paper finds Mrk 110 has compact core radio emission with possible outflow-related extension and an apparently strong radio/X-ray lag that becomes statistically insignificant under red-noise testing.

quotes.quote: Mrk 110: The spectral indices indicate that the radio emission is optically thin ( < -0.5 ) at 1--5 GHz and is optically thick ( > -0.5 ) at 5--9 GHz (see Table target ). The radio images at 1.4 and 5 GHz display extended emission which may be associated with an outflow Kukula1998,Berton2018 .

Mrk 110 exhibits a compact core in both A and B configurations, as their integrated flux densities agree within 1 , which is consistent with previous studies (see Section 2). Faint extended linear structure is present in the B configuration, possibly associated with an outflow. The D-configuration image shows some extended emission, with an integrated flux density which is about twice the higher resolution images (see Table allimages ).

In Mrk 110, the measured p() lies well above the Pearson p() for all values, reaching a factor of 10^3 for the highest || values. For example, the highest correlation of = -0.89 found above, is formally highly significant with a Pearson p = 1.0 10^ 6 . However, there are 20 delays with || 0.89 out of the 3800 delays tested, so the probability to find such a high correlation by chance in the uncorrelated data sets is actually 20/3800 = 0.5 , which is a factor of 5 10^3 higher than the value from the Pearson correlation. The probability of getting || 0.89 by chance in the 200 correlations we made in the range between t_1 - 100 and t_2 + 100 days is therefore 0.5 200 = 100 .

sources.summary: The paper presents Mrk 110 as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 observed in a dense HET variability campaign, with low continuum flux, BLR ionization stratification, and virial black-hole mass estimates.

quotes.quote: We present results of a variability campaign of Mrk 110 performed with the 9.2-m Hobby-Eberly Telescope (HET) at McDonald Observatory. The high S/N spectra cover most of the optical range. They were taken from 1999 November through 2000 May. The average interval between the observations was 7.3 days and the median interval was only 3.0 days. Mrk 110 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy.

During our campaign the continuum flux was in a historically low stage. Considering the delays of the emission lines with respect to the continuum variations we could verify an ionization stratification of the BLR. We derived virial masses of the central black hole from the radial distances of the different emission lines and from their widths.

sources.summary: The paper uses Mrk 110 to compare orbital and gravitational-redshift black-hole masses, finding a larger orientation-independent mass and a nearly pole-on accretion disk inclination.

quotes.quote: We derived a central black hole mass of M_ orbital =1.8 0.4 10^ 7 M_ in the Seyfert galaxy Mrk 110 assuming the broad emission lines are generated in gas clouds orbiting within an accretion disk. This figure depends on the inclination angle of the accretion disk. Here we report on the detection of gravitational redshifted emission in the variable fraction of the broad emission lines. We derive a central black hole mass of M_ grav =14.0 3.0 10^ 7 M_ . These measurements are independent on the orientation of the accretion disk.

For the narrow line Seyfert galaxy Mrk 110 we derive a nearly pole-on line-of-sight of the central accretion disk. The inclination angle i amounts to 21 5 . A disk inclination angle of 30 20 has been previously estimated from line profile variations (Kollatschny ).

sources.summary: The paper analyzes XMM-Newton spectra of Mrk 110, describing it as a low-redshift X-ray-bright NLS1 with broad-line gravitational redshift signatures, complex X-ray emission, and a Comptonized accretion-disc layer.

quotes.quote: Mrk 110 is a low-redshift ( z=0.03529 ) X-ray bright NLS1s. Studies of the kinematics in the central Broad Line Region (BLR) of Mrk 110 based on the variability of the Balmer and Helium (He I, He II) emission lines, indicated a connection between the BLR and the central accretion disc as well as gravitational redshift effects (Kollatschny 2004). From the detection of gravitational redshifted emission in the variable fraction of all broad optical emission lines in Mrk 110, a central black hole mass of M_ grav = (14 3) \ 10^7 M_ sun is obtained.

We have analyzed the XMM-Newton spectra of Mrk 110. The EPIC pn spectrum revealed the presence of a Comptonized accretion disc layer and the presence of a starburst component. Ionized disc models, optically thick emission from the disc and hybrid thermal/non-thermal emission models appear to be ruled out by the data. Mrk 110 is another active galaxy showing a Comptonized accretion disc layer. The optical data from Mrk 110 show asymmetric line profiles which are interpreted as gravitational redshift effects from the central black hole.

sources.summary: The paper reports energy-dependent hard-band X-ray delays in Mrk 110 and argues that broadband observations can distinguish soft-excess models for the galaxy.

quotes.quote: Mrk 110 is another NLS1 galaxy that shows evidence for time delay . The time delay increases as the hard band becomes harder. Figure f2 shows the time lag as a function of energy. The delay is proportional to the logE , consistent with simple Comptonization model .

We have fitted the spectrum of Mrk 110 with the smeared wind absorption, blurred ionized reflection and thermal Comptonization models. The power law and Compton reflection from distant material were common to these models. The three models describe the observed data statistically equally well in the 0.3-10 band but differ significantly above 10 .

sources.summary: The paper notes Mrk 0110’s SDSS variability offset, poorly constrained host luminosity, and host-template degeneracy while identifying it as an Sa galaxy.

quotes.quote: Mrk 0110 (No. 9) shows a clear discrepancy in the SDSS data due to extreme variability, and therefore has a very large ^2 . Here, we are not considering Mrk 0110 (No. 9) since L_ host is not well constrained for this source.
The SED of Mrk 0110 shows large offset in the SDSS data points. J12a discussed this source and mentioned that it is an extreme example of this behaviour.

For Mrk 0110, all the hostpol templates provide the same fit-statistic and even the same spectral parameters. However, Mrk 0110 is identified as an Sa galaxy 2012AstL...38..475K . Hence, in the main text, we mentioned the SED of spiral galaxy type-a ( host 02) as the best-fit hostpol model for this object.

sources.summary: The paper presents intensive continuum reverberation mapping of Mrk 110, finding wavelength-dependent short-timescale lags and longer optical lags that imply diffuse broad-line-region continuum contributions.

quotes.quote: We present the first intensive continuum reverberation mapping study of the high accretion-rate Seyfert galaxy Mrk 110. The source was monitored almost daily for more than 200 days with the Swift X-ray and UV/optical telescopes, supported by ground-based observations from Las Cumbres Observatory, the Liverpool Telescope, and the Zowada Observatory, thus extending the wavelength coverage to 9100 . Mrk 110 was found to be significantly variable at all wavebands. Analysis of the intraband lags reveals two different behaviours, depending on the timescale. On timescales shorter than 10 days the lags, relative to the shortest UV waveband ( 1928 ), increase with increasing wavelength up to a maximum of 2 d lag for the longest waveband ( 9100 ), consistent with the expectation from disc reverberation.

On longer timescales, however, the g-band lags the Swift BAT hard X-rays by 10 days, with the z-band lagging the g-band by a similar amount, which cannot be explained in terms of simple reprocessing from the accretion disc. We interpret this result as an interplay between the emission from the accretion disc and diffuse continuum radiation from the broad line region.

sources.summary: The paper measures multi-band lags in Mrk 110 across three epochs, finding luminosity-dependent U-band and X-ray excess lags attributed to changing BLR contribution.

quotes.quote: We have measured the wavelength-dependent lags between the X-ray, UV and optical bands in the high accretion rate ( L/L_ Edd 40 ) Active Galactic Nucleus Mrk 110 during two intensive monitoring campaigns in February and September 2019. We divide the observations into three intervals with different X-ray luminosities. The first interval, already published in Vincentelli et al. (2021), has the lowest X-ray luminosity and did not exhibit the U-band excess positive lag, or the X-ray excess negative lag that is seen in most AGN. However, these excess lags are seen in the two subsequent intervals of higher X-ray luminosity.

Recent intensive monitoring observations of the high accretion rate AGN Mrk 110 40 Eddington ratio, Meyer-Hofmeister , showed behaviour consistent with a combination of an accretion disc and the BLR, but without an X-ray or U-band excess vincentelli+2021 . In this Letter, we investigate this combination further and, in particular, the variation of the BLR contribution to the lags as a function of luminosity. We present new Swift observations of Mrk 110, which we combine with all archival data.

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 0921+525/Mrk 110 as an X-ray bright NLS1 with radio variability, complex VLA structure, circumnuclear star-formation-like extended emission, and a compact VLBA component.

quotes.quote: PG 0921+525 (Mrk 110) is an X-ray bright NLS1 at low redshift of z = 0.03529 1985ApJ...294..106V . 2006ApJ...651L..13D found significant X-ray variability in Mrk 110 which showed energy-dependent time lag. The time-scale of the variability constrained the size of X-ray emitting region to a few Schwarzschild radii. also found significant radio variability on mas scales at 5 GHz from days to weeks time-scales in PG 0921+525.

The overall structure looks like a highly curved jet or ring-like structure. The extended emission is consistent with circumnuclear star formation, similar to ultra-luminuous infrared galaxies 2017MNRAS.469..916B . PG 0921+525 presents an unresolved feature in our 5-GHz VLBA image with a flux density of 0.92 mJy.

sources.summary: PG 0921+525 has curved arcsecond-scale radio emission and a compact VLBI core-jet structure whose steep spectrum favors a weak jet over a corona.

quotes.quote: The early 5-GHz VLA B-array image of PG 0921+525 (Mrk 110, z = 0.03529 ) shows a highly curved radio structure that initially extends to the northwest and then turns to the north 1994AJ....108.1163K . In the subsequent 1.4-GHz VLA A-array image, the northern extended structure reaches about 2 5 1998MNRAS.297..366K . The latest 5-GHz tapered VLA A-array image reveals extended radio emission up to 4 5 to the south of the core, in addition to the extended radio emission to the north 2022A&A...658A..12J .

The steeper spectral index of the radio core tends to support it being the base of a weak jet rather than a corona. Due to the mixing of the radio core with the optically thin jet emission, we need to detect the genuine optically thick radio core at higher frequencies. In the 1.6-GHz VLBA image (Fig. fig:0921 a ), no extended jet is detected, while the spectral index between 1.6 GHz and 7.6 GHz is slightly steeper, ^ 7.6 _ 1.6 = -0.66 0.04 . In addition, in our new VLBA images (Fig. fig:0921 b and Fig. fig:0921 ), a compact jet-like feature is found to the southwest of the core, again supporting the core-jet structure.

sources.summary: The paper reports XMM-Newton hard X-ray delays in the Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 110, modeling its spectrum and energy-dependent lag as Comptonization near the black hole.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of hard X-ray delays in the X-ray emission of the Seyfert 1 galaxy Mrk 110, based on a long XMM-Newton observation. Cross correlation between the X-ray light curves of different energy bands reveals an energy dependent delay ranging from a few minutes to an hour. We find that the energy spectrum can be modeled by Comptonization of disk blackbody photons. The energy dependent delay can be modeled as due to the effect of Comptonization in a hot plasma confined within 10 Schwarzschild radius of the black hole.

Mrk 110 is a nearby optically bright, radio intermediate ( R 1.6 ) narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1s) at a redshift z=0.036. The optical continuum and the broad emission lines of Mrk 110 are highly variable by a factor of 2 to 8 within a timescale of 10 years - kw01,kol03 . Mrk 110 was observed on 2004 November 15 by XMM-Newton for 47.4 ks.

sources.summary: The paper applies a gravitational-redshift virial-factor method to Mrk 110, finding large line-dependent virial factors, a face-on BLR interpretation, and a correlation with BLR radius.

quotes.quote: The virial factors f are derived by equation (9) for the broad lines He II, He I, H and H (see Table 1). Considering the errors of v_ FWHM and z_ g , the f distribution is generated with equation (9) by 10^4 realizations of Monte Carlo simulation. The mean and standard deviation of this distribution are regarded as the expectation and uncertainty of f , respectively. The helium and hydrogen lines have f 8--16 that are larger than the average f 1 usually accepted for the reverberation mapped AGNs. These f values of the helium lines are slightly smaller than those of the hydrogen lines.
If f is fully generated by the oblique effect of the disc-like BLRs, 7--10 degrees (see Table 1), which confirm the nearly face-on view of accretion disc in Mrk 110 suggested by Ko03a,Ko03b .

sources.summary: The overview cites Mrk 110 as an AGN where monitoring and line-profile modeling support gravitationally affected optical emission lines, enabling estimates of central mass and accretion-disk inclination.

quotes.quote: Therefore, they used the rms profile of the spectra from the monitoring campaign of NGC5548 Peterson99 , NGC4593 Koll97 and Mrk 110 Koll03 , measured the shift between the rms profile and the mean profile, and identified the differential shift as gravitational redshift. analyzed the shift in four emission lines H , H , HeI 5876 and HeII 4686 and found the strongest z_ grav in the HeII 4686, as it originates closest to the black hole. The confirmation that the optical emission lines in Mrk 110 are gravitationally affected was given by , where emission lines are modeled under the assumption that they originate in the vicinity of a black hole.

the inclination angle of the accretion disk in the case of Mrk 110, assumed the accretion disk model of the BLR. Under this assumption, the comparison between M_ grav and M_ orbital give an estimation of the orientation angle i of the accretion disk : M_ orbital / M_ grav = sin^ 2 i . Therefore, this method makes it possible to estimate the inclination angle of the accretion disk for radio-quiet AGNs.

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 110 as an NLS1 with a narrow neutral Fe K line; its relativistic reflection component mainly explains the soft X-ray excess.

quotes.quote: Mrk 110 Mrk 110 is a NLS1 veron06 and has been observed by \ once for a net pn exposure of 33 ks. boller07 shows a complete analysis of the RGS and EPIC spectra. Only a narrow Fe K emission line was found previously.

The relativistic disc reflection model accounts for mainly the soft excess below 2 keV. The lack of the broad Fe K emission line in the iron band might be due to the extremely blurred reflection component, as seen in our modelling. Only an upper limit of the disc density ( (n_ e )<16.5 ) is found.

sources.summary: The paper reconstructs Mrk 110 temperature maps with clear alternating radial structure that moves outward and then inward, remaining visible under substantial smoothing.

quotes.quote: Mrk 110 In Figure fig:mrk110_panel , we show the temperature maps constructed using N_t = 100 and ranging from 1 to 1000. In Figure fig:mrk110_lc , we show the observed lightcurves and the model lightcurves constructed using =10 . Similar to NGC 5548 and Fairall 9, there is clear radial structure observed in the 10 models in the form of alternating positive and negative temperature fluctuations in radius.

These fluctuations move slowly outwards together, and then reverse near 8110 days, moving inward. In the = 100 model, the overall structures are less coherent, but the ingoing pattern after 8110 days is still visible. It is arguably still visible in the =1000 model.

sources.summary: PG0921+525 has three radio components: a core, a nearby optically thick new ejection, and a farther optically thin older ejection; the core dominates the VLBA emission.

quotes.quote: PG0921+525 has three components. The slopes of C1 ( _ total = -0.1 ) and C2 ( _ total = +0.4 ) are flat/inverted, and that of C3 ( _ total < -0.8 ) is steep. The T_ B is high for C1 ( > 10^ 7.7 K), and is lower for C2 ( 10^ 6.4 K) and C3 ( 10^ 6.8 K). C1 and C2 are consistent with the Gaia position ( 1.9 and 2.3 mas), and C3 is farther away ( 9.5 mas). The above properties suggest that C1 may be the core, C2 may be a new ejection as it is still optically thick and very close to the core, and C3 may be an older ejection as it is already optically thin and farther away from the core.
The radio emission is dominated by the core component, as indicated by the VLBA core/total flux ratio ( > 0.7 ) and the VLBA/VLA flux ratio ( < 0.9 ).

sources.summary: The paper excludes Mrk 110 from optical correlations because of strong variability but uses it in [O III]-SED work, where its X-ray and [O III] luminosities remain consistent.

quotes.quote: We also exclude Mrk 110 from the optical correlations (Sections 3, 4 and 5) as this source shows strong optical variability (Kollatschny et al. 2001; Kollatschny 2003) and the SDSS spectrum is very different from the (non-simultaneous) XMM-Newton OM data (see Paper-I). However, the [ OIII ] line luminosity does not change with the optical continuum, so we include this object in the [ OIII ] versus broad band SED correlations in Section 7.

Mrk 110's optical continuum is highly variable (Kollatschny et al. 2001; Kollatschny 2003). The SDSS spectrum of Mrk 110 is 1 order of magnitude less luminous than the FAST optical spectrum (Landt et al. 2011), which is just the required amount of correction we need to pull Mrk 110 back onto the best-fit correlation line. Mrk 110 (green circle) sits on the best fit line as neither its X-ray nor its [ OIII ] line luminosity are affected by the optical continuum variability.

sources.summary: The paper models Mrk 110 as a Narrow Line Seyfert 1 with soft X-ray spectrum, cutoff energy near 40 keV, and thermal-corona electron temperature around 10-20 keV.

quotes.quote: We study X-ray spectra of four Narrow Line Seyfert 1 galaxies (NLS1s), Mrk 110, SWIFT J2127.4+5654, IGR J16185 5928, and WKK 4438, using Suzaku and NuSTAR data. The spectra of the four sources are reproduced with a model consisting of a cutoff power law component, a reflection component, and a soft excess component.
