While this gives an indication of the spectral shape, a more complex spectrum was observed both in co-added Swift XRT spectra, where hints of an Fe line is seen Paliya2014 , and in a recent, deep observation ( Kynoch2017 ; D'Ammando et al., in prep).

While radio-quiet AGN commonly show a softer-when-brighter trend ( Sobolewska2009,Soldi2014 ), the only ray NLSy1s for which this kind of variability has been claimed is 1H 0323+342 (from XRT observations, Paliya2014 ). However, the recent observation of this source instead reveals a harder-when-brighter trend (D'Ammando et al., in prep).
Only 1H 0323+342 has a comparable ray luminosity, while some of the other sources have average ray luminosities larger by about three orders of magnitudes ( Abdo2009b ).

sources.summary: The paper uses published VLBA measurements for 1H 0323+342, notes its jet-shape break, and discusses how a larger black-hole mass would move the break radius closer to the sample range.

quotes.quote: The recently observed jet shape in 1H 0323+342 Hada18 demonstrates a similar behavior.
For NLSy1 1H 0323+342 we use 1.4--2.3 GHz measurements from VLBA observations Hada18 , with k_1=0.6 and k_2=1.41 , for which the jet shape break point position is estimated.

The black hole mass of 1H 0323+342 is suspected to be underestimated LeonTavares14, Hada18 . If we use for this source the mass M=10^ 8.6 M_ , obtained using the relation between black hole mass and bulge luminosity LeonTavares14 , 1H 0323+342 yields r_ break =5.6 10^6r_ g , falling much closer to the discussed above range of r_ break /r_ g distances. This may provide an additional argument favoring a higher black hole mass for this source.

sources.summary: The paper studies 1H 0323+342 as a gamma-ray radio-loud NLS1 whose distorted radio jet and relic structures support a merger-driven, precessing black-hole-binary interpretation.

quotes.quote: We investigated distorted radio structures associated with the nearest gamma-ray emitting, radio-loud NLS1 1H 0323+342. This provides supporting evidence for the merger hypothesis based on the past optical/near-infrared observations of its host galaxy. The anomalous radio morphology consists of two different structures, the inner curved structure of currently active jet and the outer linear structure of low-brightness relics. Such a coexistence might be indicative of the stage of an established black hole binary with precession before the black holes coalesce in the galaxy merger process. 1H 0323+342 and other radio-loud NLS1s under galaxy interactions may be extreme objects on the evolutionary path from radio-quiet NLS1s to normal Seyfert galaxies with larger SMBHs in classical bulges through mergers and merger-induced jet phases.

sources.summary: The paper finds multiple Swift-UVOT short-timescale variability events for 1H 0323+342, with weaker optical variability attributed to host-galaxy contamination and accretion-disc contribution.

quotes.quote: 1H 0323 + 342 : the source has been monitored on daily basis between 2010 October 28 and November 30 (see Fig. 0323_lc in Appendix UVOT_Appendix ), showing five variability events in w2 , the best sampled filter, and in particular four events between 2010 November 5 and 10 (see Fig. 0323_lc_zoom ). No significant events have been observed in v filter, where the number of exposures is comparable to the w2 filter. The lower variability observed in v filter (and more generally in the optical part of the spectrum, see the long-term variability amplitude in Table Amplitude_summed in Appendix UVOT_Appendix ) for 1H 0323 + 342 can be due to a higher contamination from thermal emission from the host galaxy. Moreover, in this source the accretion disc can contribute more significantly to the total emission in optical--UV. In the other filters only one event has been observed in 2010 November, but it can be due to a sparse sampling.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H 0323+342 as a local active galaxy in its bare-AGN sample and analyzes XMM-Newton/Swift X-ray spectra, including continuum, soft-excess, and spectral-fitting behavior.

quotes.quote: 1H 0323+342 is one of the closest ( z =0.0629) exotic NLS1 AGN that exhibits superluminal motion of its relativistic outflow. The origin of the X-ray emission from this source could be explained as due to interactions between the disc and the Compton cloud Paliya2019 . From the method of single-epoch spectrum for several broad emission lines, Landt2017 estimated its mass as 2 10^7 M_ . 1H 0323+342 has been observed with XMM-Newton and Swift /XRT, and the X-ray spectra showed the nature of a bare-AGN, such as soft-excess, low N_ H along the line of sight etc. The details of the observation log are given in Table table:obs_log .

sources.summary: The paper highlights 2MASX J0324+3410 as a remarkable NLS1-blazar composite with rapid multi-band variability, HBL-like SED, and a small disk/ring-like host.

quotes.quote: In this respect, the most remarkable object of this kind is perhaps 2MASX J0324+3410, found in our recent work zhou07 . It showed rapid variability in the radio, optical, and X-ray bands, and was even claimed to be marginally detected in TeV rays fal04 . Its non-thermal SED clearly resembles that of HBLs. Moreover, 2MASX J0324+3410 is hosted by a relatively small, apparent disk galaxy with one-armed spiral or ring galaxy morphology.

sources.summary: The paper notes J0324+3410 is a Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 with asymmetric spiral arms in its host, motivating fixed-aperture photometry and caution about polarized host starlight.

quotes.quote: J0324+3410: For this source we used a fixed 4 arcsec aperture. However, this Narrow-Line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1) has asymmetric spiral arms resolved in the host galaxy Zhou2007 . The spiral arms can potentially give non-zero polarization of the stellar light. A dedicated study is needed to clarify whether the starlight of the host has significant polarization.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1H 0323+342 as a blazar-like NLSy1 with strong X-ray variability, a small black hole mass, gamma-ray detection, and radio-loud classification.

quotes.quote: The strongest flux variation is observed in the blazar-like NLSy1 1H 0323+342, where its flux has increased by a factor of 10 after two days.
A small black hole mass of 10 ^ 7 M _ is residing in 1H 0323+342 which is a gamma-ray source, detected by Fermi , with a spectral energy distribution typical of BL Lac objects.
When data are available, NLSy1 in our sample are radio emitting objects, though not radio-loud, except for 1H 0323+342 which is a blazar-like NLSy1.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1H 0323 + 342 as a Fermi-detected NLSy1 with soft and hard X-ray detections and the only detected host-galaxy structure among the gamma-ray NLSy1 discussed.

quotes.quote: A clear soft X-ray emission component is observed also in the XMM-Newton observation of 1H 0323 + 342 collected in 23--24 August 2015 (D'Ammando et al., in prep.).
Only one source, 1H 0323 + 342, among the NLSy1 detected by Fermi LAT was detected in hard X-rays ( E> 10 keV).
Among the radio-loud NLSy1 detected in rays up to now, only for the closest one, 1H 0323 + 342 ( z = 0.061), the host galaxy structure was detected.

sources.summary: The review describes 1H 0323 + 342 as a gamma-ray narrow-line Seyfert 1 with relativistic jet evidence and an uncertain host morphology, either spiral-like or merger-ring-like.

quotes.quote: Apparent superluminal jet components were detected in SBS 0846+513 dammando12 , PMN J0948 + 0022, and 1H 0323 + 342 lister16 , supporting the presence of relativistic jets in this class of objects.

Among the NLSy1 detected by Fermi LAT up to now, the morphology of the host galaxy has been investigated only for 1H 0323 + 342, PKS 2004 447, and FBQS J1644 + 2619. Observations of 1H 0323 + 342 with the Hubble Space Telescope and the Nordic Optical Telescope revealed a structure that may be interpreted either as a one-armed spiral galaxy zhou07 or as a circumnuclear ring produced by a recent merger anton08, leon14 .

sources.summary: The INOV study identifies J032441.20 + 341045.0 as a monitored jetted RLNLSy1 with four low-redshift detection sessions and strong INOV activity possibly tied to high jet speed.

quotes.quote: Thus, three of these 8 sources, viz., J163323.59 + 471859.0, J164442.53 + 261913.3 and J170330.38 + 454047.3 are taken from the Doi2011ApJ...738..126D and the remaining 5 sources, viz., J032441.20 + 341045.0, J084957.98 + 510829.0, J094857.32 + 002225.6, J122222.99 + 041315.9 and J150506.48 + 032630.8 are taken from Zhou2007ApJ...658L..13Z , D'Ammando2012MNRAS.426..317D , Giroletti2011A&A...528L..11G , Lister2016AJ....152...12L and Orienti2012arXiv1205.0402O , respectively.

For instance, J032441.20 + 341045.0 has lowest polarization value (e.g., see Table tab:source_info ) and radio loudness value e.g., see Zhou2007ApJ...658L..13Z among 8 J- RLNLSy1s but shows strong INOV activity which perhaps could be due to very high jets speed (e.g., see Table tab:source_info ).

gmw_00000333.name: GN-z10-3
gmw_00000333.overview: GN-z10-3 is a compact, unlensed high-redshift galaxy candidate [arXiv:1809.07210, arXiv:1309.2280] with a Lyman-alpha emission line at a redshift of z=8.78 [arXiv:2104.08168]. Its spectral energy distribution is bimodal, preventing a clear distinction between a young stellar population and an older population with a Balmer break [arXiv:2104.08168].

sources.summary: The paper reports MOSFIRE spectroscopy of GN-z10-3, identifying a convincing Lyman-alpha line at z=8.78 and noting SED ambiguity between young emission and older Balmer-break solutions.

quotes.quote: We identified a convincing ( >5 ) emission line at =11891.4 1.3 \ with a FWHM=133 36km/s and an integrated flux of (1.37 0.24) 10 ^ 18 cgs (Figure GN-z10-3 ). No other line is present in the entire J-band spectra. Assuming this line is Lyman- , it corresponds to a redshift of z =8.78.

However, for GN-z10-3, despite a spectroscopic constraint of z =8.78, a re-analysis of the SED incorporating this value may still be consistent with a mature stellar population. This may appear surprising given the IRAC excess could be readily explained with strong [ ] 5007 \ emission. Figure GN-z10-3REV shows a re-analysis of the SED shown in Figure fig.sed with the redshift appropriately constrained at z=8.78 . The posterior distribution in age and stellar masses is now bimodal and thus it is not possible to distinguish between a young population whose [ O III ] 5007 \ emission contributes to the IRAC excess, and an older solution with a contribution from a Balmer break.

sources.summary: The paper selects GN-z10-3 as an unlensed z>9 candidate with an IRAC excess likely caused by a Balmer break, comparing it with simulations.

quotes.quote: In addition to considering JD1, we have selected two further unlensed sources, GN-z10-3 and GN-z9-1 seen in the CANDELS survey, with similar IRAC channel 2 excesses from the compilation of z 9 galaxies by . Although not yet spectroscopically confirmed, both have photometric redshift likelihoods consistent with a similar Balmer break at about the same epoch. Table photometry summarises the photometric properties of the three selected sources and Figure 1 provides image stamps that illustrate the HST and Spitzer data.

As can be seen, it seems reasonably likely that the IRAC channel 2 excess in GN-z10-3, and to a lesser extent, GN-z9-1 also arise from a Balmer break. In analysing the star formation histories and dust content of all 3 sources, we seek to go beyond understanding whether they share a common origin but also to examine whether their derived properties are consistent with numerical simulations of early galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper discusses GN-z10-3 as a compact bright candidate with a small low-redshift likelihood and SED-inferred stellar mass.

quotes.quote: GN-z10-3 has the highest low-redshift likelihood with only 0.2 .

The fainter two sources GN-z10-2 and GN-z10-3, while bright, are quite compact and are not detected with high enough S/N to rule out stellar contamination purely based on their surface brightness profile.
The SED fitting of the remaining two sources with clean IRAC photometry gives stellar masses of 10 ^ 9.2 0.3 for GN-z10-3 and 10 ^ 9.4 0.3 \ for GN-z9-1 with SED ages of 300 Myr.

gmw_00000334.name: MACS0416-JD
gmw_00000334.overview: MACS0416-JD is a gravitationally lensed, high-redshift galaxy located behind a Frontier Fields cluster [arXiv:2104.08168]. Observations reveal [O III] emission at a redshift of z = 9.28 [arXiv:2104.08168], though earlier photometric estimates suggested a redshift of approximately 8.45 [arXiv:1412.1472].

sources.summary: The paper treats MACS0416-JD as a lensed high-redshift galaxy, measuring ALMA [O III] emission at z=9.28, modest magnification, offset emission, line flux, and no dust continuum.

quotes.quote: Two sources in our sample, MACS0416-JD and MACS1149-JD1, are located behind Frontier Fields clusters and thus are gravitationally-lensed. We estimated the magnification for these using the online magnification calculator for the Frontier Field survey https://archive.stsci.edu/prepds/frontier/lensmodels/ magcalc which is based on lensing models developed by Hoag2016 , Caminha2017 , Jauzac2014 , Richard2014 , Kawamata2016 , Kawamata2018 , Ishigaki2015 , Johnson2014 and Grillo2015 . This yields a magnification of 1.90 ^ +0.03 _ 0.02 and 28 ^ +44 _ 11 respectively for MACS0416-JD and MACS1149-JD1.

We obtained 12.6hrs (8hrs on source) ALMA band 7 observation in Cycle 6 (ID: 2019.1.00061.S, PI: R. Ellis) to search for [ ] 88 m in MACS0416-JD. The spectral windows were setup to cover the 1- redshift probability distribution estimated from SED-fitting (Figure fig.sed , viz. [ 321.8 GHz : 325.7 GHz ] , [ 328.6 GHz : 335.6 GHz ] , and [ 340.6 GHz : 347.6 GHz ] . The data were obtained between October 2019 and January 2020 and reduced using the ALMA pipeline (v. 5.6.1-8) with a natural weighting scheme, a uv-tapper of 0.35 arcsec with a channel width of 16 MHz and a beam size of 1.00 0.76 arcsec in order to maximise the signal to noise ratio of any emission line ( Tamura2019 ). At the HST position of MACS0416-JD we identified a clear feature at =329.69 GHz corresponding to a redshift z =9.28 (Figure MACS0416-JD ). The offset between the centroid of the UV continuum and the peak of [ OIII ] 88 m emission is 0.40 arcsec, corresponding to an observed physical separation of 1.8 kpc at z 9.28. Such a small displacement is consistent with previous [ OIII ] 88 m observations at high- z (e.g. Carniani2017 , Harikane2020 ).

We determine f_ int =0.298 0.051 Jy.km.sec ^ 1 which is within the range values found previously in z 8 galaxies ( Tamura2019 , Hashimoto2018 ). Assuming a Gaussian profile, we measure a FWHM of 313 22 km sec ^ 1 . The significance of the line can be estimated via a histogram of the pixel by pixel signal to noise ratio (SNR) at the spatial position of the target. In the absence of emission, this will approximate a Gaussian distribution. If emission is present, a second peak will be seen indicating its significance. The lower panel of Figure MACS0416-JD reveals such a second peak at SNR 6 consistent with the analysis above. No dust continuum was detected to a 1- level of 18 Jy/beam.

sources.summary: The paper argues that HFF2C-9-2 is a bright high-redshift candidate: CLASH listed a low redshift, but both CLASH reanalysis and deeper HFF imaging support z ~ 8.45.

quotes.quote: This plot also shows that our object HFF2C-9-3 satisfies the colour-colour selection criterion adopted by Bouwens et al. (2014), but that our other three objects in the field (including HFF2C-9-2) do not. However, this is not surprising, because these 3 objects lie at z_ phot 8.5 - 8.6 , and the Bouwens et al. (2014) criterion is tuned to higher redshifts (the redshift window corresponding to the colour-colour criterion peaks at z 9.2 ). It is therefore clear why neither HFF2C-9-1 or HFF2C-9-2 would have been selected by Bouwens et al. (2014).

Object HFF2C-9-2 is listed in the CLASH catalogue as lying at z 1.3 , but even using the CLASH photometry our photometric-redshift code yields z 8.45 0.35 . The new deeper HFF photometry simply further strengthens the high-redshift solution (i.e. as with object HFF2C-9-1 the much deeper ACS imaging shows no evidence for any optical detection of object HFF2C-9-2).

gmw_00000335.name: GN-z9-1
gmw_00000335.overview: GN-z9-1 is an unlensed high-redshift galaxy candidate [arXiv:1809.07210] with a measured half-light radius of 0.17 arcseconds [arXiv:1309.2280]. It has a potential photometric redshift of approximately 9 [arXiv:1309.2280], supported by marginal spectroscopic evidence of a Lyman-alpha feature at a redshift of 9.89 [arXiv:2104.08168].

sources.summary: The paper reports marginal MOSFIRE evidence that GN-z9-1 lies beyond z=9, with a faint possible Lyman-alpha feature at z=9.89 requiring deeper confirmation.

quotes.quote: During the same observing run, a separate mask was designed for GN-z9-1. A total on source exposure time of 5.6hrs was secured in good seeing conditions. The data was reduced and analysed in the manner described above. However, in this case, no clear ( > 5 ) emission line over the entire J-band.

Reduced the significance threshold to 3 , a faint feature is present at =13241.2 \ with a positive signal at the expected position of the target in the slit and two negative counterparts on each side. We estimated a flux of (2.2 0.7) 10 ^ 18 cgs. Assuming this line is Lyman- , the redshift of GN-z9-1 would be z= 9.89 (Figure GN-z9-1 ). However, although consistent with the photometric redshift likelihood distribution (Figure fig.sed ), we consider this a marginal claim.

sources.summary: The paper treats GN-z9-1 as an unlensed z>9 candidate with a possible Balmer-break IRAC excess, comparing it with simulations alongside JD1.

quotes.quote: In addition to considering JD1, we have selected two further unlensed sources, GN-z10-3 and GN-z9-1 seen in the CANDELS survey, with similar IRAC channel 2 excesses from the compilation of z 9 galaxies by . Although not yet spectroscopically confirmed, both have photometric redshift likelihoods consistent with a similar Balmer break at about the same epoch. Table photometry summarises the photometric properties of the three selected sources and Figure 1 provides image stamps that illustrate the HST and Spitzer data.

As can be seen, it seems reasonably likely that the IRAC channel 2 excess in GN-z10-3, and to a lesser extent, GN-z9-1 also arise from a Balmer break. In analysing the star formation histories and dust content of all 3 sources, we seek to go beyond understanding whether they share a common origin but also to examine whether their derived properties are consistent with numerical simulations of early galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper describes GN-z9-1 as a z about 9 candidate with blank spectra, measured half-light radius, IRAC detection, and SED-derived stellar mass.

quotes.quote: For sources GN-z10-1 and GN-z9-1, the spectra are blank, showing no significant features.
For the brighter two sources GN-z10-1 and GN-z9-1, we measure half-light radii of 0 17 for both.
The SED fitting of the remaining two sources with clean IRAC photometry gives stellar masses of 10 ^ 9.2 0.3 for GN-z10-3 and 10 ^ 9.4 0.3 \ for GN-z9-1 with SED ages of 300 Myr.

gmw_00000336.name: UVISTA-1212
gmw_00000336.overview: UVISTA-1212 is one of the brightest Lyman-break galaxy candidates at z ~ 8 to 9 [arXiv:2104.08168, arXiv:1911.12832]. It is detected in the dust continuum [arXiv:2309.17386], showing a redder rest-frame ultraviolet color and an infrared excess deficit [arXiv:2309.17386].

sources.summary: The paper describes UVISTA-1212 as the sample's most luminous and massive galaxy and one of the brightest z~8 candidates, with FLAMINGOS-2 spectroscopy showing no clear J-band line.

quotes.quote: As a consequence of its detection to the shallower magnitude limit with a ground-based telescope, UVISTA-1212 is the most luminous and massive galaxy in the sample. Later in Section sec.prop we discuss how representative is our sample with respect to the larger population of sources thought to be at z 9. For all of the sources except UVISTA-1212, the most probable photometric redshift derived is above z 9 and thus a Balmer break may be the most likely explanation for the IRAC excess.

UVISTA-1212, one of the brightest z 8 candidates known to date, was observed with FLAMINGOS-2 on the Gemini-South telescope via the Fast Turnaround programme (ID: GS-2020A-FT-102, PI: Roberts-Borsani). Observations were accomplished in service mode in February 2020 in J-band using the R3K grism and a 4 pixel wide slit. From an allocation of 9.8 hours, a 8.8 hour on source exposure was taken in good seeing conditions. The data were reduced using the FLAMINGOS-2 Python cookbook for longslit observations (specifically the reduce ls script, which primarily uses the PyRaf module as a wrapper around IRAF functions to reduce the data). More specifically, the nsflat , nsreduce and nscombine were used to reduce the dark, flat and arc frames, as well as the standard star and science observations. The final 1D spectrum is shown in Figure f2_1dspec . After a careful inspection of the reduced spectra, no clear emission line was found over the entire J-band spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper reports that REBELS-37 is dust-continuum detected at z > 7.7, but has a redder UV color and an IRX deficit relative to Calzetti and SMC relations.

quotes.quote: Two of the REBELS sources at z > 7.7 are detected in the dust continuum (REBELS-4 and REBELS-37; also called XMM-355 and UVISTA-1212 respectively in  Bowler20 ), while the other five are not. REBELS-4 is in good agreement with our stacked results at slightly lower redshift, however REBELS-37 shows a redder rest-frame UV colour and deficit in IRX from both the Calzetti and SMC relations shown. We note that the slope value is more uncertain at these redshifts, due to the few bands ( H, K_s ) available for fitting, and the broader uncertainty in photometric redshift leading to a degeneracy between redshift and slope. Excluding REBELS-37, we find good agreement within the (large) errors with our z 7 stacks, although we note that the majority are upper limits on the dust continuum.

sources.summary: The paper identifies UVISTA-1212 as the brightest COSMOS candidate, with z = 9.12 and H = 24.4, and says its brown-dwarf fit is strongly excluded.

quotes.quote: The brightest object, UVISTA-1212, was found in the `deep' part of the UltraVISTA data (see Fig. fig:cosmos ) whereas the remaining candidates were found within the `ultra-deep' region.

UVISTA-1212 is the brightest object we find within COSMOS, and with a best-fitting photometric redshift of z = 9.12^ +0.20 _ 0.26 , it is one of the most luminous z 9 LBG candidates known with H = 24.4^ +0.3 _ 0.2 mag . The brown-dwarf fit can be strongly excluded based on the Spitzer /IRAC colour.

gmw_00000337.name: GS-z9-1
gmw_00000337.overview: GS-z9-1 is a high-redshift galaxy with an estimated photometric redshift of approximately 9.26 [arXiv:2104.08168]. Spectroscopic observations of the source did not detect any clear emission lines, including Lyman-alpha [arXiv:2104.08168].

sources.summary: The paper gives GS-z9-1 a photometric redshift near 9.26, reports 8 hours of X-Shooter exposure, finds no clear line, and keeps the photometric redshift assumption.

quotes.quote: GS-z9-1 has a photometric redshift of 9.26 ^ +0.41 _ 0.42 and we have obtained 9.8 hours of observing time (8.0 hours on source) from an allocation of 13.5 hours with X-Shooter/VLT (ID : 106.20ZH.001, PI: R. Ellis). Observations were undertaken in service mode in good seeing conditions ( < 1.1 arcsec) between October 2020 and January 2021 We thank ESO staff for observing during both Christmas and New Year. We carefully checked the centring of the source in the X-Shooter slit and applied the same data reduction procedure as described in section sec.macs0416 . Unfortunately no clear emission line is seen after a first inspection of the data (Figure fig:gs-z9-2_spec ). At the expected position of Ly- at z_ phot , we measure a 1 upper limit of 7 10 ^ 19 cgs.
Although we have marginal evidence that GN-z9-1 lies beyond z 9, for it, GS-z9-1 and UVISTA-1212, we will continue to assume the photometric redshifts given in Table list .

gmw_00000338.name: MACS1149-JD1
gmw_00000338.overview: MACS1149-JD1 is a gravitationally lensed galaxy [arXiv:1405.0927, arXiv:2104.08168] at a spectroscopic redshift of z = 9.11 [arXiv:1901.11045, arXiv:2002.02968]. It features a mature stellar population that began forming stars around z ~ 15 [arXiv:1806.06712, arXiv:2005.07248], alongside three compact, active star-forming clumps [arXiv:2308.13288].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes MACS1149-JD as a lensed z=9.6 galaxy, using a possible GISMO 2 mm counterpart to model dust emission, extinction, star formation, and high-redshift dust effects.

quotes.quote: A gravitationally magnified galaxy, designated MACS1149-JD was recently discovered at a photometric redshift of z =9.6 0.2 zheng12 . The massive lensing cluster (MACS J1149.6+2223), located at redshift 0.544, provides a magnification factor of =14.5 ^ +4.2 _ 1.0 . Assuming no extinction by dust, and correcting for magnification, the star formation rate (SFR), the total stellar mass, and luminosity derived from the observed UV-optical (UVO) flux are about 1.2 _ 15 ^ 1   , 1.5 10^ 8 _ 15 ^ 1   , and 2 10^ 10 _ 15 ^ 1   , respectively, where _ 15 is the lensing magnification normalized to the nominal value of 15 zheng12 . However, uncertainties in fitting the spectral energy distribution (SED) of this galaxy allow the possibility of a certain amount of extinction zheng12 .

The Goddard-IRAM Superconducting 2 Millimeter Observer (GISMO) camera at the IRAM 30m telescope on Pico Veleta staguhn13 , Spain was used to probe whether MACS1149-JD has a rest frame far-infrared counterpart. The observations detected a 2 mm source with an intensity of 400 98  Jy (see Figure  gismo ). The observed offset of 8'' (less than half the GISMO beam) is entirely consistent with this source being at the HST detected position of MACS1149-JD. The rest wavelength is 190   , close to the peak of the dust emission in star forming galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper presents HST grism spectroscopy of MACS1149-JD, finding a z~9.5 Lyman-break galaxy candidate with no strong Lyman-alpha emission and an IRAC color consistent with an evolved population.

quotes.quote: We present deep spectroscopic observations of a Lyman-break galaxy candidate (hereafter MACS1149-JD) at z 9.5 with the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) WFC3/IR grisms. The grism observations were taken at 4 distinct position angles, totaling 34 orbits with the G141 grism, although only 19 of the orbits are relatively uncontaminated along the trace of MACS1149-JD. We fit a 3-parameter ( z , F160W mag, and equivalent width) Lyman-break galaxy template to the three least contaminated grism position angles using an MCMC approach. The grism data alone are best fit with a redshift of (68 confidence), in good agreement with our photometric estimate of (68 confidence).

Our analysis rules out Lyman-alpha emission from MACS1149-JD above a 3 equivalent width of 21 , consistent with a highly neutral IGM. We explore a scenario where the red Spitzer /IRAC [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] color of the galaxy previously pointed out in the literature is due to strong rest-frame optical emission lines from a very young stellar population rather than a 4000 break. We find that while this can provide an explanation for the observed IRAC color, it requires a lower redshift ( z 9.1 ), which is less preferred by the imaging data. The grism data are consistent with both scenarios, indicating that the red IRAC color can still be explained by a 4000 break, characteristic of a relatively evolved stellar population.

sources.summary: The paper describes MACS1149-JD as a z=9.1 lensed galaxy whose red color is best explained by an old stellar population formed about 250 Myr after the Big Bang.

quotes.quote: The best case where the degeneracy has been lifted is a z=9.1 galaxy MACS1149-JD behind the cluster . MACS1149-JD was originally discovered in HST and shallow data in zheng12 . It was later detected in both, and , bands using deeper data (Fig. fig:1149 ; surfsup,huang16,zheng17 ) and its redshift was spectroscopically measured by hashimoto18 . For this galaxy the nebular emission lines are redshifted out of these bands, yet it has a strong color excess.

It is therefore highly likely the old ( 300 Myr ) stellar population is causing the red rest-frame optical color ( hashimoto18,hoag17,huang16 ). This is surprising, given the galaxies would need to start forming significant amounts of stars shortly after the Big Bang. At z=9.1 , when the Universe is only 550 Myr old, the presence of a strong Balmer break can thus provide the timing of the first star formation. In the case of MACS1149-JD the dominant stellar component formed about 250 million years after the Big Bang, corresponding to a redshift of about 15.

sources.summary: The paper includes MACS1149-JD1 as a strongly lensed high-redshift galaxy, revises its magnification, compares BAGPIPES fits, and finds its known z=9.11 redshift removes an IRAC-excess degeneracy.

quotes.quote: Two sources in our sample, MACS0416-JD and MACS1149-JD1, are located behind Frontier Fields clusters and thus are gravitationally-lensed. We estimated the magnification for these using the online magnification calculator for the Frontier Field survey https://archive.stsci.edu/prepds/frontier/lensmodels/ magcalc which is based on lensing models developed by Hoag2016 , Caminha2017 , Jauzac2014 , Richard2014 , Kawamata2016 , Kawamata2018 , Ishigaki2015 , Johnson2014 and Grillo2015 . This yields a magnification of 1.90 ^ +0.03 _ 0.02 and 28 ^ +44 _ 11 respectively for MACS0416-JD and MACS1149-JD1. The latter estimate represents a revision of the magnification adopted by Hashimoto2018 .

The BAGPIPES solution for MACS1149-JD1 can be compared with the solutions derived by and Roberts-Borsani2020 , both of which included ALMA [ ] 88 m fluxes and upper limits on the dust continuum in Band 7 as additional constraints. Although Roberts-Borsani et al advocated the use of ALMA to break the degeneracy between optical [ ] emission and a Balmer break as contributors to the IRAC excess, it is not a key issue in this case given the spectroscopic redshift of z =9.11 precludes the former case. Although an academic exercise given its redshift is known, both our photometric redshift ( z_ phot =9.44^ +0.02 _ 0.03 ) and age ( 484 ^ +17 _ 36 Myr) are consistent with the range of early estimates.

We emphasise that the inferred contribution of early star formation is uncertain in many cases, particularly for GN-z10-3 whose IRAC excess is the least prominent. Nonetheless, overall our sample is consistent with having significant star formation prior to z 10, as was concluded for MACS1149-JD1.

sources.summary: The paper presents JWST/MIRI observations of the z=9.1092 lensed galaxy MACS1149-JD1, resolving H emission into clumps with distinct star-formation histories, photon efficiencies, and kinematics.

quotes.quote: We present MIRI/JWST medium resolution spectroscopy (MRS) and imaging (MIRIM) of the lensed galaxy MACS1149-JD1 at a redshift of z = 9.1092 0.0002, when the Universe was about 530 Myr old. We detect, for the first time, spatially-resolved H emission in a galaxy at redshift above 9. The structure of the H emitting gas consists of two clumps, S and N, carrying about 60 and 40 of the total flux, respectively. The total H luminosity implies an instantaneous star formation rate in the range of 3.2 0.3 and 5.3 0.4 M_ yr ^ 1 for sub-solar and solar metallicities.

The extreme difference of EW _ 0 (H ) for Clumps S and N indicates the presence of a recent (few Myr old) burst in clump N and a star formation over a larger period of time (e.g. 100 200 Myr) in clump S, placing the initial formation of MACS1149-JD1 at a z 11-12. The different ages of the stellar population place MACS1149-JD1 and clumps N and S at different locations in the log( _ ion ) to EW _ 0 (H ) plane and above the main relation defined from intermediate and high redshift (z=3-7) galaxies detected with JWST. Finally, clump S and N show very different H kinematics with velocity dispersions of 56 4 km s ^ 1 and 113 33 km s ^ 1 , likely indicating the presence of outflows or increased turbulence in the clump N. The dynamical mass M_ dyn = (2.4 0.5) 10 ^ 9 M_ , obtained from the size of the integrated H ionized nebulae and its velocity dispersion, is within the range previously measured with the spatially-resolved [ OIII ] 88 m line.

sources.summary: The paper identifies MACS1149 JD1 as the strongest high-redshift LyC-leaker candidate in its IR diagnostic, while noting that revised [CII] measurements would lower the inferred leakage probability.

quotes.quote: In particular, MACS1149 JD1 from sits very close to the boundary. The CII _ 158 m emission line luminosity that is shown represents a 3 upper limit, indicating that it could be far less luminous. If this is confirmed to be the case, based on our modelling, MACS1149 JD1 represents a prime candidate for a galaxy that actively contributed to the reionization of the Universe.

We note that the actual luminosities for the CII _ 158 m and OIII _ 88 m emission lines of MACS1149 JD1 and A2744 YD4 are formally dependent on the amount of magnification which was assumed to be factors of 10 and 2 for each galaxy, respectively. Because the dividing line between being classified as a leaker and non-leaker in our model is not parallel to the one-to-one line, our conclusions about MACS1149 JD1 and A2744 YD4 will change if the actual magnification is much different from what is assumed. Furthermore, we note that a recent reanalysis of ALMA data for MACS1149 JD1 and A2744 YD4 has revealed [ CII ] detections that are more spatially extended compared to the [ OIII ] Carniani2020 . These results would place these galaxies much closer the the low-redshift [ CII ] SFR relation, consistent with the predictions from our simulations Katz2019b ; however, it would move the galaxies to the right on the [ CII ] [ OIII ] diagram, decreasing their probability of being LyC leakers.

sources.summary: The paper treats MACS1149-JD as a z=9.11 star-forming high-redshift galaxy, compares its expected NB1257/F125W colors, and finds its Ly luminosity below the survey limit.

quotes.quote: Recently, have detected an emission line of doubly ionized oxygen from the galaxy MACS1149-JD at z = 9.11 based on data from the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA). This star-forming galaxy was also studied with the X-shooter instrument, measuring a Ly flux of 4.3 1.1 10 ^ 18 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 .

By comparison with other studies, we included the expected location of three very high- z galaxies from the literature in our color-magnitude diagram (Fig. color_magnitude , triangles). The F125W and NB1257 magnitudes were computed using the published Ly fluxes and EWs. In particular, we derived the expected colors of the spectroscopically identified galaxies MACS1149-JD at z = 9.1096 2018Natur.557..392H,2018ApJ...854...39H,2020ARA&A..58..617O,2021MNRAS.505.3336L , EGSY8p7 at z = 8.683 2015ApJ...810L..12Z and EGS-zs8-1 at z = 7.7302 2015ApJ...804L..30O . We conclude that all these galaxies, even the two luminous galaxies in the EGS, are beyond the limiting magnitude of this work and therefore would be undetectable in our NB1257 image.
The estimated Ly luminosities at z = 9.34 of galaxies MACS1149-JD and EGSY8p7 were 4.7 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 and 1.9 10 ^ 43 erg s ^ 1 , respectively.

sources.summary: The paper resolves the z=9.1 lensed galaxy into three compact star-forming clumps plus an older extended component that holds most stellar mass and weakens the claimed Balmer break.

quotes.quote: We present measurements of stellar populations properties of a z=9.1 gravitationally lensed galaxy using deep JWST NIRISS slitless spectroscopy as well as NIRISS and NIRCam imaging from the CAnadian NIRISS Unbiased Cluster Survey (CANUCS). The galaxy is split into four components. Three magnified ( 17 ) star-forming components are unresolved, giving intrinsic sizes of <50 pc . In addition, the underlying extended component contains the bulk of the stellar mass, formed the majority of its stars 50 Myr earlier than the other three components and is not the site of most active star formation currently.

New observations with NIRISS and NIRCam reveal that the galaxy consists of three unresolved (with intrinsic sizes <50 pc ) star-forming clumps and an underlying extended galaxy component. We individually perform SED fitting of all four components (Fig. fig:SEDfit , Table tab:prop ). The galaxy component (G) is showing somewhat older stellar population, albeit with large errobars.

sources.summary: The paper describes MACS1149-JD1 as a strongly lensed z~9.6 young galaxy, rejects the nearby millimetre source association, and derives dust, infrared, SFR, and attenuation upper limits.

quotes.quote: MACS1149-JD1 is believed to be a gravitationally lensed galaxy, which is strongly magnified ( =14.5^ +4.2 _ 1.0 , 2012Natur.489..406Z ) by the Frontier Field (FF) galaxy cluster MACS J1149.6+2223 at z=0.544 ( 2007ApJ...661L..33E ). The Hubble Space Telescope (HST) and Spitzer colours indicate that MACS1149-JD1 is a young galaxy with a photometric redshift of z_ phot =9.6 0.2 ( 2012Natur.489..406Z ).

However, despite the achieved depth of _ =0.17 mJy in the AzTEC map, we have not found evidence of 1.1 mm continuum emission at the position of MACS1149-JD1. A 3.5 AzTEC detection at 11 arcsec from MACS1149-JD1, consistent with the GISMO position, is the most likely counterpart for the GISMO source. Combining the AzTEC and GISMO photometry with Herschel ancillary data we derive a z_ phot = 0.7-1.6 , which further indicates that this galaxy is not associated with MACS1149-JD1. Finally, from the non-detection of the z 9.6 galaxy MACS1149-JD1, we derive the following ( 3 ) upper limits corrected for gravitational lensing magnification and for CMB effects: dust mass <1.6 10^7 M_ , IR luminosity <8 10^ 10 L_ , SFR <14 M_ yr ^ 1 , and UV attenuation <2.7 mag.

sources.summary: The paper describes HFF4C-YJ1 as a bright z~9 candidate near HFF4C-YJ3, possibly a multiply imaged source, but notes the lens-model constraints make the system less secure.

quotes.quote: HFF4C-YJ1 and HFF4C-YJ3 -- HFF4C-YJ1 is a bright z 9 galaxy candidate in \ discovered by zheng12 . We find a faint z 9 galaxy candidate, HFF4C-YJ3, close to this galaxy (see Figures fig:colorimage and fig:multiple_candidates ). Our best-fitting mass model has a critical curve that is placed near these galaxies.

Therefore, it is possible that these two galaxies are in fact multiple images of a z 9 galaxy. The reliability of this multiple image system is not very high, because there are not many known multiple images around this system, and therefore our mass model in this region includes relatively large uncertainties. The \ colors of YJ1 and YJ3 are 0.24 0.04 and 0.16 0.23 , respectively, and are consistent with being multiple images.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M1149 JD1 as a z=9.11 ALMA-confirmed galaxy outside the z~8 inference sample, comparing tentative Ly-alpha claims with KMOS non-detection and limits.

quotes.quote: One target in our observations known as M1149 JD1, Zheng2012,Hoag2018 was recently spectroscopically confirmed at z=9.11 via [ ] 88 m emission with ALMA observations Hashimoto2018 . Our EAzY photometric redshift distribution for this galaxy put it outside of our inference redshift range (all of the p(z) is at z>9 , see Figure  fig:pz ), so it was not used in our reionization inference. However, Hashimoto2018 also report a tentative 4 detection of \ emission from this galaxy in X-shooter observations at 12271.5 \ with total line flux (4.3 1.1) 10^ 18 . Hoag2018 also targeted this galaxy with low resolution HST grism spectroscopy, including GLASS data, which covered the Lya wavelength at z=9.11 .

While they did not claim a detection, their spectra show a 2.5 feature at approximately the same wavelength and flux as Hashimoto2018 . We examined our KMOS cube and find no evidence of a feature at this wavelength.

sources.summary: The paper observes MACS1149 JD1 with ALMA band 5, reporting strong O III but no C II or continuum detection and comparing its C II deficit to simulations.

quotes.quote: Here we present new ALMA data for the two most distant, gravitationally-lensed and spectroscopically-confirmed galaxies, A2744 YD4 at z= 8.38 and MACS1149 JD1 at z= 9.11, both of which reveal intense [ O iii ] 88 m emission. In both cases we provide stringent upper limits on the presence of [ C ii ] 158 m with respect to [ O iii ] 88 m.

A similar setup was used for the MACS1149 JD1 observations, with a redshift range 8.96 z 9.16 and a total exposure time of 6.2hrs. Observations of A2744 YD4 were made with the C43-2 configuration yielding a beam size of 1.3'' 0.79''. For MACS1149 JD1, we used the configuration C43-4 to achieve a beam size of 0.75'' 0.63''.

Furthermore, only 8 simulated sources have [ C II ] 158 m properties similar to MACS1149 JD1 (i.e. SFR from 0.9 to 6.6 M_ yr ^ 1 ; L _ [ CII ] < 0.4 10 ^7 L _ ; (M _ [ M _ ] ) from 8.7 to 9.4) with mean properties : <M _ >=7.7 10 ^8 M _ , <L _ [ CII ] >=2.7 10 ^6 L _ and <Z _g >=0.25. Since our observational upper limits are 3 , this demonstrates the difficulty of reproducing our first glimpse at the weak [ C ii ] 158 m emission in z>8 sources.

sources.summary: The paper uses JD1 as the key spectroscopically confirmed z=9.11 galaxy, testing whether simulations reproduce its Balmer break, dust, and early star formation.

quotes.quote: Recent observations of the gravitationally lensed source, MACS 1149 JD1 (JD1), indicate that significant amounts of star formation likely occurred at redshifts as high as z 15 . The inferred stellar mass, dust mass, and assembly history of JD1, or any other galaxy at these redshifts that exhibits a strong Balmer break, can provide a strong test of our best theoretical models from high-resolution cosmological simulations. In this work, we present the results from a cosmological radiation-hydrodynamics simulation of the region surrounding a massive Lyman-break galaxy. For two of our most massive systems, we show that dust preferentially resides in the vicinity of the young stars thereby increasing the strength of the measured Balmer break such that the simulated SEDs are consistent with the photometry of JD1 and two other z>9 systems (GN-z10-3 and GN-z9-1) that have proposed Balmer breaks at high redshift.

provided an illustration of this method by analysing the SED, dust content, and line emission from a gravitationally-lensed source MACS 1149 JD1 (hereafter JD1) at z=9.11 . Their analysis indicated that a significant fraction of the stars in JD1 likely formed at a redshift z_ form 15.4 2.3 .

sources.summary: The paper treats MACS1149-JD1 as a z=9.11 Balmer-break template whose mature stellar population can mimic IRAC excesses otherwise attributed to nebular emission.

quotes.quote: Thus far only one system, MACS1149-JD1, hereafter JD1, has been spectroscopically-confirmed at z =9.11 hashimoto18 . Analysis of its SED attributes the IRAC excess to the Balmer break at 4000 \ consistent with a mature 200-300 Myr old stellar population providing a first tantalising glimpse of ``cosmic dawn'' at z 15 3.

Similarly, for the case of a mature stellar population with a prominent Balmer break, we select the SED fit to MACS1149-JD1 at z =9.11 from hashimoto18 . This fit represents a composite of a mature 200-300 Myrs population augmented with a younger component invoked to match the intensity of [ ] emission at 88 m discovered with ALMA; full details can be found in hashimoto18 .

The simulation shows that the Balmer break in JD1 can mimic the effect of intense line emission to within 0.1 mag in the [ 3.6 ] [ 4.5 ] colour, particularly at redshifts z 7.5, and even produce redder colours at z 8.5.

sources.summary: The paper discusses MACS1149-JD1 as a z=9.11 source whose reported offset line would require an implausibly large, highly ionized bubble, favoring a different lower-redshift source interpretation.

quotes.quote: Hashimoto2018a reported a 4 detection of a line in the z=9.11 source MACS1149-JD1. The line is offset by 450 60 from their detection of [ OIII ] 88 m. Based on Figure fig:lya_transmission_minDV , if the comes from the same source as the [ OIII ] , the environment of MACS1149-JD1 must be extremely highly ionised ( >10^ 6 ) and in a bubble 1 pMpc. Figure fig:R_opticallythin shows the size of the proximity zones produced by galaxies of a given . Given the observed faintness of MACS1149-JD1 = 18.5 0.1 based on lens modelling and fits to photometry and grism spectroscopy, Hoag2018a , it is impossible for it to produce such a large proximity zone, even with high , steep and low gas density. We thus agree with a possible interpretation by Hashimoto2018a that the emission comes from a different, slightly lower redshift, source compared to the [ OIII ] emission in MACS1149-JD1.

sources.summary: The paper finds MACS1149-JD1 chemically enriched to at least 0.1 solar metallicity, supporting extended previous star formation and a Balmer-break interpretation with roughly 100 Myr stellar ages.

quotes.quote: The strongest indications of extended star formation based on chemical evolution are for MACS1149-JD1 and B14-65666, where our method indicates relatively high metallicity. The lower bound on metallicity is 0.1 solar in both cases, considering both systematic and measurement uncertainties. Such enrichment suggests previous star formation lasting several tens of Myr or more as discussed above.

This result bolsters previous interpretation of MACS1149-JD1 where the IRAC excess cannot be explained by line emission, clearly favoring a Balmer break with stellar population age 100 Myr; Hashimoto2018 .

sources.summary: The paper describes MACS1149-JD as a z 9.5 galaxy whose Hubble Frontier Fields data confirm a strong Lyman break, though its color keeps it outside the authors’ final sample.

quotes.quote: The cluster M1149 contains the z 9.5 galaxy MACS1149-JD, first reported in Zheng12 based on CLASH data Postman12 . The deeper HFF data confirmed the strong Lyman break of the source Zheng17 and a weak continuum detection in an HST grism spectrum is consistent with the photometric redshift of z_ phot =9.5 0.1 Hoag17 . The source lies just barely outside of our color selection, however, with a measured color of J_ 125 H_ 160 =1.14 0.07<1.2 , which is why it is not included in our sample.

sources.summary: The review identifies MACS1149-JD1 as a z=9.11 lensed galaxy detected in [O III], whose line redshift and colors imply very early star formation.

quotes.quote: The highest redshift is MACS1149-JD1 at z=9.11, a lensed galaxy detected in the [ OIII ] line. No redshift was known from the optical before, and the [ OIII ] line was used to measure the redshift. The colors of its stellar population show that star formation began at z=15 in this galaxy Hashimoto2018 .

sources.summary: The paper applies the obscured-AGN near-zone scenario to lensed MACS1149-JD1, deriving the ionizing emissivity, UV magnitude, and X-ray luminosity needed for its blue-shifted line.

quotes.quote: The lensed galaxy MACS1149-JD1 observed at z 9.11 hashimoto2018 displays a blue-shifted Lyman- line relative to its [ OIII ] 88 m with a velocity offset v_ = -450 km/s. This corresponds to R_ = 0.37 pMpc, which was difficult to achieve mason2020 with the observed faintness of the galaxy ( M_ UV = -18.5 ). If the ionization zone of the above galaxy arises due to an AGN, we find that the required ionizing emissivity is N_ ion = 1.34 10^ 56 s ^ 1 , which corresponds to M_ 1500 = -24.99 , or an intrinsic X-ray luminosity of 10^ 44.73 ergs s ^ 1 in the 0.5 - 7 keV band.

gmw_00000339.name: PMN J 0948+0022
gmw_00000339.overview: PMN J0948+0022 is a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1102.3264, arXiv:2305.07443] that was the first of its class detected in gamma-rays [arXiv:1102.3264, arXiv:1406.1934]. It hosts a highly beamed relativistic jet [arXiv:1102.3264, arXiv:2305.07443] and exhibits rapid multiwavelength variability and strong flaring activity [arXiv:1303.3030, arXiv:1410.0755].

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J0948+0022 as the first gamma-ray RL-NLS1 galaxy, observed with global 22 GHz e-VLBI and showing compact radio structure, relativistic beaming, and polarization.

quotes.quote: We constrain the observational properties of the radio emission in the first RL-NLS1 galaxy ever detected in gamma-rays, PMN J0948+0022, i.e., its flux density and structure in total intensity and in polarization, its compactness, and variability. We performed three real-time e-VLBI observations of PMN J0948+0022 at 22 GHz, using a global array including telescopes in Europe, East Asia, and Australia. These are the first e-VLBI science observations ever carried out with a global array, reaching a maximum baseline length of 12 458 km. The observations were part of a large multiwavelength campaign in 2009. The source is detected at all three epochs.

The structure is dominated by a bright component, more compact than 55 as, with a fainter component at a position angle 35^ . Relativistic beaming is required by the observed brightness temperature of 3.4 10^ 11 K. Polarization is detected at a level of about 1 .

sources.summary: The paper treats PMN J0948 + 0022 as an archetypal gamma-ray NLS1, recognized for flat-spectrum radio emission, a small-angle relativistic jet, and line behavior affected by jet activity.

quotes.quote: There are a few data available on these NLS1, but one of them -- PMN J0948 + 0022 -- has been the target of two multiwavelength campaigns in 2009 (Abdo et al. 2009a) and 2010 (Foschini et al. 2011). Therefore, I will study this case as an archetypical example of this new class. PMN J0948 + 0022 ( z=0.585 ) was recognized as an anomalous NLS1 by Zhou et al. (2003), who discovered its characteristics of NLS1 together with strong radio emission with a flat spectrum, which in turn suggested the presence of a relativistic jet viewed at small angles (see also Yuan et al. 2008). This specific source was the first of this class to be detected at high-energy rays by means of Fermi /LAT (Abdo et al. 2009b, Foschini et al. 2010).

It is evident that the measurement of the FWHM (Zhou et al. 2003, Yuan et al. 2008) has been done during a period of low continuum flux, i.e. with low jet activity. The period of relatively low jet flux measured during the 2009 MW Campaign is slightly greater and, during the high flux, the values are greater by a factor 3 4. As from Sect. 2, the FWHM of the line becomes narrower when the jet continuum increases, but in this case the measurement has been done with the lowest contribution from the jet. Therefore, this is a really NLS1 and the jet activity can only make the line even narrower.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PMN J0948+0022 as the most gamma-ray-luminous Fermi RL-NLSy1, with large flares, substantial variability, and prominent spectral curvature.

quotes.quote: PMN J0948+0022 is the first RL-NLSy1 galaxy detected in the ray band by Fermi LAT. An average analysis of 5 years of the LAT data gives the ray flux and TS of 1.3 10 ^ 7 and 3300 respectively. From its weekly binned light curve, the peak flux value of (7.74 1.07) 10 ^ 7 is observed during a recent GeV flare 2013ATel.4694....1D .

Few significant flaring activities are observed and we consider two of them for further analysis. These flaring periods are noted as P2 and P3 in Figure lc . We find a value of F_ var of 0.443 0.029. This clearly hints at the existence of substantial flux variability in this source.
PMN J0948+0022 has the most prominent curvature in its ray spectrum ( 8 ) whereas 4 significant curvature is noticed in the spectrum of 1H 0323+342.

sources.summary: The paper monitors PMN J0948+0022 as a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1, finding rapid polarized-flux variability, high polarization, compact synchrotron emission, and jet-aligned PA.

quotes.quote: PMN J0948+0022 We performed optical photopolarimetric monitoring of the RL-NLSy1 galaxy PMN J0948+0022 on 2012 December to 2013 February triggered by the flux enhancement in near infrared and ray bands. Thanks to one-shot polarimetry of the HOWPol installed to the Kanata telescope, we have detected very rapid variability in the polarized-flux light curve on MJD 56281 (2012 December 20). The rise and decay times were about 140 sec and 180 sec, respectively. The PD reached 36 3 at the peak of the short-duration pulse, while PA remained almost constant. The high PD provides a clear evidence of synchrotron emission within a highly ordered magnetic field at the emission site.

These results provide new observational evidence that highly ordered magnetic field is present inside a very compact emission region of the order of 10^ 14 cm and imposes severe constraint on theoretical studies unless central black hole mass is much smaller than currently considered. we found that PA in MJD 56202 is aligned to the parsec scale jet. Temporal profiles of the total flux and PD showed highly variable but well correlated behavior and discrete correlation function analysis revealed that no significant time lag of more than 10 min was present.

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA and VLBA imaging of PMN J0948+0022, finding aligned pc-to-Mpc jet structures, a steep outer lobe, superluminal 100-pc-scale jet motion, and a pressure-supported conical streamline.

quotes.quote: The narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy PMN J0948+0022 is an archetype of gamma-ray-emitting NLS1s in active galactic nuclei (AGNs). In this study, we investigate its radio structures using archival data obtained using the Karl G. Jansky very large array (VLA) and the very long baseline array (VLBA). The new VLA images reveal an outermost radio emission separated by 9.1 arcsec. Its resolved structure and steep spectrum suggest a terminal shock in a radio lobe energized by the jet from the PMN J0948+0022 nucleus. This large-scale radio component is found at almost the same position angle as that of the pc-scale jet, indicating a stable jet direction up to 1 Mpc.

The VLBA image reveals the jet structure at distances ranging from =0.82 milliarcsec to 3.5 milliarcsec, corresponding to approximately 100 pc, where superluminal motions were found. The jet width profile ( ^ 1.12 ) and flux-density profile ( ^ 1.44 ) depending on the distance along the jet suggest that the jet kinetic energy is converted into internal energy in this region. The jet is causally connected in a nearly conical streamline, which is supported by ambient pressure at 100-pc scales in the host galaxy of PMN J0948+0022.

sources.summary: PMN J0948 + 0022 shows simultaneous X-ray, UV, and optical variability and a broken X-ray spectrum, interpreted as jet-dominated emission with a soft component.

quotes.quote: A strong correlation between X-ray, UV, and optical emission and simultaneous flux variations have been observed in 1H 0323 + 342, SBS 0846 + 513, PMN J0948 + 0022 as expected in case the jet radiation is the dominant mechanism. Correlated multiband variability favours the jet-dominated scenario also in FBQS J1644 + 2619 and PKS 2004--447. The summed X-ray Telescope spectra of 1H 0323 + 342, SBS 0846 + 513, PMN J0948 + 0022, and FBQS J1644 + 2619 are well fitted by a broken power law with a break around 2 keV.

Moreover, in order to compare the variability at different frequencies, we quantify the observed variability of 1H 0323 + 342, SBS 0846 + 513, PMN J0948 + 0022, PKS 1502 + 036, FBQS J1644 + 2619, and PKS 2004--447 in optical, UV, and X-rays bands through the variability amplitude, V_ amp , calculated as the ratio of maximum to minimum flux corrected for Galactic extinction. The variability amplitude may depend on the sampling of the light curves at the different frequencies. However, at least for a comparison between different bands, the simultaneity of observations in optical, UV, and X-rays guaranteed by the Swift observations helps mitigate this problem.

The variability amplitude in X-rays of the six sources varies between 2.7 and 8.5. These jetted NLSy1 are less variable than the other non-jetted NLSy1, suggesting that a different emission mechanism is driving the flux variations.

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J0948+0022 as a strongly radio-loud, gamma-ray NLS1 with beamed relativistic jet components, then models its intermediate and flaring states with disc-BLR external Compton emission.

quotes.quote: PMN J0948+0022, with a moderately high redshift (z = 0.5846, ), is classified as a typical radio loud NLSy1 galaxy with strong ( RL 10^ 3 ) and variable radio emission. Its inverted radio spectrum and very high brightness temperature ( 10^ 13 \ K ) derived from variation of the radio flux suggested the presence of a relativistic jet strongly beamed towards the observer ( ). This was later confirmed when PMN J0948+0022 turned out to be the first narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy detected in rays by the Fermi Large Area Telescope ( ). VLBI monitoring during the first ray flare ever detected in a NLS1, that occurred in July 2010, with shortest and even intraday variability time scales ( Foschini_2011 ) revealed polarization, change of the EVPA vector of about 90 and a variable radio spectral shape, probably related to the ray flare. The source is seen as a bright compact core, with large brightness temperatures ( ), indicating a heavy Doppler beaming. Since then its variable radio spectrum has been intensively monitored (see e.g and for a review). The existence of several superluminal components in the jet of this source points towards a small viewing angle and large Lorentz factor (e.g. ).

Again, the intermediate and the flaring states of PMN J0948 + 0022 were modelled in a disc BLR-IC scenario where the blob is below the BLR and in a torus-IC scenario where the emission region is at the outermost edge of the BLR. The results of the broad-band modelling of the two activity states for PMN J0948+0022 are shown in Fig. fig:PMN_Disc_BLR and fig:PMN_Torus in the BLR- and torus-dominated scenarios, respectively and the corresponding parameters are given in table pars_table_PMN . In the disc BLR dominated scenario, the BLR reprocesses 40 of the disc luminosity and the HE emission is explained by the IC scattering of the BLR photons by relativistic electrons of the blob. The X-ray corona has a fixed luminosity L _ X = 0.3 L_ d . The X-ray spectrum is mainly produced by the disc-IC component for both low and high states.

sources.summary: The paper reports Fermi gamma-ray detection of PMN J0948+0022, classifying it as a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 quasar with a relativistic jet and blazar-like SED.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery by the Large Area Telescope (LAT) onboard the Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope of high-energy gamma-ray emission from the peculiar quasar PMN J0948+0022 ( z=0.585 0.001 ). Contrary to the expectations, the optical spectrum of this quasar shows only narrow lines [ FWHM( H ) 1500 km/s ] and the typical characteristics of narrow-line Seyfert 1 type galaxies. However, the strong radio emission and the flat spectrum suggest the presence of a relativistic jet, which can now be confirmed by the detection of MeV-GeV photons.

PMN J0948+0022 is therefore the first radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 quasar to be detected at gamma-rays and the third type of ray emitting AGN, after blazars and radiogalaxies. The ray detection by Fermi confirmed this suggestion and allowed to build a complete SED: it was found that PMN J0948+0022 has the characteristics of flat-spectrum radio quasars (FSRQ), but with low power, relatively small mass ( 1.5 10^ 8 M_ ) and high accretion ( 40 the Eddington value).

It is immediately evident that PMN J0948+0022 is in the region of blazars, with the observed emitted power well above the radiogalaxies region. The study of the morphology of the source at radio frequencies shows a very compact source from 1.7 to 15.4 GHz (see Doi et al. 2006), except for the data at 1.4 GHz from the NVSS (Fig. fig:XRTFIRST ).

sources.summary: The paper presents global e-VLBI observations of the radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 PMN J0948+0022, emphasizing its gamma-ray jet, variability, compact radio emission, and polarization.

quotes.quote: A conclusive evidence for the existence of relativistic jets in these AGN has been obtained thanks to the Large Area Telescope (LAT) on board Fermi , which has revealed bright gamma-ray emission from the radio loud NLS1 PMN J0948+0022 just a few week after the launch, with flux at E>200 MeV is 4.0 0.3 10^ 8 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 and photon index = 2.6 0.1 discovery , making it one of the brightest gamma-ray AGNs at high latitude lbas ; using multi wavelength (MWL) data, it has been possible to estimate some basic parameters for this source, such as the size of the emitting region, the magnetic field value, and the particle energy range discovery,Foschini2010 . A more accurate view could only be attained through a dedicated study based on simultaneous broad band data and therefore a large campaign was organized between March and July 2009, covering gamma-rays, X-rays, UV, optical, and radio; indeed, although radio emission is generally produced somewhat out of the gamma-ray zone, single dish observations can still yield valuable complementary information on correlated flux and spectral variability; in addition, VLBI observations give independent constraints on the jet Doppler factor and viewing angle.

At all epochs, the source was clearly detected and imaged. The angular resolution was around 0.2 0.5 mas in the first and second epoch and the rms noise level about 1 mJy beam ^ 1 . The image shows a point-like source, although some structure seems to be present in the visibility amplitudes also in agreement with other published images of the sources, e.g. mwl,Foschini2011 .

The total flux density is varying between 300 and 700 mJy; although the exact magnitude of the variability can not be asserted due to a significant uncertainty on the absolute flux scale, it is clear that the source was variable from epoch to epoch. From the angular resolution and the flux density, we obtain a corresponding value for the brightness temperature of T_ B >3.1 10^ 10 K.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PMN J0948+0022 as a radio-loud narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy, finding a complex X-ray spectrum with soft excess and hard power-law emission.

quotes.quote: We analysed the archival data of radio loud narrow line Seyfert1 galaxy PMN J0948+0022 in the energy range 0.3--10.0 keV. The X-ray data revealed the spectrum in 0.3--10.0 keV energy band is not a simple power-law as described in literature. Instead it consisted of a power-law with soft excess below 2.5 keV. The X-ray spectrum was fitted with four different models and it was shown the soft excess component of the spectrum in 0.3--2.5 keV energy range could be described reasonably well within the framework of the thermal Comptonization model as well as relativistically blurred reflection model. The power-law component required to fit the spectrum beyond 2.5 keV was found to be rather hard compared to the ones observed in other obscured active galactic nuclei.

PMN J0948+0022 is a radio-loud narrow line Seyfert 1 (RLNLS1) galaxy, with cosmological redshift z = 0.5846 and radio loudness parameter R 846 fos11 , detected in rays by Large Area Telescope (LAT) on board satellite abdo+09a, abdo+09b . It is also one of the six such sources detected by /LAT in the MeV--GeV region http://www.asdc.asi.it/fermi2lac/ .

sources.summary: PMN J0948 + 0022 is one of five Fermi-detected radio-loud NLSy1 galaxies and showed significant GeV variability, flaring, and blazar-like SED properties.

quotes.quote: Up to now 5 radio-loud NLSy1 galaxies have been detected at high significance by Fermi LAT: PMN J0948 + 0022, PKS 1502 + 036, 1H 0323 + 342, PKS 2004 447, and SBS 0846 + 513. Here we analyze the first four years of ray observations of these sources.

A different level of activity has been clearly observed in these five objects, with significant variability in SBS 0846 + 513 and PMN J0948 + 0022. One of the key question is the maximum power released by the jets of radio-loud NLSy1, and for this reason ray flaring activities from these sources are catalyzing a growing interest in the astrophysical community. The first answers arrived in 2010 July when PMN J0948 + 0022 underwent a ray flaring activity with a daily peak flux of 1 10 ^ 6 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 foschini11 . The first spectral energy distributions (SEDs) collected for the 4 ray NLSy1s detected in the first year of Fermi operation showed clear similarities with blazars: a double-humped shape with disk component in UV, physical parameters blazar-like and jet power in the average range of blazars . The comparison of the SED of PMN J0948 + 0022 during the 2010 July flaring activity with that of 3C 273, a typical FSRQ, shows a more extreme Compton dominance in the NLSy1.

sources.summary: The paper reports minute-scale optical polarization variability in PMN J0948+0022, inferring synchrotron emission from a compact, ordered jet region in a radio-loud NLSy1 galaxy.

quotes.quote: We report on optical photopolarimetric results of the radio-loud narrow line Seyfert 1 (RL-NLSy1) galaxy PMN J0948+0022 on 2012 December to 2013 February triggered by flux enhancements in near infrared and ray bands. Thanks to one-shot polarimetry of the HOWPol installed to the Kanata telescope, we have detected very rapid variability in the polarized-flux light curve on MJD 56281 (2012 December 20). The rise and decay times were about 140 sec and 180 sec, respectively. The polarization degree (PD) reached 36 3 at the peak of the short-duration pulse, while polarization angle (PA) remained almost constant. In addition, temporal profiles of the total flux and PD showed highly variable but well correlated behavior and discrete correlation function analysis revealed that no significant time lag of more than 10 min was present.

The high PD and minute-scale variability in polarized flux provides a clear evidence of synchrotron radiation from a very compact emission region of 10^ 14 cm size with highly ordered magnetic field. Such micro variability of polarization are also observed in several blazar jets, but its complex relation between total flux and PD are explained by multi-zone model in several blazars. The implied single emission region in PMN J0948+0022 might be reflecting a difference of jets between RL-NLSy1s and blazars.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PMN J0948+0022 as the first Fermi-confirmed GeV radio-loud NLS1, with quasar-like radio power, a possible relativistic jet, and variable multiwavelength SEDs.

quotes.quote: PMN J0948+0022 ( z=0.5846 , Abdo et al. 2009b). It is the first confirmed GeV RL NLS1 galaxy by Fermi /LAT (Abdo et al. 2009b). Both the radio power and radio loudness of this source fall in the range of the classical radio quasars with P_ rad > 10^ 26 W Hz ^ 1 and R_ RL > 1000 (Zhou et al. 2003). Zhou et al. (2003) proposed that a relativistic jet may be hosted in the center of this object, which is similar to blazars. A multiwavelength campaign from March to July in 2009 indicates that a flux decrease from optical to ray bands was followed by an increase of radio emission (Abdo et al. 2009b), showing flux variation from radio to ray bands.

The broadband SEDs in different campaigns of this source were reported in Abdo et al. (2009b) and Foschini et al. (2012). We selected only those SEDs that have the confirmed Fermi /LAT detections from Abdo et al. (2009b) and Foschini et al. (2012). Eleven SEDs are selected for our analysis, as shown in Figure 1(). Note that the SED observed on 2009 Aril 15 is consistent with that observed on 2009 June 14 within the observational errors, indicating that the source is roughly in the same stage during the two observational campaigns, as shown in Figure SEDs (). Similarly, the SEDs observed on 2009 May 25 and 2009 June 4 are also consistent within the error bars. Therefore, we obtain nine SEDs in different states.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0948+0022 as the first gamma-ray detected radio-loud NLS1, notes intraday variability, and models its best-sampled infrared-to-ultraviolet low and high states.

quotes.quote: Specifically, they are: J0948+0022, the first RL-NLS1 to be detected in rays (Abdo et al. 2009a,b, Foschini et al. 2010).

Short timescale variability for factor-of-two flux changes is also reported by some authors. Specifically, Foschini (2011a) reported intraday variability for J0948+0022 and J1505+0326, while Palyia et al. (2014) found 3-hour variability of J0324+3410 during its outburst of 2013 August 28 to 2013 September 1 (see Online Material Table 9).

Just as an example, we focus on the infrared-to-ultraviolet band of J 0948+0022 , which is the best sampled source, being the first to be detected at rays. Fig. fig:zoom0948sed displays the two extreme states from the available data: the lowest activity state (LS, 2009 May 15, see also Abdo et al. 2009b, Foschini et al. 2012) and the highest state (HS, 2012 December 30). In both cases, we model the synchrotron emission (dotted grey line) with a power-law model with an exponential cutoff. The disk emission (dashed grey line) is the standard Shakura--Sunyaev model as expected from a black hole of M=7.5 10^7 M_ (see Table tab:masses ). Previous modelling (Abdo et al. 2009b, Foschini et al. 2012) supposed constant disk luminosity equal to L_ disk 0.4L_ Edd , as measured by fitting the optical/UV emission with a standard Shakura--Sunyaev disk.

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J0948 + 0022 as a Fermi-detected gamma-ray NLSy1 with strong flares, blazar-like variability, and XMM-Newton emission dominated by its jet.

quotes.quote: Five radio-loud NLSy1 galaxies have been detected at high significance by Fermi LAT so far: 1H 0323 + 342, SBS 0846 + 513, PMN J0948 + 0022, PKS 1502 + 036, and PKS 2004 447 , with a redshift between 0.061 and 0.585. The average apparent isotropic luminosity of these sources in the 0.1--100 GeV energy band is between 10 ^ 44 erg s ^ 1 and 10 ^ 47 erg s ^ 1 , a range of values typical of blazars . This may be an indication of a small viewing angle with respect to the jet axis and thus a high beaming factor for the ray emission, similarly to blazars.

Several strong ray flares were observed from SBS 0846 + 513 (Fig.  Fig1 ) and PMN J0948 + 0022, reaching a peak apparent isotropic ray luminosity of 10 ^ 48 erg s ^ 1 , comparable to that of the bright FSRQ . In particular, SBS 0846 + 513 and PMN J0948 + 0022 showed a ray flaring activity combined with a moderate spectral evolution , a behaviour that was already observed in bright flat spectrum radio quasars (FSRQ) and low-synchrotron-peaked BL Lacs . Variability and spectral properties of these two NLSy1s in rays indicate a blazar-like behaviour.

The spectral modelling of the XMM-Newton data of PMN J0948 + 0022 shows that emission from the jet most likely dominates the spectrum above 2 keV, while a soft X-ray excess is evident below 2 keV (Fig.  Fig2 ).

sources.summary: The paper models PMN J0948+0022 as an NLSy1 SED case whose weak extreme-UV/soft-X-ray photon field changes C III, O III, He II, and dust-suppression behavior.

quotes.quote: To represent the SED of a NLSy1 we use the galaxy PMN J0948+0022 ammando2015 , shown as the magenta dashed line. The NLSy1 galaxy PMN J0948+0022 SED has no pronounced emission around 0.1 keV, rather it has an excess of harder photons.

Interestingly, this result is consistent with the SDSS spectrum of PMN J0948+0022, where [ O III ] emission is not present see their Fig. 2, lower panel tanaka2014 . Finally, our model shows that He II emission in this object is not significantly affected by the presence of dust. Rather, it is fairly constant close to 0.1 pc and decreases slowly as a function of radial distance.

The differences in line emission of C III ] , [ O III ] and He II from the other SEDs considered in our study are connected with the fact that the PMN J0948+0022 SED has fewer photons in the extreme UV and soft X-ray band.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes PMN J0948+0022 as a GeV narrow-line Seyfert 1 with FSRQ-like properties, strong multiwavelength variability, optical and gamma-ray QPOs, and no corresponding 15 GHz radio QPO.

quotes.quote: We dealt with and analyzed the 8-year observation data of Fermi /LAT and all the observation data of Swift /XRT for the first confirmed GeV-NLS1 PMN J0948+0022, and also collected its long-term observation data in optical V-band and radio 15 GHz from the literature. It was found that this source shows significant flux variations in multi-wavelength. It demonstrates the similar characteristics to the typical FSRQs, but does not show a dependence of the spectral index variation to the flux variation in the GeV band. A QPO of 490 days at a 99 confidence level in the ray lightcurve is found for PMN J0948+0022.

Similar QPO signature is also observed in the optical V-band, i.e., 440 days at a 99 confidence level. The observed correlation between ray and optical fluxes further strengthens the QPO behavior in PMN J0948+0022. No similar QPO signature is observed in the 15 GHz lightcurve, and no statistical correlation of variability between ray and 15 GHz is found.

sources.summary: The paper finds PMN J0948+0022 has strongly curved gamma-ray emission and alternating flaring states, including an 18.9-hour peak with FSRQ-like apparent luminosity.

quotes.quote: PMN J0948+0022: Its spectrum measured in the narrow 0.1-10 GeV range appeared with a significant curvature Fig. sed_all (h). The comparison of LP and PL models yields a log likelihood ratio of 95.2 , so the PL modeling can be rejected with a significance of 9.76 . The LP model compared with the observed spectrum is shown in Fig. sed_all (h) with blue color (for parameters see Table tab_SD ).

The light curve with 15-day (blue) and adaptive bins (red, 20 ) shows that the source is alternatingly in its flaring or quiescent states (Fig. var_all [ e ] ). Several times, the source flux was above 10^ 6 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 and during an extremely active state from MJD 56290.96 to MJD 56294.60, it reached a peak flux of (1.51 0.31) 10^ 6 photon cm^ 2 s^ 1 within 18.9 hrs, corresponding to an apparent isotropic luminosity of L_ 1.09 10^ 48 erg s^ 1 , which is typical for the brightest FSRQs. For most of the time the photon index is >2.5 , the hardest one being =2.13 0.18 . The observed rapid flux changes in intra-day scales and the amplitude of the increase once more show that in the band NLSY1s behave similar to blazars.

sources.summary: The paper selects PMN J0948 + 0022 as a promising NLSy1 target, describing its early Fermi-LAT detection, two later outbursts, failed VERITAS follow-up, and high-energy modeling.

quotes.quote: A comprehensive sample of narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxies has been discussed by , who collected from literature twenty sources. Among them, three sources seem to be the most promising ones to be investigated at energies above a few tens of GeV, namely SBS 0846 + 513, PMN J0948 + 0022, and PKS 1502 + 036. We briefly report here their discovery literature, while for a detailed description of their high-energy characteristics we refer to and references therein.

PMN J0948 + 0022 ( z=0.585 ) is the first NLSy1 detected in the energy band already during the first months of operations of Fermi-LAT in 2008 . Two intense outbursts were then detected in 2010 July and at the end of 2012 . During the latest outburst, there was also an attempt to detect the source at very high-energy (VHE) with VERITAS at energies E>200 GeV, but without success . We note that VERITAS observations began almost a week after the maximum, during the decaying phase of the flare.

sources.summary: J0948+0022 is described as a previously known very radio-loud NLS1 with an inverted unresolved radio source, evidence for relativistic beaming, and optical variability.

quotes.quote: J0948+0022 : The object was first discovered for its radio emission in various radio surveys ( S_ 5GHz 0.2--0.3 Jy). It was identified with a NLS1 galaxy, based on its SDSS spectrum, by wilm02 and zhou03 , and was studied in detail as one of the few previously known very RL NLS1 galaxies. The radio source has an inverted radio spectrum and was unresolved with VLBI at milli-arcsec resolution.

These observations were explained in terms of relativistic beaming doi06 . A very high brightness temperature ( 10^ 13 K) inferred from radio flux variations suggest the presence of a jet with relativistic beaming zhou03 . Long-term optical variability with an amplitude of about 1 mag in the B band was also noted in zhou03 .

sources.summary: The paper describes PMN J 0948+0022 as a gamma-ray narrow-line Seyfert 1 with compact variable radio emission, a relativistic jet, high luminosity, and a strong 2010 outburst.

quotes.quote: Here we report preliminary results about the observations of the July 2010 ray outburst of PMN J 0948+0022 , when the source flux exceeded for the first time 10^ 6 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 ( E>100 MeV).

One of these sources, PMN J 0948+0022 ( z=0.5846 ) is classified a typical NLS1, but it displays also strong, compact and variable radio emission, with inverted spectrum, suggesting the possibility of the presence of a relativistic jet (Zhou et al. 2003). The confirmation came with the detection of high-energy variable rays by Fermi /LAT (Abdo et al. 2009b, Foschini et al. 2010a). A multiwavelength campaign performed in March-July 2009 displayed coordinated variability at all frequencies, thus confirming that the source detected by Fermi is indeed the high-energy counterpart of PMN J 0948+0022 (Abdo et al. 2009c). PMN J 0948+0022 , the first NLS1 detected at rays, was soon followed by three more (Abdo et al. 2009a).

The question was answered in July 2010, when PMN J 0948+0022 underwent a strong outburst (Donato et al. 2010, Foschini 2010c) with a peak flux of 10^ 6 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 ( 0.1-100 GeV), corresponding to a luminosity of 10^ 48 erg s ^ 1 (Fig. fig1 ).

sources.summary: The paper finds PMN J0948 + 0022 has the richest monitored dataset, a mostly inverted and highly dynamic spectrum, and at least four prominent lagged radio events.

quotes.quote: PMN J0948 + 0022: It is evident from the light curves and the spectra shown in Figure fig:LC-nlsy that the available dataset for PMN J0948 + 0022 is much richer than that for the other two NLSy1s. The spectrum appears mostly inverted representative of variability type 1. The spectral index below 10 GHz ranges between marginally steep or flat ( 0.1 ) to highly inverted reaching values of +1.0 .

Its evolution is exceptionally dynamic with significant evolution happening even within one month. It appears that for this source a sampling of two weeks would be necessary. Its light curve shows at least 4 prominent events which emerge with time lags at different bands.
