We find that the stochasticity caused by mass assembly histories is enhanced in systems with virial masses 10^ 10 M_ because of their sensitivity to the photoionizing intergalactic radiation field after the epoch of reionization. This results in qualitatively different star formation histories in late- and early-forming galaxies and it might explain the peculiar star formation histories of irregular dwarf galaxies such as Leo A and Aquarius.

The agreement between observations of Leo A and the model is very good: in all time bins except for the earliest one, the two agree to within 30 , and the difference in the earliest bin is only a factor 5.

sources.summary: The paper uses Leo A as one of six isolated Local Group dwarfs with extended stellar-halo observations, fitting ghostly-halo models to infer pre-reionization star-formation efficiency.

quotes.quote: We compare the model to available observations of stellar haloes in six isolated dwarf galaxies in the Local Group (Leo T, Leo A, IC 10, WLM, IC 1613, NGC 6822) to infer the star formation efficiency in dwarf galaxies at the epoch of reionization. We find an efficiency of star formation in dark matter haloes with masses 10^6-10^8 M _ at z 7 in rough agreement with independent methods using data on the luminosity function of ultra-faint dwarf galaxies but systematically higher by a factor of 3-5. The systematic uncertainty of our results is still large, mainly because available observations of stellar halo profiles do not extend over a sufficiently large distance from the center of the host dwarf galaxy. Additional observations, easily within reach of current telescopes, can significantly improve the accuracy of this method and can also be used to constrain the present day dark matter masses of dwarf galaxies in the Local Group.

KangR:2019 have compiled a list of six dwarf galaxies (Leo T, Leo A, WLM, IC 1613, IC 10, and NGC 6822) located in the Local Group, but outside the virial radii of the Milky Way and Andromeda, showing robust or tentative evidence of the existence of an extended stellar halo. In the collected observational data sets, radial density profiles of stars are provided out to several half-light radii from the central galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper describes Leo A as having an unusual star-formation history dominated by intermediate-age stars, sparse old horizontal-branch populations, and insufficient timing data for this analysis.

quotes.quote: LEO A - This galaxy has the most unusual SFH in our sample. cole07 found very low level of star formation starting 13 Gyr ago, in agreement with the small number of RR Lyrae stars detected (8: dolphin02 , and 10: bernard09b ). cole07 further found that 90 of all star formation occurred in the last 8 Gyr, resulting in the only galaxy in the LCID sample with a dominant intermediate-age population. As a result, the HB inside and to the blue of the instability strip is very scarcely populated by old stars. Moreover, the time sampling in our ACS observations was poor and not adequate to accurately estimate the periods, and therefore the mean magnitudes, of the RR Lyrae stars.

sources.summary: Leo A is described as a low-metallicity Local Group dwarf whose C-stars need confirmation and whose recent star formation contrasts with a scarcity of AGB stars.

quotes.quote: The C-stars in Leo A still need to be carefully studied. These would likely be the most metal poor C-stars in the Local Group, if confirmed, given that the present day HII region abundance is a mere 3 of solar Vanzee06 . The stars in the C-star region of the colour-colour diagram for Leo A look more untidy than the usual AGB sequence, and my well be the result of confusion or young (massive) stars in HII region.

But Leo A is a galaxy with a similar luminosity to Sculptor, and a current metallicity (from H II region spectroscopy) which is similar to the average metallicity found in Sculptor. Leo A also formed most of its stars over the last 5 Gyrs and yet there are very few, if any, AGB in Leo A (see Fig. sfh ).

sources.summary: The paper treats Leo A as one of seven isolated Local Group dwarf galaxies, measuring red-giant velocities and dispersions and comparing isolated dwarfs with Milky Way and M31 satellites.

quotes.quote: We measured velocities of \ individual red giant stars in seven isolated dwarf galaxies in the Local Group: NGC 6822, IC 1613, VV 124 (UGC 4879), the Pegasus dwarf irregular galaxy (DDO 216), Leo A, Cetus, and Aquarius (DDO 210). We also computed velocity dispersions, taking into account the measurement uncertainties on individual stars. None of the isolated galaxies is denser than the densest Local Group satellite galaxy. Furthermore, the isolated dwarf galaxies have no obvious distinction in the velocity dispersion--half-light radius plane from the satellite galaxies of the Milky Way and M31. The similarity of the isolated and satellite galaxies' dynamics and structural parameters imposes limitations on environmental solutions to the too-big-to-fail problem, wherein there are fewer dense dwarf satellite galaxies than would be expected from cold dark matter simulations.

sources.summary: The paper’s model prefers substantial Leo A rotation but only weakly over a non-rotating model, with a possible stellar rotation nearly perpendicular to the H I disk.

quotes.quote: Leo A: Although our model prefers a fair amount of rotation in this galaxy ( =1.99_ 1.09 ^ +0.99 ), our analysis yields only weak evidence for rotation in Leo A compared to a non-rotating model ( B_ rot = 1.50 ). There is no rotation seen in H I gas Young1996 . Our potential rotation at 33 is almost perpendicular to the H I disc at =102 . A larger kinematic sample size will be required to make a stronger statement about the rotation.

sources.summary: The paper applies a 2D star-formation-history method to Leo A, finding inside-out star formation over 400 Myr, asymmetric spreading, and present-day alignment with H i structure.

quotes.quote: Based on multicolor stellar photometry data we have found that in the Leo A galaxy during the last 400 Myr star formation was propagating according to the inside-out scenario. Star-forming regions have spread strongly asymmetrically from the center and their present day distribution correlates well with the H i surface density maps.

Our results show that during the last 400 Myr star formation in this galaxy followed an inside-out scenario. At the beginning ( 400 Myr ago), a clear star-forming region is visible residing at the center of Leo A. Over the next 200 Myr it evolved into an asymmetrical shape, which correlates well with the current H i surface density distribution.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 69 as a Local Group Milky Way satellite whose H I disk is dominated by a Type 1 hole plus smaller holes.

quotes.quote: DDO 69 DDO 69 (PGC 28868, UGC 5364, Leo A) is a Local Group galaxy and Milky Way satellite. It is 0.8 Mpc away with V-band magnitude 11.7 . The galactic disk is dominated by a Type 1 hole (No. 4), and the rest of the holes are quite small in size. The \ disk of the galaxy extends to 1 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper studies DDO 69 as a nearby small dwarf irregular with modest star formation, faint far-infrared emission, and an extremely low dust-to-gas ratio for its metallicity.

quotes.quote: Four dwarf irregular galaxies with properties typical of normal dwarfs were selected for observations with \ for this study: DDO 69, DDO 70, DDO 75, and DDO 210. The photometry sample is slightly different from the spectroscopy sample of - DDO 69, DDO 70, and DDO 75 are common between the two, but DDO 210 has no \ emission line data. They are all nearby (less than 1.3 Mpc) and small (R _D between 0.17 and 0.48 kpc, M_ HI of 7.2 10^ 7 M_ or less), with modest star formation rates of 0.012 M_ yr ^ 1 or less.
DDO 69 is much fainter overall, and the PACS flux for the full galaxy is 1.4 times higher than that determined from the notably noisy MIPS image.

DDO 69 is a particularly interesting case, in that is has an extremely low DGR for its metallicity -- similar to previous results for I Zw 18 and SBS 0335-052 (as shown by RemyRuyer2014 and further discussed by Schneider2016 ) and a few sources in the H I rich sample from DeVis2017a,DeVis2017b , suggesting some dwarf galaxies are extremely dust poor at a given Z and gas mass compared to the bulk of dwarfs studied in the literature.

sources.summary: The paper presents Cepheid monitoring of Leo A, derives a distance modulus, and compares it with RR Lyrae, TRGB, red-clump, and red-horizontal-branch distance estimates.

quotes.quote: Leo A For Leo A we find a distance modulus of m-M=24.47 0.10 0.06_ ZP (Fig. o25_leo_plr ) which is consistent with the findings of 2002AJ....123.3154D , 1998AJ....116.1244T , and 2002AJ....124..896S . 2002AJ....123.3154D searched for RR Lyr stars in Leo A and derived m-M=24.51 0.12 . 1998AJ....116.1244T used a combination of ground-based and HST data to derive a tip-of-the-red-giant-branch (TRGB) distance of m-M=24.5 0.2 . They also used red clump stars yielding a discrepant m-M=24.2 0.2 . 2002AJ....124..896S 's even deeper HST data allowed the detection of red horizontal branch stars and concluded that they and the TRGB are in better agreement with m-M=24.5 0.2 .

sources.summary: The paper summarizes Leo A as a delayed-star-formation dwarf with possible reionization-survivor status, young clusters, low-metallicity HII regions, and use in star-formation-history tests.

quotes.quote: Leo A Leo A (DDO 69) shows delayed star formation, like DDO 210, and only minimal star formation happened before 10 Gyrs ago. As such, it is sometimes argued as being a possible survivor of reionization Cole2007,Cole2014 . presented detailed studies of Leo A based on Subaru/SuprimeCam data, in addition to using HST ACS fields at large radius. reported the discovery of a young star cluster in Leo A. studied the HII regions in Leo A, confirming that its interstellar medium has one of the lowest metallicities of nearby galaxies ( vanzee2006 find 12 + log(O/H) = 7.30 0.05 ). This low metallicity makes it a useful system for calibration (e.g. for Cepheid observations Bernard2013 ). Leo A was also used by to test whether there are differences between star formation histories derived from colour magnitude diagrams compared to integrated light studies.

sources.summary: The paper centers on Leo A as a very metal-poor dwarf irregular, modeling its anomalous and classical Cepheids and finding its variables agree with low-metallicity pulsation predictions.

quotes.quote: We investigate the theoretical scenario concerning the large sample of variables recently discovered in the dwarf, metal-poor irregular galaxy Leo A, focusing the attention on the "Anomalous" Cepheid phenomenon and its correlation with RR Lyrae stars, Classical and Population II Cepheids.

Recently, Dolphin et al. (2002, hereafter D02) reported the results of a search for short-period ( P 2 days) variables in Leo A, a Local Group dwarf irregular galaxy characterized by a very low metal abundance ( Z 0.0004).

The comparison with selected evolutionary tracks shows that Cepheids in Leo A should have masses from 1.9 to 3 M_ , where the lower limit is the theoretical prediction for the occurrence of pulsators, whereas the upper limit is only indicating the lack of more massive evolving stars. Thus the Leo A variables beautifully confirm that the distribution in the M_V log P diagram of massive pulsators which experienced the He flash is the natural extension of the distribution of more massive pulsators characterized by a quiet He ignition, at least for masses M 4.0 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper uses Leo A to illustrate how extended H I can bias inferred gas fractions, noting that reducing the true gas mass makes it resemble other galaxies.

quotes.quote: An example is Leo A where Young Lo (1996) find that emission from the inner galaxy is about 20 percent of the whole emission. We show its position in all figures for the entire mass, but with an arrow pointing towards the loci if we took an mass five times smaller. Fig. f:mhimrat shows that this galaxy also has a very low ratio of dynamical to mass, significantly smaller than most other galaxies. However, if one reduces the true galactic gas mass, this object becomes more similar to the others.

sources.summary: The review highlights Leo A as a dwarf irregular with a low early star formation rate, most activity in the last 8 Gyr, and a main peak a few Gyr ago.

quotes.quote: The first of such impressive studies is that of Leo A ( ), whose CMD and SFH are plotted in Fig. leoa . In Leo A the star formation activity was present, although quite low, at the earliest epochs, and 90 of the activity occurred in the last 8 Gyr, with the main peak around 2 Gyr ago and a secondary peak a few hundreds Myr ago.
Leo A, with the main SF peak a few Gyr ago (Fig. leoa ) is quite typical.

sources.summary: The paper identifies Leo A as a distant dwarf irregular with full star-formation history, groups it among field isolated slow dwarfs, and notes it still forms stars and retains gas.

quotes.quote: Two dIrr, IC1613 and Leo A Skillman2014, Cole2007 ; two dT, LGS3 and Phoenix Hidalgo11sfhlgs3, Hidalgo09sfhphoenix ; and two isolated dSph in the Local Group, Cetus and Tucana Monelli10sfhcetus, Monelli10sfhtucana were studied.

The distant dIrr galaxies with known full SFHs, IC1613, Leo A and DDO210, are all located over 400 kpc away from the MW or M31, and have negative (or small for DDO210) Galactocentric radial velocities, M31 and the Local Group center of mass (Figure global ).

In fact, tidal effects could play a role in the final removal of gas at late times in some slow MW satellites, such as Leo I or Fornax, which stopped their star formation only recently, as opposed to field isolated slow dwarfs, like Leo A or IC1613, that are still forming stars and retain sizable amounts of gas.

sources.summary: The review discusses Leo A in mass-metallicity relations, saying its planetary-nebula oxygen abundances deviate only at very low mass but align with extrapolated SDSS relations.

quotes.quote: The MZR with oxygen PN abundances seems to deviate from the stellar one only for masses as smaller as that of Leo A ( 10 ^7 M _ ). However, if the comparison with the mass-metallicity relation for the SF galaxies in the SDSS, and its extrapolation, are considered, Leo A is not anymore an outlier of the MZRs. Moreover, it is worth noticing that the dIrr galaxy Leo A is perfectly consistent with the extrapolation of the asymptotic logarithmic fit of the SDSS mass-metallicity relation (the dashed line that reaches stellar masses as smaller as log(M /M _ ) 6.6) shown in Figure fPNeMZR bottom panel.

gmw_00000348.name: M86
gmw_00000348.overview: M86 is a giant elliptical galaxy in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:0806.0866, arXiv:0904.0526] that is falling toward the cluster center [arXiv:1007.1580, arXiv:2306.04513]. It exhibits a long, ram-pressure-stripped X-ray tail [arXiv:0806.0866, arXiv:2404.02035] and shows signs of tidal interactions, including a stellar bridge and streams [arXiv:1611.04435, arXiv:2103.05006].

sources.summary: The paper presents Chandra observations of M86, finding an extended halo, cool stripped gas, and a very long ram-pressure tail that constrains its Virgo orbit.

quotes.quote: We present results from a mosaic of nine Chandra observations of M86 and the surrounding field. We detect three main diffuse components: the Virgo ICM at 2.4 keV, the extended halo of M86 at 1.2 keV, and the cooler central and stripped gas of M86 at 0.8 keV. The most striking feature is a long tail of emission, which consists of a plume 4 north of M86 and two main extensions emanating from the plume. Based on the morphology and temperature structure of the tail, we conclude that it is formed by ram pressure stripping of M86 as it falls into the Virgo cluster and interacts with the Virgo ICM, in agreement with earlier work. The tail is 150 kpc in projection, and a simple estimate gives a lower limit on the true length of the tail of 380 kpc, making this the longest ram pressure stripped tail presently known.

M86 (NGC 4406) is a bright elliptical (E3/S0) galaxy in the Virgo cluster of galaxies. It is the dominant member of one of the larger subgroups within Virgo (Binggeli \ 1993; B ohringer \ 1994; Schindler \ 1999). Its line-of-sight velocity relative to M87, the dominant member of the Virgo cluster, is -1550 km s ^ 1 , much higher than the average cluster velocity dispersion (Smith et al. \ 2000).

sources.summary: The paper uses X-ray absorption near M86 to place the stripped gas in front of M86, but concludes the M86 halo alone cannot explain NGC 4388 stripping.

quotes.quote: We report the detection of an X-ray absorption feature near the galaxy M86 in the Virgo cluster. The absorber has a column density of 2-3 10^ 20 cm^ 2 , and its position coincides with the peak of an intracluster \ cloud which was removed from the galaxy NGC 4388 presumably by ram pressure. These results indicate that the \ cloud is located in front of M86 along the line-of-sight, and suggest that the stripping was primarily created by an interaction between NGC 4388 and the hot plasmas of the Virgo cluster, not the M86 halo. By calculating an X-ray temperature map, we further detected an X-ray counterpart of the \ cloud up to 3^ south of M86.

In contrast, OG05 and 2009A&A...502..427V proposed an alternative view: N4388 is interacting, at a speed > 2800 km s ^ 1 , with the hot gas halo of the M86 group which is assumed to be located outside the Virgo cluster. Since M86 has a projected distance of only 10 kpc from the plume, they can actually be very close in three dimensions. Due to large systematic uncertainties on the line-of-sight distance of N4388 (e.g., 13.6 Mpc from 1997A&A...323...14S ; 19.2 Mpc from 1997ApJS..109..333W ; and 38.7 Mpc from 2007A&A...465...71T ), the origin of the stripping in N4388 remain unclear. The cold \ plume, which has a column density sufficient to significantly absorb background X-ray photons, is found covering an X-ray bright, 15 \ 5 \ field in the central 20 \ region of the M86 group (OG05). This makes the X-ray absorption measurement a promising way to constrain the line-of-sight geometry between N4388 and M86.

sources.summary: The paper places a peculiar X-ray transient near M 86, describes stripped gas and stars around the elliptical, and argues that an ongoing wet minor merger could associate the event with M 86.

quotes.quote: The source position is 12.16 arcminutes from the centre of M 86, and it does not fall in the M 86 _B=25 magnitude per arcsec ^2 isophote area ( 1991rc3..book.....D ) that has an approximate radius of 8.5 arcminutes in the direction of the location. However, it was found that M 86 is falling into the Virgo cluster and that gas is stripped off the galaxy ( 2008ApJ...688..208R ). The projected stripped gas lies close to the position of . Furthermore, the deep optical INT images we investigated show that stars, possibly stripped off the galaxy SDSS J122541.29+130251.2, follow the stripped gas, which suggests that M 86 shows signs of a recent minor merger (see Figure stream ).

The small projected distance on the sky between the position of and that of the stream of stripped stars strengthens the association between and M 86. The stream of stars falls between the wedge-shaped hot gas structure visible in the X-ray images of the field ( 2008ApJ...688..208R ), suggesting that some or all of the gas may come from the infalling galaxy, making this a wet minor merger.

We discovered a peculiar transient ( ) in the direction of M 86. Furthermore, we found evidence for an ongoing wet minor merger between M 86 and the galaxy SDSS J122541.29+130251.2. This activity makes it conceivable that the transient is located at the distance of M 86.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes M86 by detecting its core, plume, tail, and extended halo, measuring temperature structure, and estimating gas and dynamical masses that imply halo gas stripping.

quotes.quote: The M86 core, the plume, and the tail extending toward the northwest were clearly detected, as well as the extended halo around them. From the position angle 45D to 275D , the surface brightness distribution of the core and the extended halo was represented relatively well with a single model of 0.5 up to 15' - 20' . The X-ray spectra of the core was represented with a two-temperature model of kT 0.9  keV and 0.6  keV. The temperatures of the core and the halo have a positive gradient in the center, and reach the maximum of kT 1.0  keV at 7' , indicating that the halo gas is located in a larger scale potential structure than that of the galaxy. The temperatures of the plume and the tail were 0.86 0.01  keV and 1.00 0.01  keV.

From the surface brightness distribution, we estimated the gas mass ( 3 10^ 10 M_ ) and the dynamical mass ( 3 10^ 12 M_ ) in <100  kpc. The gas mass to the dynamical mass ratio was 10^ 3 - 10^ 2 , suggesting a significant fraction of the halo gas has been stripped. M86 (NGC 4406) is a bright elliptical galaxy in the Virgo cluster, located about 1.26D , or about 350 kpc in projection, from the Virgo cluster center M87.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M 86 as a Virgo subclump interacting with M 87, with comparable luminosity, similar GC velocity dispersion, and a GC recession offset interpreted as possible tidal stripping.

quotes.quote: A smaller galaxy cluster centered on M 86 is passing around the main core of the Virgo Cluster surrounding M 87. We can roughly estimate how much the velocity dispersion of the GCs around M 87 increased using the impulse approximation for high-speed encounters; i.e., we assume that the perturber moves with a constant velocity on a linear trajectory and the GCs do not move during the encounter. If the perturbing object has a mass of M_ p , a velocity of v_ p , and the pericentric distance of the encounter is b , then the tidal force increases the velocity of a GC at distance of x from the center of M 87 by approximately mo v = 2GM_ p x v_ p b^2 . If we substitute in this equation for v_ p the average velocity dispersion in the Virgo Cluster, 700 km s ^ 1 binggeli93 , the dynamical mass of the M 86 subclump, M_ p = 3 10^ 13 M _ bohringer94 , the current projected distance of M 87 from M 86, d=350 kpc, and x = 50 kpc for the characteristic distance of a GC, we obtain v = 150 km s ^ 1 .

We turn again to M 87 and M 86. These galaxies have approximately the same luminosity. If the smaller M 86 subclump increased the velocity dispersion of the GCs of M 87, then the GCs of M 86 had to be affected even more. According to park12 , the velocity dispersion of the GCs of M 86 is virtually the same as that of M 87, around 300 km s ^ 1 .
The average recession velocity of the GC system of M 86 is different from that of the M 86 core by over 100 km s ^ 1 , which can be a sign of tidal stripping.

sources.summary: The paper studies M86 as a Virgo giant elliptical with an offset globular-cluster density peak, distorted contours, and a bridge to M84 suggesting interaction history.

quotes.quote: Rhode Department of Astronomy, Indiana University Bloomington, IN 47405-7105, USA Enrico Vesperini Department of Astronomy, Indiana University Bloomington, IN 47405-7105, USA We have carried out a search for substructure within the globular cluster systems of M84 (NGC 4374) and M86 (NGC 4406), two giant elliptical galaxies in the Virgo Cluster. We use wide-field (36 x 36 ), multi-color broadband imaging to identify globular cluster candidates in these two galaxies as well as several other nearby lower-mass galaxies. Our analysis of the spatial locations of the globular cluster candidates reveals several substructures, including: a peak in the projected number density of globular clusters in M86 that is offset from the system center and may be at least partly due to the presence of the dwarf elliptical galaxy NGC 4406B; a bridge that connects the M84 and M86 globular cluster systems; and a boxy iso-density contour along the southeast side of the M86 globular cluster system. We divide our sample into red (metal-rich) and blue (metal-poor) globular cluster candidates to look for differences in the spatial distributions of the two populations and find that the blue cluster candidates are the dominant population in each of the substructures we identify. We also incorporate the measurements from two radial velocity surveys of the globular clusters in the region and find that the bridge substructure is populated by globular clusters with a mix of velocities that are consistent with either M86 and M84, possibly providing further evidence for interaction signatures between the two galaxies.

A Spatial Offset in the Peak Surface Density of the M86 Globular Cluster System Feature A in Figure fig:all_map is a peak in the GC system surface density that is approximately 0.7 away from the center of M86. This surface density peak is similar to an over-density reported by dabrusco15 that lies in roughly the same area. In addition to the unexpected position of the surface density peak, there is a distortion in the central region of the M86 GC system where the surface density contours are elongated along the northeast to southwest direction. Since the masked portion of the galaxy (the central 200 x 200 pixels, which are masked because they are saturated in the CCD images) is close to this surface density peak, we first attempted to determine if this offset might be due to the same kinds of edge effects that created the offset in the M84 surface density peak. Decreasing the kernel size did not cause the peak to migrate towards the center of M86, so we created a 1 wide annulus just outside and surrounding the masked region and calculated the surface density within this annulus.
The lopsided morphology of the innermost region of the M86 GC system seen in the original KDE map is still present in this new map.

sources.summary: The paper relates M86's X-ray halo to supersonic stripping, citing its large Virgo-relative velocity, X-ray luminosity, temperature, sharp surface-brightness edge, and irregular plume.

quotes.quote: However, a supersonic stripping process has often been related to the X-ray halo surrounding the Virgo elliptical NGC4406/M86 (e.g. Nulsen 1982; Rangarajan et al. 1995). This galaxy has a blue shift of 230 km/s, which added to the recession velocity of the Virgo cluster of 1150 km/s results in an estimated velocity of the galaxy relative to the ICM of 1380 km/s out along the line of sight. The galaxy's total X-ray luminosity in the 0.1--2.4 keV band is L_X 4 10^ 41 erg/s within a radius of 80 kpc, and the emission temperature is T_X 0.8 keV (Brown Bregman 1998). The observed X-ray map is shown in Fig. xBproj together with some synthetic maps from the simulations.

The X-ray image of the M86 region has probably two major distinctive features which are thought to indicate ongoing stripping. One is the sharp decline in X-ray surface brightness on the north side, which makes the ROSAT/PSPC contour levels almost semi-circular. The other is the emission plume to the north-west of the emission maximum, best seen in the ROSAT/HRI data. The plume itself is very irregular and shows emission maxima and `holes', as well as significant variation in the emission temperature (Rangarajan et al. 1995).

sources.summary: The paper detects a weak, extended 1.4 GHz radio halo around the elliptical galaxy M86 and interprets it as evidence of a past interaction with Virgo intracluster gas.

quotes.quote: We detect a low surface brightness radio halo with a flux density of 5 1.5 Jy centered close to the elliptical galaxy M86. This halo is much weaker than that observed in the Coma cluster. It is reminiscent of a past interaction between the intracluster medium of M86 and a low density gas, belonging most probably to the Virgo cluster.

Based on Figs. fig:subobservedprofile , fig:subanalyticalprofile , fig:finalmap , and fig:ring the radio halo around M86 is a robust feature of low surface brightness ( 50 mK or 38 mJy/( 12 beam) at a distance of about 1 from M86). Its radial extent is 2 to the west. It is not possible to determine its extent to the east because of the emission of M87 and its sidelobe residuals. In Figs. fig:subobservedprofile and fig:subanalyticalprofile the M86 radio halo appears to be asymmetric, which is mainly due to the way how the radially symmetric emission was subtracted. Thus it is unclear whether the M86 radio halo is symmetric or not.

sources.summary: The paper studies M86's globular clusters, finding bimodal colors, different blue and red spatial profiles, a red-cluster distribution tied to the stellar halo, a negative color gradient, and a blue tilt.

quotes.quote: We present a photometric study of the globular clusters (GCs) in the Virgo giant elliptical galaxy M86 based on Washington CT_1 images. The colors of the GCs in M86 show a bimodal distribution with a blue peak at (C-T_1)=1.30 and a red peak at (C-T_1)=1.72 . The spatial distribution of the red GCs is elongated similarly to that of the stellar halo, while that of the blue GCs is roughly circular. The radial number density profile of the blue GCs is more extended than that of the red GCs.

The radial number density profile of the red GCs is consistent with the surface brightness profile of the M86 stellar halo. The GC system has a negative radial color gradient, which is mainly due to the number ratio of the blue GCs to the red GCs increasing as galactocentric radius increase. The bright blue GCs in the outer region of M86 show a blue tilt: the brighter they are, the redder their mean colors get.

sources.summary: The paper describes M86 as a Virgo elliptical moving supersonically through the ICM, with a long stripped X-ray tail and evidence for interaction with NGC 4438.

quotes.quote: Observations of the Virgo Cluster have shown that it is composed of three separate sub-groups: one centered on M87, the Brightest Cluster Galaxy (BCG); one centered on M86, an elliptical galaxy roughly one degree ( 300 ) to the west of M87; and the final sub-group centered on NGC 4472 (also known as M49) approximately 4.5^ to the south of M87 Binggeli1987,Binggeli1993,Bohringer1994 . Spectroscopic studies of M86 have shown that it has an absolute blue-shifted velocity of 250 ^ 1 while M87 has a red-shifted velocity of 1300 ^ 1 . Given an average temperature of the ambient Virgo ICM of 2.3 corresponding to a sound speed of 700 ^ 1 Urban2011 , M86 must be traversing through the Virgo Cluster supersonically with a Mach number of at least M 2 .

To the north-west of M86 a long tail of diffuse gas is observed, which has been discussed in detail by and . The length of this tail is 150 in projection, although given the large line-of-sight velocity of M86 with respect to the ambient Virgo ICM this is likely a conservative lower limit to its true length. The width of this tail in projection, however, is roughly uniform along its entire length. To the east of M86, diffuse emission is observed out to the neighboring galaxy NGC 4438.

sources.summary: The survey describes M86 as a massive Virgo elliptical whose halo contains tidal streams and a newly identified broad southeast shelf, likely from an older, massive accretion event.

quotes.quote: Another area of high projected galaxy density in Virgo is the region containing the massive ellipticals M86 and M84, although the two ellipticals themselves may be well separated along the line of sight ( Mei 2007). The presence of an extensive, diffuse ICL component in this region is indicated by the large number of intracluster PNe found in the field (Arnaboldi 1996, Okamura 2002), as well as by the discovery of an intracluster Type Ia supernova (Smith 1981). As shown in J+10, the halos of both M86 and M84 contain a number of small streams suggestive of tidal stripping of dwarf galaxies orbiting the massive ellipticals.

Also extending to the SE along the major axis of M86 is a broad shelf of light which does not trace the elliptical isophotes of the giant elliptical. This feature, not originally identified in the analysis of J+10, has a surface brightness of =27.3 and \ color of 0.85. Given its rather large size ( 40 \ arcmin ^2 ) the total luminosity in this feature is significant: L=3.5 10^8 . Unlike the majority of the streams J+10 identified in M86's halo, which are typically smaller and lower in luminosity than this ``SE shelf,'' this feature is more similar to the broad accretion structures seen in M49 and M87, and may be tracing an older and more massive accretion event in M86's past.

sources.summary: The paper treats M86 as a massive Virgo galaxy whose concentrated low-velocity globular clusters form an excluded infalling group, while other GCs trace a bridge toward M87.

quotes.quote: The massive nearby galaxies M86 and M84 have 3000 GCs each pen08 . The analysis above, therefore, leads us to expect that only 0.3 GCs from these galaxies could contaminate our sample.

In addition, we identify another group of GCs at 30 < R_ maj < 150 , that appears to be a broad feature in the velocity-position phase-space diagram connecting M87 with M86. The mean radial velocity of these GCs is = -66 , which is about 1370 lower than the systemic velocity of M87.

The GCs with low radial velocities are mostly located in the region west of M87 (between it and M86). Note that the low velocity GCs that are strongly concentrated on M86 (systemic velocity of 224 ) are considered to belong to it and are not plotted in this figure. For this analysis, we only used the 692 GCs that have higher radial velocities in order to focus on the main structure of the Virgo Cluster, assuming that the low velocity GCs constitute an infalling group associated with M86.

sources.summary: M86 is described as a Virgo infalling galaxy with a ram-pressure stripped plume, a cool corona in the hot ICM, and a cold front hidden by projection.

quotes.quote: Another example of a galaxy infalling into a cluster potential is M86. X-ray emission from M86 was first observed with the Einstein Observatory and subsequently by ROSAT. Its unusual ``plume'' was explained as a ram pressure stripped galactic corona produced as M86 crosses the core of the Virgo cluster at supersonic velocity . The Chandra image and temperature map confirm a cool ( 0.6 keV) corona embedded in the hot ( 3 keV) Virgo ICM .

Based on the difference between the velocity of M86 and the Virgo cluster core, about 1500 km sec ^ 1 , M86 must be crossing the Virgo core at supersonic velocity. However, we see no clear cold front as in NGC1404 (for the image and temperature map of M86 see Fig. 6 and Fig. 7 in Forman et al. ). The most likely explanation is that M86 is moving nearly in the line of sight, directly towards us, and the resulting cold front (and possible shocks) are difficult to see in projection.

sources.summary: The paper treats M 86 as an alternative interaction partner for NGC 4438, noting connecting H filaments, a possible 100 Myr encounter, and its intracluster medium.

quotes.quote: Recent deep H observations of the M 86 region (Kenney et al. 2008) revealed a highly complex and disturbed ISM/ICM. NGC 4438 is connected to M 86 by several faint H filaments. This suggests a tidal interaction between the two galaxies. Although the tidal interaction might have occured with M 86 100 Myr ago, strong ongoing ram pressure is still required to explain the CO(1--0) distribution and kinematics.

The higher mass of M 86 compared to that of NGC 4435 allows a larger impact parameter for an NGC 4438 -- M 86 encounter. Since M 86 has its own intracluster medium, the need for strong ongoing ram pressure affecting the ISM of the inner galactic disk, implies that ram pressure stripping due to M 86 certainly did not remove and might not have significantly affected the ISM of NGC 4438 in this region.

sources.summary: The paper presents Herschel SPIRE imaging of M86, finding complex cold-dust structures tied to atomic and ionized gas, likely stripped from NGC 4438 and heated by interaction-related processes.

quotes.quote: 17, A-1180 Wien, Austria Submitted to A A Herschel Special Issue We present Herschel SPIRE observations at 250-500 m of the giant elliptical galaxy M86 and examine the distribution of the resolved cold dust emission and its relation with other galactic tracers. The SPIRE images reveal three dust components: emission from the central region; a dust lane extending north-south; and a bright emission feature 10 kpc to the south-east. We estimate that 10^6 M_ of dust is spatially coincident with atomic and ionized hydrogen, originating from stripped material from the nearby spiral NGC 4438 due to recent tidal interactions with M86.

In summary, we present submm images of M86 which reveal a complex dust morphology with emission detected 10 kpc from the centre, towards the southeast. The unprecedented resolution of SPIRE has revealed, for the first time, a strong spatial correlation between the cold dust ( 10^6 M_ , g/d of 20-80 ) and the warm ionized gas in M86. This result strongly favours a scenario whereby the dust in M86 originates from material stripped from the nearby spiral NGC 4438.

sources.summary: The paper treats M 86/NGC 4406 as a giant elliptical near the blueshifted-galaxy centroid whose satellite cloud may be merging into Virgo around M 87.

quotes.quote: Binggeli et al. [ 7 ] and Jerjen et al. [ 16 ] proposed an explanation for the observed shift of the centroid of the galaxies with negative velocities relative to the dynamic center of Virgo in terms of a separate grouping of galaxies associated with the giant elliptical galaxy NGC 4406 (M 86). In the sky this galaxy lies near the centroid of the blueshifted galaies (see the right hand frame of Fig. 3), and its radial velocity, 374 km s ^ 1 , is close to the average velocity of our sample of galaxies (see Fig. 2). According to these authors, M 86, together with the cloud of satellites surrounding it, is incident onto (merging with) the center of Virgo (M 87) from the further edge of the cluster at a relative velocity of 1400 km s ^ 1 , which is a bit more than twice the mean square virial velocity of the members of Virgo.

Moving at this velocity, M 86 and its companions will intersect the virial radius of the cluster (1.8 Mpc) after 1.3 billion years and will continue moving towards our Galaxy. The distances to M 86 and six other galaxies with negative radial velocities, VCC 200, VCC 810, VCC 815, VCC 846, VCC 928, and NGC 4419 have been measured [ 15--17 ] from the surface brightness fluctuations (SBF). The distance of M 86, itself, 17.5 0.4 Mpc, and the average distance of the other six galaxies, 17.3 1.1 Mpc are the same, to within the limits of error, as the average for the cluster as a whole (17.0 Mpc).

sources.summary: The paper describes M86 as an IR-bright giant elliptical whose dust and gas morphology points to tidal stripping and interaction with NGC4438.

quotes.quote: M86 is a well-known IR-bright giant elliptical. Two dust features were detected with IRAS and originally attributed to dust stripped from M86 due to its motion through the cluster. The discovery of atomic gas offset from the center of M86 and decoupled from its stellar disk supports a tidal interaction (Li van Gorkom 2001), confirmed when Kenney et al. \ (2008) detected strong H features extending from M86 to the nearby spiral NGC4438. The distribution and velocity of the ionized gas provide clear evidence for tidal interaction between these two giants.

SPIRE observations of M86 and NGC4438 showed that the dust emission is spatially correlated with the ionized gas between the two galaxies (Gomez et al. \ 2010; Cortese et al. \ 2010a) implying that the dust is material stripped from the nearby spiral. In Figure fig:spireegals , the appearance of the dust emission in M86 is clearly different to the other ellipticals in our sample, with faint filamentary features seen within D(25) .

sources.summary: The paper places a transient ULX in M86, giving the giant elliptical’s Virgo-cluster membership, center, distance, effective-radius context, and low-surface-brightness environment.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The centre of the galaxy is located at RA 12 ^ h 26 ^ m 11 814, Dec. +12 56 45 49

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of the transient ultraluminous X-ray source (ULX) CXOU J122602.3+125951 (hereafter M86 tULX-1), located 3 52 ( 19 kpc) northwest of the centre of the giant elliptical galaxy M86 (NGC 4406) in the Virgo Cluster. Based on our analysis of deep optical imaging of M86, it is probably not located in a globular cluster. M86 is a member of the Virgo Cluster.

The centre of the galaxy is located at RA 12 ^ h 26 ^ m 11 814, Dec. +12 56 45 49 skrutskie2006 . We adopted a distance to M86 of 16.83 0.54 Mpc mei2007 to convert flux rates to luminosities. Since the effective radius of M86 is 3 38 0 24 kormendy2009 , the galactocentric radius of M86 tULX-1 corresponds to 1.14 effective radii.

sources.summary: The paper describes M86 as a Virgo massive elliptical with a KDC, known shells, outer-shell detections, and a stellar halo containing filaments, a bridge, stream, and plumes.

quotes.quote: M86 is a massive elliptical galaxy in the Virgo cluster. It is long known to have a KDC be88,bs92,bsg94 and has also been studied by integral field spectroscopy a3d2 . M86 is a known shell galaxy for94 .

A deep image reveals around five shells in the outskirts, see Fig. fig:virgo . The stellar halo hosts several other substructures like several low-surface-brightness filaments, a tidal bridge, stream, and plumes mih05,mih17,jnw10 . Some substructures cannot be safely assigned to M86 since its halo is partially overlapped, in projection, with the halo of M84, although the galaxies themselves may be well separated along the line of sight mei07 .

sources.summary: The paper describes M86 as a Virgo galaxy with luminous extended X-ray emission and a coherent stripped tail, whose CGM may strip slowly over about 2 Gyr.

quotes.quote: Another interesting observation in literature is that of the unusually luminous and extended emission from the galaxy M86 (NGC4406) in the Virgo cluster, first reported by Forman1979ApJ using Einstein X-ray Observatory. Forman1979ApJ suggest that the X-ray emitting tail associated with M86 originates from within the galaxy. M86 orbit is at a projected distance of 0.4 0.8 \ Mpc from M87 (the cluster center; Mei2007 ) and is moving with a line-of-sight velocity of 1550 \ km \ s^ 1 .

The ambient ICM density around M86 at a projected distance of 0.4 \ kpc is 2 10^ 4 \ cm^ 3 which would imply a ram pressure of 5.1 10^ 12 \ dyn \ cm^ 2 . Ehlert2013 observe that the stripped X-ray emitting tail forms a uniform and coherent structure as large as 150 \ kpc . Our 'cloud crushing' regime suggests that the M86's CGM (of atleast 200 \ kpc ) would be stripped slowly over a drag timescale of 200 \ kpc/1550 \ km \ s^ 1 2 \ Gyr , assuming it has been moving at its present velocity.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4406 as M86, a luminous early-type Virgo galaxy with an X-ray plume, a kinematically distinct core, and low-confidence globular-cluster bimodality.

quotes.quote: NGC 4406 This luminous early-type (E3/S0) Virgo galaxy is also known as M86. It is a rare example of an early type galaxy with an X-ray plume, presumably resulting from ram pressure stripping ran95 . A kinematically distinct core was found by ben88 .
It was part of the same HST study by forb96 which included NGC 4365.

Like NGC 4365, no obvious bimodality was detected in the short exposure images. kun99 found tentative evidence for bimodality with peaks at = 0.98 and 1.17; here we formally find peaks at = 0.986 and 1.145 but still with bimodality detected at a low confidence level.

sources.summary: The paper models M86 as a massive Virgo perturber whose infall near M87 changes galaxy orbits, increases ram-pressure encounters, and may explain high-velocity H i deficient spirals.

quotes.quote: The giant elliptical galaxy M86 has a total mass of about one tenth of the whole cluster mass (Schindler et al. 1999). The perturbation of the total gravitational field due to M86 is thus not negligible. We have added the gravitational potential corresponding to M86 ( =0.5, r_ C =74.2 kpc, and _ C =6.26 10^ 3 M _ pc ^ 3 ).

We suggest that the existence of the special galaxy population with high radial velocities at large projected distances is linked to the trajectory of M86 perturbing the gravitational potential. In this scenario these galaxies pass the cluster center with high velocities. The increased velocity leads to an enhanced ram pressure ( p_ ram v_ gal ^ 2 ).

sources.summary: The paper presents M86 as the brightest Virgo early-type galaxy detected in H I, with cold gas likely accreted recently through interaction with a companion.

quotes.quote: The brightest ETG detected in in the Virgo cluster is M86 (VCC 881, NGC 4406), an S0/E3 galaxy with a long plume of X-ray emission to the North-West, which is thought to be due to hot gas swept back by the ram pressure caused by the motion of M86 through the intracluster medium (Forman et al. 1979). M86 is also the ETG with the largest H luminosity found in the survey of ionised gas in a sample of ETG with hot gas by Trinchieri di Serego Alighieri (1991). The rings and filaments visible in their H image, reproduced at the center of Fig. 2, suggest that the gas might result from a recent interaction with a gas rich object.

Therefore cold gas cannot survive for very long in M86 and, if present, must have been accreted less than a gigayear ago. A candidate gas-donor could be NGC 4406B (VCC 882), a dE galaxy located at a projected distance of 1.4 arcmin to the North-East of the centre of M86.

sources.summary: The paper presents M86 as a Virgo elliptical whose interaction with NGC 4438 produced filaments, stripped gas, and a possible gravitational-heating signature without clear AGN activity.

quotes.quote: Suggestive evidence for gravitational heating comes from new observations of the Virgo galaxy M86 (figure fig:m86 ) kenney_spectacular_2008 , where we have discovered a remarkable set of large scale H + [ NII ] filaments connecting the giant elliptical to NGC 4438, a spiral galaxy 23' ( 120 kpc) away. Spectroscopy of the filaments show a fairly smooth velocity gradient between the galaxies, strongly suggesting a recent gravitational interaction between them. The data suggest that as NGC 4438 passed by M86 at high velocity, its cold gas was probably stripped by the ISM of the elliptical, causing the spiral to become HI-deficient. Despite being x-ray bright, M86 shows no clear signs for an active nucleus. Moreover, the total kinetic energy of the cold gaseous component of the spiral at closest approach was comparable to the current total thermal energy stored in the hot halo of the elliptical.

sources.summary: The paper finds M86 has several compact radial streams and plumes, consistent with accretion or stripping of dwarf galaxies, but not the extended stream system seen around M87.

quotes.quote: Around M86, we find several small radial streams, including ones to the north (Region 1), south (Region 4), southeast (Region 5), and east (Region 6). These small features are very unlikely to be fitting artifacts, as they are coherent and consistent across many isophotes of the galaxies. We also find a few small plumes between M86 and M84 (Regions 2 and 3). We have verified that all these features can be seen in the unsubtracted image, and are therefore not artifacts of the subtraction process. It is tempting to link up features 1 and 4, and features 2 and 6, into continuous streams that cross the face of M86, as would be expected from tidal stripping of small orbiting satellites.

sources.summary: The paper describes M86 as a giant elliptical whose planetary-nebula velocities and distance estimates inform the near line-of-sight encounter with M87.

quotes.quote: Conclusions from ICPNs and dwarfs LOSVDs are that M87 and M86 are falling towards each other nearly along the LOS, and in a phase just before their first close pass.

The observed LOSVDs of the PNs, GCs (C ot e et al. 2001), and (dE+dS0) in the Virgo core can be interpreted as evidence that the two massive sub-clusters in the Virgo core associated with the giant ellipticals M87 and M86 are currently falling towards each other - more or less along the LOS, with M87 falling backwards from the front and M86 forwards from the back - and will eventually merge, i.e. the entire core of the Virgo cluster must then be out of virial equilibrium and dynamically evolving.

PNLF distances (Jacoby et al. 1990) and ground-based surface brightness fluctuation distances indicate that M86 is behind M87 by just under 0.15 mag. However, the most recent surface brightness fluctuation measurements find that M87 and M86 are only at very slightly different distances. Within the errors, the distance moduli (M87: 31.18 0.07 , M86: 31.13 0.07 ) are consistent with being either at the same distance or separated by 1-2 Mpc.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M86 as a likely infalling Virgo galaxy/subclump with strongly negative velocity, then argues its velocity difference makes it unlikely to be the core of the same infalling group.

quotes.quote: With M86 most likely falling in from behind e.g. Zhang2015 and M87 deviating from the mean of the velocity distribution of galaxies, Binggeli1987 concluded that even the Virgo cluster core is a dynamically young structure.

This interpretation was based on the above positive relative velocity of M87 and nearby dwarfs, and the strongly negative velocities of M86 and some galaxies around it Markarian1961,Binggeli1987 . In the scenario outlined by Binggeli1993 , the M86 subclump would be several times less massive than the M87 subclump, which, at first glance, seems to fit our above picture of a recent infall of a massive group. However, the velocity difference of 2000 km s ^ 1 between M86 and the phase-space aggregation is very large and they move in opposite directions. In our simulations (Sect. sec:sim ), the former group's core does oscillate around the cluster center, but this oscillation is damped rather quickly after the initial passage, making it unlikely that M86 was the core of the very same infalling group of which the phase-space aggregation possibly represents the former outskirts.

sources.summary: The paper discusses M86 as a Virgo galaxy with an X-ray plume, likely ram-pressure stripped hot gas, complex temperature structure, and fairly uniform Fe, Mg, and Si abundances.

quotes.quote: An X-ray study of M86 with (Forman 1979) revealed a peak of emission centered on M86 and a ``plume'' extending north-west of the galaxy. Ram-pressure stripping of the hot gas from M86 as the galaxy traverses the Virgo cluster was suggested to explain the observed structure (Forman 1979; Fabian, Schwartz Forman 1980; Takeda, Nulsen Fabian 1984). The spectrum of M86 was adequately represented by radiation from an optically thin hot plasma with kT=0.8 keV, although no estimate of the Fe abundance could be made (Forman, Jones Tucker 1985). Spatial analysis of the X-ray brightness of M86 from the ROSAT PSPC and HRI data was presented in Rangarajan (1995; hereafter RWEF). In addition to the plume, they identified a southern extension (from the center to about 3 \ to the south), a Void (to the north of the galaxy) and a north-eastern arm (extending from the center more than 5 \ to the north-east).

sources.summary: XMM-Newton observations show M86 undergoing X-ray halo disruption, with a possible Mach 1.4 shock, low-entropy plume and arms, and interactions likely affecting its gas halo.

quotes.quote: In this context we present new XMM-Newton observations of M86 group. The unique combination of high spatial and spectral resolution and large field of view of XMM-Newton allows an in-depth investigation of the processes involved in the spectacular disruption of this object. We identify a possible shock with Mach number of 1.4 in the process of crushing the galaxy in the North-East direction. The latter is ascribed to the presence of a dense X-ray emitting filament, previously revealed in the RASS data. The shock is not associated with other previously identified features of M86 X-ray emission, such as the plume, the north-eastern arm and the southern extension, which are found to have low entropy, similar to the inner 2 kpc of M86.

sources.summary: The paper finds M86's halo hard to isolate, with regular brighter isophotes giving way to blended irregular diffuse light, filaments, and a southern common envelope.

quotes.quote: Because of the close proximity of M86, M84, and a number of smaller galaxies, uniquely tracing out any individual halo in that complex of galaxies proves difficult. The isophotes of both M86 and M84 appear fairly regular out to a surface brightness of 26.5 (J), as noted by Malin (1994). At fainter surface brightnesses, the isophotes blend together and become quite irregular, and we see a ``crown'' of diffuse light (at = 27--28) to the north of the M84/M86 system (K), and a diffuse extension to the south of the pair, blending into the network of galaxies to the south.

Also visible around M86 are several low surface brightness filaments (L1--L3), one to the northeast ( 25.5), one to the west ( 26.5), and one to the north ( 27.5). We also see a common envelope of light (M) surrounding the complex of galaxies to the south of M86, including NGC 4413, IC 3363, and IC 3349, along with a tidal bridge connecting NGC 4413 and IC 3363, and perhaps extending to NGC 4388 to the east.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4406 (VCC 881) as a Virgo giant elliptical, modeling its globular-cluster system and stellar light with core/Sersic-profile parameters.

quotes.quote: NGC 4406 (VCC 881) NGC 4406 is another giant elliptical in Virgo; its GCS has been studied by mean of the Mosaic Imager on the 4m Mayall telescope at the KPNO (Rhode Zepf 2004) in the B , V and R bands. The resulting best fit core model is characterized by _0=26.76 arcmin ^ 2 , r_c=3.52 arcmin and =1.19 . The galaxy light is well fitted by a Sersic core model (Eq. Sersic ), whose parameters have been determined by Ferrarese et al. (2006).

sources.summary: The paper presents M86 as a giant elliptical whose collision with NGC4438 could explain the plume, H filaments, velocity structure, and interaction geometry.

quotes.quote: However, recent H observations favour a different scenario in which NGC4438 has collided with the giant elliptical galaxy M86 kenney08 .

A more plausible scenario is that the plume has been created during the collision between NGC4438 and M86 ( V 244 ) as recently unveiled by . A complex structure of H filaments connects the two galaxies and spans a velocity range ( 240 <V< +70 ) consistent with the value observed in the plume.
The presence of a clear velocity gradient in the H filaments between M86 and NGC4438 kenney08 suggests in fact that the tidal acceleration is not parallel to the plane of sky.

sources.summary: NGC 4406 is described as M 86, a Virgo-infalling group dominant galaxy with a central radio point source and no clear X-ray/radio plasma interaction features.

quotes.quote: NGC 4406 Also known as M 86, NGC 4406 is the dominant member of a small group falling at high velocity towards the centre of the Virgo cluster (NGC 4486/M 87). Our 4.9 VLA radio data reveal a central point source. XMM-Newton and Chandra observations of the galaxy are discussed by Finoguenov04 and Randall08 , respectively. Our Chandra image shows only the core of the X-ray emitting gas.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4406 as a Virgo early-type detection near M87, gives projected and line-of-sight distances, and notes it is the only central Virgo detection.

quotes.quote: Galaxies detected in Virgo are NGC 4262, NGC 4406, NGC 4694 and NGC 4710. Their projected distance from M87 is 1.0, 0.5, 1.3 and 1.6 Mpc, respectively. Surface-brightness fluctuation distances are available for NGC 4262 and NGC 4406, and place them at a distance of 1.8 and 0.4 Mpc from M87 along the line of sight, respectively (Paper I). NGC 4406 is the only galaxy detected in \ close to the cluster centre, so that the \ detection rate in this region is just 1/26=4 4 percent.

sources.summary: The paper describes VCC 881 as a lenticular galaxy with ram-pressure-related dust and tail features, plus a globular-cluster mean age near 9.7 Gyr.

quotes.quote: VCC 881 (NGC 4406). This lenticular galaxy has a dust trail in the central region that is considered to be due to ram-pressure-stripping by its companion VCC 882 2000AJ....120..733E . The H feature produced by a collision with NGC 4438 2008ApJ...687L..69K and a ram-pressure-stripped tail against the Virgo intracluster medium 2008ApJ...688..208R are also reported. 2012ApJ...757..184P estimated the spectroscopic age of 8 GCs with the Faint Object Camera and Spectrograph on the Subaru telescope. The estimated mean age of eight GCs is reported to be 9.7 Gyr.

sources.summary: M86 is treated as the possible host of FXRT 1: the association is weak, the galaxy has low star formation, and the inferred X-ray peak luminosity depends on that association.

quotes.quote: FXRT 1/XRT 000519 identified previously by Jonker2013 is located in the outskirts of the galaxy M86 ( m_ R = 8.6 AB mag; 17 Mpc) in the Virgo cluster, at an angular (projected) distance of 12 2 ( 60 kpc). This association is still under debate; the Poisson probability of a chance alignment is 3.6 10 ^ 4 based on its angular offset and the space density of m_ R < 9 mag galaxies using the GLADE catalog; Dalya2018 , implying a possible association; however, the binomial probability that this FXRT is a background source is 0.3, indicating that the association with M86 is weak (see Sect. sec:population for more details).
FXRT 1/XRT 000519 is probably related with M86, which has a low SFR ( 0.01 M _ yr ^ 1 ), as expected for elliptical galaxies.
For FXRT 1, assuming the association with M86 16.4 Mpc; Jonker2013 , it is characterized by a peak luminosity of 6 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 (see Table tab:F_L_para ).

sources.summary: M 86 is described as a massive elliptical falling toward Virgo, detected in radio continuum, and likely dominated by star formation rather than nuclear activity.

quotes.quote: VCC 881 (NGC 4406, M 86) While close in projection, the massive elliptical galaxy and it's surrounding group, which is extended in the X-rays Bohringer1994TheImages , likely lie around 2 Mpc behind M 87 Cantiello2018TheBeyond . M 86 is rapidly falling towards the cluster center from behind, as indicated by the blue-shifted spectrum. The galaxy was first discovered in the radio continuum at 4.9 GHz as faint point source in Dunn2010TheGalaxies , and recently also detected at 1.5 GHz by Grossova2022VeryGalaxies . In the latter work, it was concluded that the radio emission could be dominated by star-formation instead of nuclear activity; furthermore, no signs of X-ray cavities were observed.

sources.summary: The paper places M 86 in Virgo subcluster A’s secondary distance peak, giving its distance and velocity and noting that nearby galaxies do not form an obvious concentration around it.

quotes.quote: The most luminous galaxy in the secondary distance peak is M 86 (VCC 881) at 19.1 1.1 Mpc and with a velocity v_h = -224 . While there are a few other galaxies (VCC 828, 997, and 1414) with distances of 19 to 20 Mpc, v_h 500 , and projected locations within 0.5 Mpc of M 86, there is no obvious spatial concentration of the galaxies in the secondary peak around this bright galaxy. Figure clusterAplots compares the distances, magnitudes, sky locations, velocities, and colors of galaxies in the primary and secondary distance peaks of subcluster A, where we use 18 Mpc as the dividing line. M 86 is marked with a square box in the figure.

sources.summary: NGC 4406 is used as a Virgo comparison galaxy; its bluer integrated colours and weaker globular-cluster bimodality contrast with NGC 4365 and the other ellipticals.

quotes.quote: They are all members of the Virgo cluster and have comparable absolute luminosities although NGC 4365 is the fainter of the four if we adopt the distance moduli of Tonry et al. \ ( ).

NGC 4365, NGC 4486 and NGC 4649 have similar colours, while NGC 4406 is bluer in both \ and . According to the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED) all galaxies except NGC 4406 are classified as ellipticals, while NGC 4406 is listed as S0/E3.
In NGC 4406 the evidence for bimodality is weaker although there is still a hint of a blue peak at about the same colour as in the other galaxies.

sources.summary: NGC 4406 is the paper's strongest candidate for intermediate-age globular clusters, based on a small set of red clusters, though age dating remains model-dependent.

quotes.quote: The only indicative for intermediate ages is the presence of half a dozen GCs with (g-k) 3.7 in NGC 4406.

We conclude that age dating GC systems as old ( 11, 14 Gyrs) or intermediate age ( 2, 3 Gyrs) depends on the choice of the SSP model. Padova SSPs with isochrones for old ages are in good agreement with the data. Moreover there is no evidence for significant differences in the GC age distributions among the galaxies studied here. NGC 4406 could be an exception.
NGC 4406 is found to be the strongest candidate for having intermediate ages.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4406, M 86, as a Virgo giant elliptical with faint central point-like radio emission and ram-pressure-related X-ray features.

quotes.quote: NGC 4406* NGC 4406, know as M 86, is located in the Virgo cluster and flying in the direction towards us. Our new VLA A configuration data at 1.5 GHz (Figure fig:chandra_vla_ps_b d) reveal a faint, point-like radio emission in the center of this giant elliptical. Similarly, found point-like radio emission in the nuclear region at higher frequencies at around 4.9 GHz in the C configuration data.

gmw_00000349.name: source A
gmw_00000349.overview: Source A is a jetted radio quasar with a flux density of 60 mJy in its milliarcsecond-scale compact structure [arXiv:2202.09650]. Very Long Baseline Array imaging of the source reveals a one-sided core-jet structure typical of blazars [arXiv:2202.09650].

sources.summary: The paper identifies source A as the brightest FIRST component, with VLBA data showing a one-sided core-jet structure typical of blazars and likely a jetted radio quasar.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: right ascension _ A = 14^ h 56^ min 08.119733^ s and declination _ A = +50^ 48^ 36.30050^

quotes.quote: According to the FIRST data (Fig.  nvss-first ), the brightest of the three sources is A. Its 5-GHz Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) image displayed in Fig.  vlbi (left) is adopted from . The one-sided core--jet structure is typical for blazars.

However, source A itself appears as a prominent jetted radio quasar with 60  mJy flux density in its mas-scale compact structure, according to the VLBI data (Fig.  vlbi , left). It is weak in X-rays and optical, with no redshift available.

gmw_00000350.name: source B
gmw_00000350.overview: Source B is an active galactic nucleus that is identified as the X-ray and optical source within its group [arXiv:2202.09650]. High-resolution radio observations resolve it as a weak, compact radio core with blazar-like properties [arXiv:2202.09650].

sources.summary: The paper describes source B as the X-ray and optical source in the group, with EVN data resolving it as a compact weak radio source and blazar-like object.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Its phase-referenced coordinates are _ B = 14^ h 56^ min 03.63929^ s and _ B = +50^ 48^ 25.9759^

quotes.quote: To make a 5-GHz image of source B (Fig.  vlbi , right), we analysed publicly available data obtained from the European VLBI Network (EVN) archive (project EW017A, 2015 Oct 27--28, PI: Z. Wu). The participating radio telescopes were Effelsberg (Germany), Jodrell Bank Mk2 (UK), Westerbork (The Netherlands), Medicina, Noto (Italy), Onsala (Sweden), Sheshan, Nanshan (China), Toru n (Poland), and Yebes (Spain). This weak radio source was observed in phase-reference mode, with the calibrator J1439+4958 separated by 2.7^ from the target.

The source is well modelled with a weak (flux density 2.2 0.3  mJy) compact (full width at half-maximum 0.6  mas) circular Gaussian brightness distribution component. Its phase-referenced coordinates are _ B = 14^ h 56^ min 03.63929^ s and _ B = +50^ 48^ 25.9759^ , with estimated errors within 1  mas each. Swift X-ray Telescope observations with 18^ resolution revealed that source B (1SWXRT J145603.6+504826) is the X-ray source in the group.

gmw_00000351.name: NGC 4639
gmw_00000351.overview: NGC 4639 is a barred spiral galaxy [arXiv:1504.06282] and a member of the Virgo Cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0106261] that hosts a low-Eddington Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0809.1750, arXiv:1504.06282]. The galaxy features an unresolved radio core with an inverted spectral index [arXiv:1902.10670], a bar lacking H-alpha emission [arXiv:1504.06282], and no detected X-ray variability [arXiv:0809.1750].

sources.summary: The paper detects NGC 4639 as an unresolved radio source and fits its spectrum with an inverted spectral index after excluding the 1.5 GHz point.

quotes.quote: We detected radio emission in six out of ten sources: NGC 3185, NGC 3941, NGC 4477, NGC 4639, NGC 4698 and NGC 4725. Images of the detected sources (contours and coloured maps) are shown in Figs fig:contourMaps3185 fig:contourMaps4725 . A source is considered detected if the peak intensity is 3 . However, a source exhibiting a peak intensity 3 S_ P < 6 is considered as marginal detection, following the same criterion as in HU01 . Following this criterion, the detection rates are 5/10 in the L-band (with NGC 4477 and NGC 4698 as marginal detections), 6/10 in the C-band (with NGC 3941 as marginal detection), 5/10 in the X band (with NGC 3185 as marginal detection) and 4/10 in Ku band. As can be seen from images shown in Figs fig:contourMaps3185 fig:contourMaps4725 , four out of six sources (NGC 4477, NGC 4639, NGC 4698 and NGC 4725) are are not resolved in our VLA observations, and for them we could only set an upper limit to their sizes which correspond to half of the beam size, in analogy with HU01 .

We have built radio spectra for the sources which have been detected in two or more frequencies: NGC 3185, NGC 3941, NGC 4477, NGC 4639, NGC 4698 and NGC 4725 (Fig. fig:radiospectra upper limits are represented as arrows), considering all the available data points. We performed a weighted linear least squares fit using the Sci-Py scipy routine curve fit , weighting for the uncertainties in the flux densities. In three cases, NGC 4477, NGC 4639 and NGC 4698, the targets were found to fit a typical power-law S_ ^ I ^ to the spectrum We define a steep spectrum as one having 0.5, and a flat one as 0.5, following PG13 , with S_ ^ . We also define an inverted spectrum as one having 0 . For NGC 4639 we considered only the 5.5, 9 and 14 GHz data points, as the 1.5 GHz data point seems to deviate with respect to the power-law behaviour, being probably associated with a different emitting component which dominates at lower frequencies. We found an inverted spectral index for NGC 4639 and NGC 4698 of = 0.40 0.01 and = 0.13 0.07, respectively, and a steep spectral index of = +0.52 0.09 for NGC 4477.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4639 as a low-Eddington Seyfert 1; both MOS and ASCA light curves show no significant variations, despite its small black hole mass.

quotes.quote: We chose four from these sources, namely NGC 4235, NGC 4639, NGC 5033 and NGC 5273, because there exist estimates of their central black hole mass (hence we can estimate their L _ Edd ) and their nuclear luminosity is small: their L _ X /L _ Edd ratio is smaller than 10^ 3 . Therefore, it is possible that accretion in these objects operates in a radiatively inefficient mode. First, we used archival \ and \ data to measure their X-ray variability amplitude and we compared it with the variability amplitude of more luminous Seyferts.

NGC 4235 and NGC 4639 do not show significant variations: ^ 2 _ 4235 = 15.07/21 degrees of freedom (dof), and ^ 2 _ 4639 = 26.1/27 dof, respectively. On the other hand we do observe significant variations in the NGC 5503 and NGC 5273 light curves ( ^ 2 _ 5033 =44.3/22 and ^ 2 _ 5273 =552/30 dof, respectively). We use the normalized excess variance, ^ 2 _ NXS (e.g. Nandra \ 1997) as a measure of the intrinsic variability amplitude of the light curves which show significant variations. This estimator is defined as, ^ 2 _ NXS = S^ 2 < _ err ^ 2 > <x>^2 , where <x> , and S^ 2 =(1/ N_ data ) _ i=1 ^ N_ data (x_ i <x>)^2 are the mean and variance of the light curve, while < ^2_ err >=(1/ N_ data ) _ i=1 ^ N_ data ^ 2 _ err,i is the average contribution of the Poisson noise process to the observed scatter around the mean (N _ data is the number of points in the light curve). Its square root (the so called ``fractional root mean square amplitude", f_ rms ) indicates the average variability amplitude of a source as a fraction of the light curve mean. We find that f_ rms,5033 =4 1 and f_ rms,5273 =18 1 (errors account only for the measurement errors in the light curve points and have been estimated according to the prescription of Vaughan , 2003). In order to investigate further the variability of NGC 4235 and NGC 4639, we considered \ data as well. The main advantage of the \ light curves is that they are are longer than the \ light curves. In the case of NGC 4235, we employed its 0.5-10 keV, 5760 s binned, SIS 0+1 combined light curves from the 58 ksec long, December 1998 observation (sequence number 76005000; the light curves were downloaded from the TARTARUS database). The background subtracted light curve is plotted in the upper panel of Fig. fig:lcs2 . We detect significant variations ( ^ 2 =39.8/10 dof) with f_ rms,0.5-10 keV =6.2 1.8 . NGC 4639 has also been observed by . However, its background subtracted, 0.5--10 keV, SIS 0+1 combined TARTARUS light curve (shown in the bottom panel of Fig. fig:lcs2 ) does not reveal any significant variations during its 40 ksec observation in December 1998 (sequence number 76007000).

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4639 as a Seyfert AGN host, masks its nuclear emission for H-alpha work, and notes its bar lacks H-alpha emission.

quotes.quote: Specifically, for the AGN hosts NGC 4151 and NGC 4639, the central emission saturated the detector and in consequence the continuum subtraction could not be removed properly, leaving a residual that had to be masked out.

To consider only star formation, we have to take into account that some of the galaxies of our sample have nuclear activity: NGC 918 (no specific classification; Palumbo1983 ); NGC 4151 (Seyfert type 1.5; Khachikian1974 ) and NGC 4639 (Seyfert 1.0; Ho1997 ), where all nuclear activity classifications have been collected in VeronCetty2006 . The H emission in these cases is not all coming from star formation, so the derived SFRs in these galaxies should be understood as upper limits. We have masked the nuclear emissions in these three cases and excluded them from the integration (very small in the cases of NGC 918 and the low-luminosity AGN of NGC 4639), so that the global H luminosities are not biased due to AGNs.
Bars are usually dominated by old population stars, and in some cases do not show H emission (e.g., in the SB galaxies NGC 4639, NGC 5334 or NGC 5921).

sources.summary: The paper flags NGC 4639 as anomalous in the Virgo Tolman-Bondi model because its positive velocity conflicts with expected backside infall toward Virgo.

quotes.quote: NGC 4639 has a positive velocity when -- according to the model -- it should be falling into Virgo from the backside. Can we explain this strange behaviour? We note it is at a small angular distance from Virgo ( =3.0 ). Perhaps NGC 4639 has fallen through the centre and still has some of its frontside infall velocity left. There are, however, no reports of significant hydrogen deficiency which one would expect if the galaxy actually had travelled through the centre.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4639 as the discrepant Virgo Cepheid case, placing it in class 3 with low HI deficiency and a larger inferred distance.

quotes.quote: Five of the six distances cluster about a mean distance of 14.6 Mpc, while the sixth one (NGC 4639) gives a larger distance (21 Mpc). This is perfectly explained by the Tolman-Bondi model, and this was in fact the initial motivation of this study.
On the contrary, the insignificant deficiency of NGC 4639 leads to put it into class 3, with a derived Virgo distance intermediate between NGC 4321 and NGC 4548.

Then, four of the five galaxies classified in class 2 now fall into class 1 (infalling galaxies), NGC 4639 is the only true member of the Virgo cluster among the six, and NGC 4548 position cannot be explained by the model.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4639 as the nearby perturbing spiral whose modeled retrograde passage can account for NGC 4654's stellar asymmetry, with an orbit chosen to match observed position and velocity.

quotes.quote: However, NGC 4654 does not only show an asymmetric gas disk, but also has an asymmetric stellar distribution. This might be due to a gravitational interaction with the nearby spiral galaxy NGC 4639. In order to investigate the possibilities of tidal or ISM--ISM interaction, the code presented in Vollmer et al. (2001) has been extended and contains now a non--collisional component.

In this simulation we assume that NGC 4654 has been gravitationally perturbed by its companion NGC 4639. Since we need a close encounter in order to generate the observed asymmetry of the stellar disk of NGC 4654 without disrupting it, I choose a retrograde encounter. The perturbing galaxy is modeled as a plummer sphere with a mass of 10^ 11  M _ and a length scale of 2 kpc.
